years. Share this map with your counselor, and discuss three factors that scientists believe affect the global
weather and temperature. Discuss with your counselor three impacts of climate change and how these changes
could impact sustainability of food, water, or other resources.| |Sustainability|9. Learn about career opportunities in the sustainability field. Pick one and find out the education, training, and
experience required. Discuss what you have learned with your counselor and explain why this career might
interest you.|5. Do the following:
a. After completing requirements 1 through 4, have a family meeting. Discuss what your family has learned about
what it means to be a sustainable citizen. Talk about the behavioral changes and life choices your family can make
to live more sustainably. Share what you learn with your counselor.
b. Discuss with your counselor how living by the Scout Oath and Scout Law in your daily life helps promote
sustainabilityandgood stewardship.| |**Swimming**|(new) 3. Correctly perform the following strokes:
a. Demonstrate the front crawl or the trudgen using good form.
b. Demonstrate the back crawl using good form.
c. Demonstrate the sidestroke using good form.
d. Demonstrate the breaststroke using good form.
e. Demonstrate the elementary backstroke using good form.|New requirement.| ## **Scouts BSA Merit Badge Updates Effective January 1, 2024** ****These modifications are anticipated, minor adjustments may occur prior to final release on January 1, 2024**** Updated 12/1/23 by the National Scouts BSA Subcommittee |**MERIT BADGE**|**CHANGED REQUIREMENT**
**(Including Requirement Number)**|**EXISTING REQUIREMENT**
**(Including Requirement Number)**| |---|---|---| |Swimming|4. Swim continuously for 150 yards in a strong manner using each of the following strokes in any order; front
crawl or trudgen (25 yards); back crawl (25 yards); sidestroke (25 yards); breaststroke (25 yards); and
elementary backstroke (50 yards).|3. Swim continuously for 150 yards using the following strokes in good form and in a strong manner: front crawl or
trudgen for 25 yards, back crawl for 25 yards, sidestroke for 25 yards, breaststroke for 25 yards, and elementary
backstroke for 50 yards.| |Swimming|NOTE: All remaining requirements are shifted upward in number; i.e. old requirement 4 becomes requirement
5, etc.|| _**If you have any update suggestions for merit badges please submit them to merit.badge@scouting.org**_

title: Mb Requirement Updates 2025
heading: Mb Requirement Updates 2025
text:
## **SCOUTS BSA MERIT BADGE COMMITTEE Merit Badge Requirement Updates (Effective January 1, 2025)** |**MERIT BADGE**|**UPDATED REQUIREMENT**|**ORIGINAL REQUIREMENT**| |---|---|---| |Animal Science|1. Name two breeds of livestock in each of the following classifcatons: horses, dairy catle, beef catle, sheep, hogs, poultry, and
goats. Tell their principal uses and merits. Tell where the breeds originated.|1. Name four breeds of livestock in each of the following classifcatons: horses, dairy catle, beef catle, sheep, hogs, poultry,
and goats. Tell their principal uses and merits. Tell where the breeds originated.| |Animal Science|2. List two diseases that afict the animals in each of the classifcatons in requirement 1. Describe the symptoms of each
disease and explain how each is contracted and how it could be prevented.|2. List fve diseases that afict the animals in each of the classifcatons in requirement 1. Also list fve diseases of poultry.
Describe the symptoms of each disease and explain how each is contracted and how it could be prevented.| |Animal Science|3. Draw models of the digestve tracts of ruminants, horses, pigs, and poultry. Explain how the diferences in structure and
functon among these types of digestve tracts afect the nutritonal management of these species.|3. Explain the diferences in the digestve systems of ruminants, horses, pigs, and poultry. Explain how the diferences in
structure and functon among these types of digestve tracts afect the nutritonal management of these species.| |Animal Science|5. Do the following:
a. Explain how agricultural producers make desired improvements to their stock through breeding practces and programs.
b. Explain the terms pure breeding and cross breeding.
c. List two modern technologies used in the producton of horses, dairy catle, beef catle, sheep, hogs, poultry, or goats. and tell
what the advantages are of using the technology.|5. Explain the importance of setng clear goals for any animal breeding program. Tell how purebred lines of animals are
produced. Explain the practce of crossbreeding and the value of this practce.| |Animal Science|**Sheep or Goat Opton (choose one and complete the following based upon the choice)**
6.
a. Make a sketch of a live lamb or goat. Show the locaton of the various wholesale and retail cuts.
b. Discuss how wools are sorted and graded.
c. List products that can be made from the milk.
d. Based on your choice, do ONE of the following:
1. Raise a lamb or goat from weaning to market weight. Keep records of feed intake, weight gains, medicaton, vaccinaton, and
mortality. Present your records to your counselor for review.
2. Visit a farm or ranch where sheep or goats are raised. Describe what you saw and explain what you learned. If you cannot visit
a sheep or goat farm or ranch, view a video from a breed associaton, or research the Internet (with your parent's permission) for
informaton on sheep or goats. Tell about your fndings.
e. Defne the following terms for a sheep (wether, ewe, ram, lamb) OR a goat (doe, nanny, kid, buck, billy).|**Sheep Opton**
6.
a. Make a sketch of a live lamb. Show the locaton of the various wholesale and retail cuts.
b. Discuss how wools are sorted and graded.
c. Do ONE of the following:
1. Raise a lamb from weaning to market weight. Keep records of feed intake, weight gains, medicaton, vaccinaton, and
mortality. Present your records to your counselor for review.
2. Visit a farm or ranch where sheep are raised. Describe what you saw and explain what you learned. If you cannot visit a
sheep farm or ranch, view a video from a breed associaton, or research the internet (with your parent or guardian’s
permission) for informaton on sheep. Tell about your fndings.
d. Describe some diferences between the producton of purebred and commercial lambs. Then select two breeds that would
be appropriate for the producton of crossbred market lambs in your region. Identfy which breed the ram should be.
e. Defne the following terms: wether, ewe, ram, lamb.| |Archery|1. Do the following:
a. Explain what a projectle is, and why any device that shoots a projectle at high speed must be handled with care and respect,
and used only in approved locatons.
b. Explain the fve range safety rules.
c. Explain the four whistle commands used on the range.
d. Explain how to safely remove arrows from the target and return them to your quiver.
e. Tell your counselor about your local and state laws for owning and using archery equipment.|1. Do the following:
a. Explain the fve range safety rules.
b. Explain the four whistle commands used on the range.
c. Explain how to safely remove arrows from the target and return them to your quiver.
d. Tell about your local and state laws for owning and using archery equipment.| |Archery|5. Working under the supervision of a certfed USA Archery Level 1 Instructor or a certfed long-term camp staf member (i.e., a
Natonal Camping School [NCS] Range Actvites Director; or a Rangemaster over age 18 who is trained by a NCS Range Actvites
Director or by a USA Archery Level 1 Instructor), do ONE of the following Optons:|5. Do ONE of the following optons.| |Archery|**Opton A: Recurve Bow or Longbow**
5
a. Name and point to the parts of the recurve or longbow you are shootng.
b. Explain how to properly care for and store recurve bows and longbows.
c. Demonstrate and explain USA Archery’s eleven “Steps of Shootng” for the bow you are shootng.
d. Demonstrate the proper way to string a recurve bow or longbow.
e. Using a bow square, locate and mark with dental foss, crimp-on, or other method, the nocking point on the bowstring of the
bow that you are using.
f. Do ONE of the following:
1. Using a recurve or longbow and arrows with a fnger release, shoot a single round of ONE of the following:
a. NFAA feld round of 14 targets and make a score of 60 points
b. Scoutng America feld round of 14 targets and make a score of 80 points
c. A World Archery/USA Archery indoor round and make a score of 80 points (indoor rounds may be shot outdoors if this is more
convenient)
d. An NFAA indoor round and make a score of 50 points (indoor rounds may be shot outdoors if this is more convenient)
2. Shootng 30 arrows in fve-arrow ends at an 80-centmeter (32-inch) fve-color target at 10 yards and using the 10 scoring
regions, make a score of 150 points.|5 Opton A—Using a Recurve Bow or Longbow
a. Name and point to the parts of the recurve or longbow you are shootng.
b. Explain how to properly care for and store recurve bows and longbows.
c. Demonstrate and explain USA Archery’s “Steps of Shootng” for the bow you are shootng.
d. Demonstrate the proper way to string a recurve bow or longbow.
e. Using a bow square, locate and mark with dental foss, crimp-on, or other method, the nocking point on the bowstring of
the bow that you are using.
f. Do ONE of the following:
1. Using a recurve or longbow and arrows with a fnger release, shoot a single round of one of the following BSA, USA Archery,
or NFAA rounds:
a. An NFAA feld round of 14 targets and make a score of 60 points
b. A BSA Scout feld round of 14 targets and make a | score of 80 points
c. World Archery/USA Archery indoor* round and make a score of 80 points
d. An NFAA indoor* round and make a score of 50 points
*The indoor rounds can be shot outdoors if this is more convenient.
2. Shootng 30 arrows in fve-arrow ends at an 80-centmeter (32-inch fve-color target at 10 yards and using the 10 scoring
regions, make a score of 150 points.
3. As a member of the USA Archery Junior Olympic Archery Development program (JOAD), earn your indoor or outdoor green,
purple, and gray achievement award pins using a recurve bow or longbow.
4. As a member of the NFAA’s Junior Division, earn a Cub or Youth 100-score Progression Patch.| ## **SCOUTS BSA MERIT BADGE COMMITTEE Merit Badge Requirement Updates (Effective January 1, 2025)** |**MERIT BADGE**|**UPDATED REQUIREMENT**|**ORIGINAL REQUIREMENT**| |---|---|---| |Archery|5
a. Name and point to the parts of the compound bow you are shootng.
b. Explain how to properly care for and store compound bows.
c. Demonstrate and explain USA Archery’s eleven “Steps of Shootng” for the bow you are shootng.
d. Explain why it is necessary to have the string or cable on a compound bow replaced at an archery shop.
e. Locate and mark with dental foss, crimp-on, or other method, the nocking point on the bowstring of the bow that you are using.
f. Do ONE of the following:
1. Using a compound bow and arrows with a fnger release, shoot a single round of ONE of the following:
a. NFAA feld round of 14 targets and make a score of 70 points
b. Scoutng America feld round of 14 targets and make a score of 90 points
c. World Archery/USA Archery indoor round and make a score of 90 points (indoor rounds may be shot outdoors if this is more
convenient)
d. NFAA indoor round and make a score of 60 points (indoor rounds may be shot outdoors if this is more convenient).
2. Shootng at an 80-centmeter (32-inch) fve-color target using the 10 scoring regions, make a minimum score of 160 points.
Accomplish this in the following manner:
Shoot 15 arrows in fve-arrow ends, at a distance of 10 yards
AND
Shoot 15 arrows in fve-arrow ends, at a distance of 15 yards.
**Opton B: Compound Bow**|5 Opton B—Using a Compound Bow
a. Name and point to the parts of the compound bow you are shootng.
b. Explain how to properly care for and store compound bows.
c. Demonstrate and explain USA Archery’s “Steps of Shootng” for the bow you are shootng.
d. Explain why it is necessary to have the string or cable on a compound bow replaced at an archery shop.
e. Locate and mark with dental foss, crimp-on, or other method, the nocking point on the bowstring of the bow that you are
using.
f. Do ONE of the following:
1. Using a compound bow and arrows with a fnger release, shoot a single round of one of the following BSA, USA Archery, or
NFAA rounds:
a. An NFAA feld round of 14 targets and make a score of 70 points
b. A BSA Scout feld round of 14 targets and make a score of 90 points
c. A World Archery/USA Archery indoor* round and make a score of 90 points
d. An NFAA indoor* round and make a score of 60 points
2. Shootng at an 80-centmeter (32-inch) fve-color target using the 10 scoring regions, make a minimum score of 160 points.
Accomplish this in the following manner: Shoot 15 arrows in fve-arrow ends, at a distance of 10 yards AND Shoot 15 arrows in
fve-arrow ends, at a distance of 15 yards.
3. As a member of the USA Archery Junior Olympic Archery Development program (JOAD), earn your indoor or outdoor green,
purple, and gray achievement award pins | using a compound bow.
4. As a member of the NFAA’s Junior Division, earn a Cub or Youth 100-score Progression Patch.| |Astronomy|4. Do the following (if instructon is done in a planetarium, Scouts must stll identfy the required stars and constellatons outside
under the natural night sky):
a. Identfy in the sky 10 constellatons, at least four of which are in the zodiac.
b. Identfy in the sky eight conspicuous stars, fve of which are of magnitude 1 or brighter.
c. Make two sketches of the Big Dipper or Cassiopeia from direct observaton. In one sketch, show the Big Dipper’s or
Cassiopeia's orientaton in the early evening sky. Make another sketch, showing its positon several hours later. In both sketches,
show the North Star and the horizon. Record the date and tme each sketch was made.
d. Explain what we see when we look at the Milky Way.|4. Do the following*:
a. Identfy in the sky at least 10 constellatons, at least four of which are in the zodiac.
b. Identfy in the sky at least eight conspicuous stars, fve of which are of magnitude 1 or brighter.
c. Make two sketches of the Big Dipper. In one sketch, show the Big Dipper’s orientaton in the early evening sky. In another
sketch, show its positon several hours later. In both sketches, show the North Star and the horizon. Record the date and tme
each sketch was made.
d. Explain what we see when we look at the Milky Way.| |Astronomy|6. Do the following:
a. Sketch the face of the Moon and indicate fve seas and fve craters. Label these landmarks.
b. Sketch the phase and positon of the Moon, at the same hour and place, for four nights (or days) within a one-week period by
direct observaton. Include landmarks on the horizon such as hills, trees, and buildings. Explain the changes you observe.
c. List the factors that keep the Moon in orbit around Earth.
d. With the aid of diagrams, explain the relatve positons of the Sun, Earth, and the Moon at the tmes of lunar and solar eclipses,
and at the tmes of new, frst-quarter, full, and last-quarter phases of the Moon.|6. Do the following:
a. Sketch the face of the Moon and indicate at least fve seas and fve craters. Label these landmarks.
b. Sketch the phase and positon of the Moon, at the same hour and place, for four nights within a one-week period. Include
landmarks on the horizon such as hills, trees, and buildings. Explain the changes you observe.
c. List the factors that keep the Moon in orbit around Earth.
d. With the aid of diagrams, explain the relatve positons of the Sun, Earth, and the Moon at the tmes of lunar and solar
eclipses, and at the tmes of new, frst-quarter, full, and last-quarter phases of the Moon.| |Astronomy|8b. Plan and partcipate in an observaton session that includes using binoculars or a telescope and includes at least 10 celestal
objects beyond those observed in requirement 4. These might be lunar features, Messier objects, additonal constellatons or
planets, or artfcial satellites including the Internatonal Space Staton. List the celestal objects you want to observe and fnd
each in a star chart, guidebook or by using an app. Prepare a log or notebook. Discuss with your counselor what you hope to
observe prior to your observaton session. Review your log or notebook with your counselor aferward.|8b. Plan and partcipate in a three-hour observaton session that includes using binoculars or a telescope. List the celestal
objects you want to observe and fnd each on a star chart or in a guidebook. Prepare a log or notebook. Discuss with your
counselor what you hope to observe prior to your observaton session. Review your log or note-book with your counselor
aferward.| |Astronomy|(new) 8f. With your parent or guardian and counselor's approval, use online observing opportunites such as SkyNet to observe
planets, comets, galaxies, and/or the sun. Describe your experience to your counslor.|| |Astronomy|9. Do ONE of the following:
a. Identfy three career opportunites that would use skills and knowledge in astronomy. Pick one and research the training,
educaton, certfcaton requirements, experience, and expenses associated with entering the feld. Research the prospects for
employment, startng salary, advancement opportunites and career goals associated with this career. Discuss what you learned
with your counselor and whether you might be interested in this career.
b. Identfy how you might use the skills and knowledge in astronomy to pursue a personal hobby. Research the additonal
training required, expenses, and afliaton with organizatons that would help you maximize the enjoyment and beneft
you might gain from it. Discuss what you learned with your counselor and share what short-term and long-term goals you might
have if you pursued this.|9. Find out about three career opportunites in astronomy. Pick one and fnd out the educaton, training, and experience
required for this profession. Discuss this with your counselor, and explain why this profession might interest you.| ## **SCOUTS BSA MERIT BADGE COMMITTEE Merit Badge Requirement Updates (Effective January 1, 2025)** |**MERIT BADGE**|**UPDATED REQUIREMENT**|**ORIGINAL REQUIREMENT**| |---|---|---| |Aviaton|1.
Do the following:
a. Defne “aircraf”. Describe three kinds of aircraf today, and their typical uses.
b. Provide a brief overview of the evoluton of fight, and discuss three notable tmes in history important to aviaton.
c. Explain the diference between a fxed wing and a rotary wing aircraf, and the benefts of each.
d. Explain the operaton of piston, turbine, and jet engines.
e. Using a model aircraf, describe the four forces that act on an aircraf in fight.
f. Explain how an airfoil generates lif, specifcally notng Bernoulli’s principle.
g. Identfy and describe the aerodynamic control surfaces on the aircraf of your choice, and explain how they operate to control
its at ude and directon of fight.
h. Explain the purposes and functons of the various instruments found in a typical single-engine aircraf: at ude indicator,
heading indicator, altmeter, airspeed indicator, turn and bank indicator, vertcal speed indicator, compass, navigaton,
communicaton, and engine performance indicators.
**Aviaton Basics and Mechanics of Flight.**|1. Do the following:
a. Defne “aircraf.” Describe some kinds and uses of aircraf today. Explain the operaton of piston, turboprop, and jet
engines.
b. Point out on a model airplane the forces that act on an airplane in fight.
c. Explain how an airfoil generates lif, how the primary control surfaces (ailerons, elevators, and rudder) afect the airplane’s
at ude, and how a propeller produces thrust.
d. Demonstrate how the control surfaces of an airplane are used for takeof, straight climb, level turn, climbing turn,
descending turn, straight descent, and landing.
e. Explain the following: the sport pilot, the recreatonal pilot, and the private pilot certfcates; the instrument ratng.| |Aviaton|2.
Do ONE of the following:
a. Build a model FPG-9. Get others in your troop or patrol to make their own model, then organize a competton to test the
precision of fight and landing of the models.
b. Build a rubber-band driven balsa wood airplane. Fly the plane for 25 feet, with a successful take-of and landing.
c. Build (or obtain) a fuel-driven or batery-powered electric model aircraf or drone. Obtain The Recreatonal UAS Safety Test
(TRUST) certfcaton, and fy the aircraf with a successful take-of and landing.
**Principles of Flight.**|2. Do TWO of the following:
a. Take a fight in an aircraf, with your parent or guardian’s permission. Record the date, place, type of aircraf, and duraton
of fight, and report on your impressions of the fight.
b. Under supervision, perform a prefight inspecton of a light airplane.
c. Obtain and learn how to read an aeronautcal chart. Measure a true course on the chart. Correct it for magnetc variaton,
compass deviaton, and wind drif to determine a compass heading.
d. Using one of many fight simulator sofware packages available for computers, “fy” the course and heading you
established in requirement 2c or another course you have ploted.
e. Explain the purposes and functons of the various instruments found in a typical single-engine aircraf: at ude indicator,
heading indicator, altmeter, airspeed indicator, turn and bank indicator, vertcal speed indicator, compass, navigaton (GPS and
VOR) and communicaton radios, tachometer, oil pressure gauge, and oil temperature gauge.
f. Create an original poster of an aircraf instrument panel. Include and identfy the instruments and radios discussed in
requirement 2e.| |Aviaton|3.
Do TWO of the following:
a. Using a fight simulator sofware package, set a course and fy the headings you have established with a successful take-of
and landing.
b. Under supervision, perform a prefight inspecton of an aircraf.
c. Observe and/or partcipate in an aircraf maintenance actvity. Describe the maintenance schedule and requirements for an
aircraf of your choice.
d. Obtain and learn how to read an aeronautcal chart. Measure a true course on the chart; correct it for magnetc variaton,
compass deviaton, and wind drif to determine a navigatonal heading for an aircraf.
e. With your parents or guardian’s permission, take a discovery fight in an aircraf. Record the date, place, type of aircraf, and
duraton of fight. Report on your impressions of the fight.
**Flight Operatons.**|3. Do ONE of the following:
a. Build and fy a fuel-driven or batery-powered electric model airplane. Describe safety rules for building and fying model
airplanes. Tell safety rules for use of glue, paint, dope, plastcs, fuel, and batery pack.
b. Build a model FPG-9. Get others in your troop or patrol to make their own model, then organize a competton to test the
precision of fight and landing of the models.| |Aviaton|4.
Do ONE of the following:
a. Visit an airport. Afer the visit, report on how the facilites are used, how runways are numbered, and how runways are
determined to be "actve."
b. Visit a Federal Aviaton Administraton facility: Airport Trafc Control Tower (ATCT), Terminal Radar Approach Control (TRACON),
Air Route Trafc Control Center (ARTCC), or Flight Standards District Ofce (FSDO). Report on the operaton and your impressions
of the facility.
c. Visit a military aviaton facility. Learn how that facility supports defense and/or civilian actvites. Report on the operaton and
your impressions of the facility.
d. Visit an aviaton museum or atend an air show. Report on your impressions of the museum or show, and what you learned from
the experience.
**Airport Operatons.**|4. Do ONE of the following:
a. Visit an airport. Afer the visit, report on how the facilites are used, how runways are numbered, and how runways are
determined to be “actve.”
b. Visit a Federal Aviaton Administraton facility—a control tower, terminal radar control facility, air route trafc control center,
or Flight Standards District Ofce. (Phone directory listngs are under U.S. Government Ofces, Transportaton Department,
Federal Aviaton Administraton. Call in advance.) Report on the operaton and your impressions of the facility.
c. Visit an aviaton museum or atend an air show. Report on your impressions of the museum or show.| ## **SCOUTS BSA MERIT BADGE COMMITTEE Merit Badge Requirement Updates (Effective January 1, 2025)** |**MERIT BADGE**|**UPDATED REQUIREMENT**|**ORIGINAL REQUIREMENT**| |---|---|---| |Aviaton|5.
Do the following:
a. Explain the following: the student pilot, the recreatonal pilot, the remote pilot, and the private pilot certfcates.
b. Describe the benefts of the instrument ratng.
c. Explain the following: the commercial pilot certfcate, the airline transport pilot certfcate, and certfed fight instructor (CFI).
d. Identfy an Aviaton Exploring Post and/or Civil Air Patrol facility in your area. Learn about their actvites and membership
requirements.
e. Identfy three career opportunites that would use skills and knowledge in aviaton. Pick one and research the training,
educaton, certfcaton requirements, experience, and expenses associated with entering the feld. Research the prospects for
employment, startng salary, advancement opportunites and career goals associated with this career. Discuss what you learned
with your counselor and whether you might be interested in this career.
**Personal & Professional Aviaton Opportunites.**|5. Find out about three career opportunites in aviaton. Pick one and fnd out the educaton, training, and experience required
for this profession. Discuss this with your counselor, and explain why this profession might interest you.| |Chemistry|1. Do the following and discuss with your counselor:
a. Obtain safety data sheets (SDS) for sucrose (sugar), isopropyl alcohol (rubbing alcohol), and a waterproofng spray. Compare
their propertes and potental hazard(s), including handling and disposal guidelines and toxicity informaton.
b. What types of personal protectve equipment (PPE) are recommended for each, and why? How does the recommended PPE
change with toxicity and route of exposure?
c. Review the pictograms in Secton 2 of each SDS. What do they mean, and how do you see them used in your community?
d. Discuss the safe storage of chemicals. How does the safe storage of chemicals apply to your home, your school, your
community, and the environment?|1. Do EACH of the following:
a. Describe three examples of safety equipment used in a chemistry laboratory and the reason each one is used.
b. Describe what a safety data sheet (SDS) is and tell why it is used.
c. Obtain an SDS for both a paint and an insectcide. Compare and discuss the toxicity, disposal, and safe-handling sectons
for these two commonhousehold products.
d. Discuss the safe storage of chemicals. How does safe storage apply to your home, your school, your community, and the
environment?| |Chemistry|2.
Afer successfully completng requirement 1, do the following:
a. Discuss with your counselor why you think soap and alcohol are used to clean cuts and scrapes. Discuss how you could test
your hypotheses with your counselor.
b. Coat your hands with a mixture of cooking oil and a nontoxic powder, such as sand or cocoa. Wash your hands with water and
observe what happens. Coat your hands again, then wash with soap and water. Discuss the diferences with your counselor.
c. Perform an experiment to demonstrate how rubbing alcohol afects microbial growth using baker’s yeast. Discuss the results
with your counselor.
**Chemistry and First Aid.**|2. Do EACH of the following:
a. Predict what would happen if you placed an iron nail in a copper sulfate soluton. Then, put an iron nail in a copper sulfate
soluton. Describe your observatons and make a conclusion based on your observatons. Compare your predicton and original
conclusion with what actually happened. Write the formula for the reacton that you described.
b. Demonstrate how you would separate sand (or gravel) from water. Describe how you would separate table salt from water,
oil from water, and gasoline from motor oil. Name the practcal processes that require these kinds of separatons and how the
processes may difer.
c. Describe the diference between a chemical reacton and a physical change. Observe one of each and share your
observatons with your counselor.| |Chemistry|3.
Afer successfully completng requirement 1, do EACH of the following:
a. Perform a Maillard, or browning, reacton. Discuss with your counselor whether a physical or chemical change has occurred,
and what happened to molecules in the food during the cooking process.
b. Prepare and use red cabbage indicator to measure the pH of fve common household liquids, including water. Discuss your
observatons with your counselor.
**Chemistry in the Kitchen.**|3. Construct a Cartesian diver. Describe its functon in terms of how gases in general behave under diferent pressures and
diferent temperatures. Describe how the behavior of gases afects a backpacker at high alttudes and a scuba diver
underwater.| |Chemistry|4.
Afer successfully completng requirement 1, do TWO of the following:
a. Design and conduct an experiment to compare at least two diferent types of waterproofng. Discuss with your counselor which
one you would prefer to use on your tent and which one you would prefer to use on your boots, and why.
b. Describe the four classes of fres, and the four classes of fre extnguishers. Discuss with your counselor how sand, baking
soda, and the four classes of extnguishers work to put out fres.
c. Under your counselor’s supervision and/or the supervision of a knowledgeable adult approved in advance by your counselor,
conduct fame tests of at least fve elements using a kitchen or propane torch.
d. Under your counselor’s supervision and/or the supervision of a knowledgeable adult approved in advance by your counselor,
synthesize nylon (may be from a kit).
**Chemistry and Camping.**|4. Do EACH of the following:
a. Cut a round onion into small chunks. Separate the onion chunks into three equal portons. Leave the frst porton raw. Cook
the second porton of onion chunks untl the pieces are translucent. Cook the third porton untl the onions are caramelized, or

title: Scouts Bsa Facilitator Guide 2025
heading: Scouts Bsa Facilitator Guide 2025
text:
## **Scouts BSA Position Specific Training Facilitators Guide** 

**==> picture [216 x 298] intentionally omitted <==**

**==> picture [126 x 69] intentionally omitted <==**

Copyright © 2025 Scouting U, Scouting America 

All rights reserved.  No part of this product may be reproduced, distributed, or transmitted in any form or by any means, including photocopying, recording, or other electronic or mechanical methods, without the prior written permission of the publisher. Document Revision Date: 09/30/2025 

## Contents 

Why Training? ..................................................................................................................... 3 Presentation of Modules .................................................................................................... 3 Training Requirements ........................................................................................................ 4 Scoutmaster & Assistant Scoutmaster Training ................................................................. 6 References: ..................................................................................................................... 7 Handouts: ........................................................................................................................ 7 Merit Badge Counselor Training ......................................................................................... 8 References: ..................................................................................................................... 8 Handouts ......................................................................................................................... 8 Committee Chair Training: .................................................................................................. 9 References: ................................................................................................................... 10 Handouts: ...................................................................................................................... 10 Committee Member Training ........................................................................................... 11 References: ................................................................................................................... 11 Handouts: ...................................................................................................................... 12 

2 | P a g e 

## **Why Training?** 

To do anything well, people need training, in whatever form that takes. Training may be formal or informal, online or face-to-face; it may help someone learn to ride a bicycle, drive a car, or perform better at a job. Training can be conducted in a group setting or individually. In the end, the result of high-quality training is a person’s ability to do something new or better, and a feeling of satisfaction from the accomplishment. 

How effectively the Boy Scouts of America influences the lives of youth depends on its leaders and their ability to apply the aims and methods of Scouting—these are the keys. Developing the ability to use these keys is what these courses are all about. 

## **Presentation of Training Modules:** 

The content covered in these courses is exactly the same as is available online, thus whether taken online or in a face-to-face setting all leaders receive the same content.  During face-toface training, Facilitators should load and run each module (download from the internet in advance) and then lead a short discussion following each presentation. Thus, the Facilitator should be familiar with the material prior to presenting them to others. 

It will be impossible to cover everything that might be on the minds of all participants! Specific and detailed questions should be addressed during breaks as well as during follow-up coaching sessions with the troop’s unit commissioner. Facilitators should try to follow the recommended timing charts in this Guide as is possible in order to finish in a timely manner. 

The presentation of these training modules will require a computer linked display device (e.g., monitor or video projector). The modules can be downloaded to the computer from https://filestore.scouting.org/filestore/training/ScoutsBSA/ScoutsBSAoffline.zip . Once downloaded you can unzip the file and there will be individual zip files for each module. Unzip each module.  To run a module – open the directory created for the module when unzipped and double-click the index_scorm.html file to launch the module. 

It is recommended that you use Google Chrome for running the modules. 

Consider giving the References material and Handouts to participants on a Thumb Drive. Recording of course completion is done by using the Training Attendance Form in the Guide to Leader Training on Scouting.org. The previous course codes, indicated in each training schedule enclosed, still apply. 

It is recommended that coffee, juice, donuts, fruit, etc. be available for the registration and break times. 

3 | P a g e 

## **Scouts BSA Position Trained Requirements:** 

The following table details the courses included in the online Learning Plans associated with the positions of Scoutmaster and Assistant Scoutmaster, Merit Badge Counselor, Troop Committee Chair and Troop Committee Member, as well as the timeframe in which they should be completed. This information provides training facilitators an overview of position trained requirements and highlights the extent of course overlap associated with most positions. 

**PLEASE NOTE THAT HAZARDOUS WEATHER TRAINING IS A PREREQUISITE THAT MUST BE COMPLETED IN THE BSA LEARN CENTER PRIOR TO ATTENDING THIS COURSE FOR SCOUTMASTER AND ASSISTANT SCOUTMASTER.  CREDIT SHOULD NOT BE GIVEN FOR COMPLETION WITHOUT EVIDENCE OF THIS REQUIREMENT.** 

4 | P a g e 

Note: Once a module is completed for one course, it will also apply toward any other position requiring that same module. 

SCOUTMASTER & ASSISTANT SCOUTMASTER BEFORE FIRST MEETING SCOUTING ORGANIZATION AIMS & METHODS OF SCOUTS BSA ROLE OF UNIT KEY 3 ROLES OF SCOUTMASTER AND SPL TROOP MEETING FIRST 30 DAYS 

PATROL METHOD ADVANCEMENT PATROL LEADERS COUNCIL MEETING FIRST 60 DAYS OUTDOOR PROGRAMS TROOP COMMITTEE UNIFORMS FOR SCOUTS BSA POSITION TRAINED ANNUAL TROOP PROGRAM PLANNING INTRO TO MERIT BADGE(S) OUTDOOR ETHICS HAZARDOUS WEATHER TRAINING

TROOP COMMITTEE MEMBER 

BEFORE FIRST MEETING

ADVANCEMENT 

5 | P a g e 

## **Scoutmaster & Assistant Scoutmaster Training:** 

Scoutmaster Position-Specific training, course Code S24, is the BSA’s initial level of training for adult leaders of Scouts BSA troops. It is designed primarily for Scoutmasters and assistant Scoutmasters. However, committee members and other adults connected to a Scouts BSA troop are welcome to attend. 

When Scoutmaster Position-Specific training and Introduction to Outdoor Leader Skills (IOLS) have both been completed, new adult leaders are considered “Trained” and will have the tools needed to conduct an effective Scouting program. 

The face-to-face style of Scoutmaster Position-Specific training requires approximately five (5) hours to complete. The following presentation sequence is recommended to facilitate finishing in a timely manner.

## **Merit Badge Counselor Training:** 

This session, course Code D76, is the BSA’s training for Merit Badge Counselors. Although designed primarily for Merit badge Counselors, committee members and other interested adults are welcome to attend. 

The face-to-face Merit Badge Counselor Position-Specific training requires about one and onehalf (1½) hours to complete. The following presentation sequence is recommended to facilitate finishing in a timely manner.

title: Training Resources
heading: Training Resources
text:
---
authority_level: official_policy
scraped_date: '2026-03-18'
slug: training-resources
source: scouting.org
source_url: https://www.scouting.org/training/resources-for-program-trainers-training-committees/
title: Resources for Program Trainers and Training Committees
---

I’m a NEW Scouting America Volunteer – What’s next? Training Why Scouting America Training? One of the important tasks you are asked to do as you join and continue to be a part of scouting is to be a trained leader. Why? Our youth depend on it and depend on YOU to keep them safe and provide a FUN, and engaging program. . . and training makes your job as a leader fun and easier as well! Trained leaders understand Scouting America’s mission, vision, purpose, methods and values. Trained leaders deliver the promise of scouting to our youth – the promise of adventure, leadership and growth. A trained leader is knowledgeable and more confident in the role being performed. Trained leaders impact the quality of programs, safety, and a whole lot more. In other words, a trained leader is better prepared to make Scouting all it can be! How can I get trained? Here are four steps to becoming a trained leader. 1. Set up a my.scouting account Visit My.Scouting and create an account. Once you have an account you can access online training. 2. Complete Safeguarding Youth Training This is the first training that all leaders must take and is a part of our commitment to keeping our youth safe. Scouting America places the greatest importance on creating the most secure environment possible for our youth members. This training is to be taken every year. https://www.scouting.org/training/youth-protection/ 3. Complete Hazardous Weather Training This training is required for all adults that work directly with youth. It must be taken every two years. You can find this training at https://www.scouting.org/training/health-and-safety/ . 4. Position Specific Training Scouting America’s Learning Center, at My.Scouting , offers training for all positions for adult leaders in Scouting. Select your position and a learning plan will be created for you. The plan includes modules that are designed to allow you to complete them at your own pace. To access training after the initial set up, log into my.scouting and click on My Training from the dropdown menu. From My Training page, click on Requirements to see what trainings are required for your position. To learn more about the Leader Specific Training for your program, visit: https://www.scouting.org/training/position-specific-courses/ . Additional training information can be located at https://www.scouting.org/training/ Scouting U continually updates its polices and training materials to make sure that our program is relevant and reflects best practices for keeping our youth safe and engaged in the program. The “Trained to Make a Difference” campaign aims to increase the number of trained direct contact leaders at all levels of councils. Our goal is to improve the percentage of trained direct contact leaders to 55% in all councils; we have a ways to go! Below are links to assets you can use for your campaign: Trained to Make a Difference logo Trained Leader Flyer (11×17) Trained Leader Flyer (8.5×11) Quality Council Scorecard Cub Scout Position-Specific Training Facilitated Learning Materials ( for use by Council/District Training Staff ) Instructor Syllabus (Updated Sept 2025) Leaders who complete either the online or facilitated training are considered trained for their position. Basic Adult Leader Outdoor Orientation (BALOO) (Updated Oct 2025) This one-and-a-half-day course is designed as an introduction to the Cub Scout outdoor program for leaders interested in adding a camping component to their Pack activities. BALOO training consists of an online pre-requisite component in addition to an overnight hands on practical. Click here to access the prerequisite training. Scouting America’s Cub Scout level camping policies will be taught along with the discovery of the necessary tools to help units carry out a successful camping experience. Completion of this course is mandatory for a MINIMUM OF ONE adult on a Pack overnighter. Facilitated Learning Materials ( for use by Council/District Training Staff ) Instructor Syllabus This course should not be taught with IOLS. As part of a two-part blended learning format, the on-line modules are the first step to become BALOO trained. The on-line training modules contain introductory and basic information that will be built upon during the practical training that is completed during an in-person, council instructed course. The on-line component must be completed prior to the practical component and the course facilitator should check this before allowing enrollment into this part of the course. The in-person syllabus is facilitated by the council training committee and the syllabus must be followed to include the overnight portion of the training. Otherwise the training code will not be awarded. CubChat Live Presentations that provide information on topics of interest to Cub Scouts. Cub Chat Live (every Friday at 2pm CT) – Facebook and YouTube Scouts BSA Position-Specific Training This training is intended to provide the following positions with the information and tools they need to conduct a successful program: Scoutmaster/Assistant Scoutmaster Merit Badge Counselor (for teaching Citizenship in Society Merit Badge please take the Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion in Scouting Volunteer Training – click here to go to the course.) Troop Committee Troop Committee Chair Scoutmasters and assistant Scoutmasters who complete the online or facilitated training and Introduction to Outdoor Leader Skills are considered “trained” for those positions. All other positions who complete the online or facilitated training are considered “trained” for their positions. Facilitated Learning Materials ( for use by Council/District Training Staff ) Instructor Syllabus (Updated Sep 2025) Download Instructions for Scouts BSA Offline Modules Offline Versions of modules for syllabus Introduction to Outdoor Leader Skills (IOLS) Working as patrols, this hands-on course provides adult leaders the practical outdoor skills they need to lead Scouts in the out-of-doors. Upon completion, leaders should feel comfortable teaching Scouts the basic skills required to obtain the First Class rank. Along with Scoutmaster Specifics this course is required of all direct contact leaders registered in Scouts BSA Troops in order to be considered “trained”. Facilitated Learning Materials ( for use by Council/District Training Staff ) Instructor Syllabus Spanish Version Introduction to Leadership Skills for Troops (ILST) The purpose of the ILST course is to teach Scouts with leadership positions about their new roles and how to most effectively reach success in that role. It is intended to help Scouts in leadership positions within their troop understand their responsibilities and to equip them with organizational and leadership skills to fulfill those responsibilities. ILST is the first course in the series of leadership training offered to Scouts and is a replacement for Troop Leadership Training. Completion of ILST is a prerequisite for Scouts to participate in the more advanced leadership courses National Youth Leadership Training (NYLT) and the National Advanced Youth Leadership Experience (NAYLE). View The ILST Syllabus Troop Position Description Cards The Adventure Plan (TAP) – CURRENTLY UNAVAILABLE The Adventure Plan (TAP) is now available on tap.scouting.org, which provides Unit leaders with a digital toolkit of all resources useful in planning and conducting outdoor adventures. Troop Talk Live! Presentations that provide information on topics of interest to Scouts BSA leaders. Troop Talk Live (second Wednesday of each month at 2 pm CT) – Facebook and YouTube Venturing Position-Specific Training This training is intended to provide the following positions with the information and tools they need to conduct a successful program: Crew Advisor/Associate Advisor Merit Badge Counselor Crew Committee Crew Committee Chair Crew Board of Review Leaders who complete the online or facilitated training are considered “trained” for those positions. Facilitated Learning Materials ( for use by Council/District Training Staff ) Instructor’s Syllabus Spanish Syllabus Crew Committee Challenge The Crew Committee Challenge is designed for crew committee members and becomes the course (along with the Venturing version of Youth Protection training) crew committee members need to be considered “trained.” in lieu of the online training. The instructor guide below is for trainers to conduct the course for crew committee members. Instructor Syllabus Introduction to Leadership Skills for Crews (ILSC) The Introduction to Leadership Skills for Crews Course (ILSC) has been created to help Venturers learn and practice the attitudes and skills that good leaders demonstrate. The course is a series of lessons divided into three modules: Leading Self, Leading Others, and Leading Crews. The syllabus contains six and a half hours of instruction, but a course length may vary depending on facilitators’ choice of games, the content chosen for more extensive discussion, and the needs of the crew. Completion of ILSC is a prerequisite for Venturers to participate in the more advanced leadership courses, such as, National Youth Leadership Training (NYLT), National Advanced Youth Leadership Experience (NAYLE) and Kodiak. The 2021 Revision of Introduction to Leadership Skills for Crews (ILSC) instructor-led course includes the following major changes: This version of ILSC was reviewed by the NYLT task force to ensure that it was compatible with the new NYLT syllabus Course format revised to make it easier to read online or as a downloaded document. EDGE is now referred to as Scouting EDGE to align with NYLT during the Team Development session. Leadership tied to the Venturing motto “Lead the Adventure” Venturing terminology updated Vision session revised to align with NYLT “Start with Why” (reference to mission statement eliminated). Distinction between delegating and tasking added to the session on delegating. Session on calendaring (time management) added Session on keeping the crew on task added New games/activities with options added (a few activities were “retired”). Feedback from Venturers included as sidebars. View The ILSC Syllabus Crew Officer Orientation Training This training will assist your Crew in understanding the roles and responsibilities of Crew officers, as well as how to effectively plan meetings and events. It is recommended that this training be conducted annually with the entire Crew to ensure all members comprehend the officers’ roles. Additionally, this training is required for advancement to the Discovery Rank. Download the Facilitator Guide. Passport to High Adventure A guidebook to enable older Scouts and Venturers, with guidance from their adult leaders, to plan and safely carry out unit high-adventure treks using Leave No Trace techniques. Sea Scouts Basic Leader Training Leaders who complete the online or facilitated training are considered “trained” for those positions. Facilitated Learning Materials ( for use by Council/District Training Staff ) Instructor’s Syllabus Click here for other Sea Scouts Leader Training such as Seabadge. National Courses Wood Badge National Youth Leadership Training (NYLT) National Youth Leadership Training is an exciting, action-packed program designed for councils to provide youth members with leadership skills and experience they can use in their home crews. The program is usually presented by individual Scout councils in a week-long program, although it can be split over two weekends. To learn more click here. National Advanced Youth Leadership Experience (NAYLE) The National Advanced Youth Leadership Experience (NAYLE) is an exciting program where Venturers can enhance their leadership skills at the National High Adventure bases. Scouts will expand upon team-building and ethical decision-making skills learned in National Youth Leadership Training (NYLT). To learn more click here. NYLT Leadership Academy The NYLT Leadership Academy trains youth staff to be top-notch presenters, evaluators, and leaders of their councils’ National Youth Leadership Training (NYLT) courses. This intensive one-week course will improve course (NYLT) knowledge and problem-solving skills, which will help them run world-class NYLT programs. To learn more Click Here Leadership Challenge Philmont Leadership Challenge – click here to learn more. Powder Horn The decision has been made to retire the Powder Horn Training Course. We would like to thank all the volunteers and youth who helped create and carryout Powder Horn throughout its existence. Wood Badge / NYLT Course Director Conference Registration For schedule and registration links click here. Request for Authorization to Conduct a National Training Course (Wood Badge and NYLT) The first step for a council to conduct one of these advanced training courses is to submit a request form. Directions for sumittal are on the form. Request Form Request for Waiver The History of Wood Badge Book Wood Badge Branding Movie License FAQ Please use this link for information on movie licenses.

# SCOUTS BSA TROOP LEADER GUIDEBOOK VOLUME 1 (OFFICIAL HANDBOOKS BOY SCOUTS OF AMERICA 19)

# Contents

## Introduction: Welcome to Scouts BSA

# INTRODUCTION: WELCOME TO SCOUTS BSA

Whether you are a Scoutmaster,

an assistant Scoutmaster,

or a parent volunteer, you play a

key role in delivering this program

to the youth of your community.

## Scouts BSA: A Program As Strong As Its Leaders

Somewhere this evening, a Scoutmaster will watch proudly as a parent or guardian pins an Eagle Scout badge on their Scout's uniform. Somewhere else, a young Scout will feel her heart soak as the most tender of Tenderfoot Scouts make it through a campout without losing a compass—or their composure.

Somewhere tomorrow, a parent or guardian will smile while teaching a young daughter with words once heard from a Scoutmaster years ago. Somewhere, a young Scout facing an ethical challenge will hear the words echo internally, "A Scout is trustworthy."

And somewhere years from now, you will look back in awe at the lives you have touched, at the adults your youth have become.

Scouts BSA is a results-oriented program for developing leaders of character. Its alumni include some of the most prominent figures on the national stage, along with key leaders in communities across the country and around the globe. In fact, however, the program is only as strong as you make it. Your leadership and enthusiasm will give your Scouts all the support you need so you can give your Scouts all the opportunities they deserve.

## Support Resources

Our support for you starts with this two-volume Troop Leader Guidebook. Volume 1 offers an introduction to the Scouts BSA program and addresses new or relatively inexperienced leaders. Volume 2 is designed to be useful. Volume 2 goes into more advanced topics, everything from visioning and high-adventure planning to conflict resolution and working with Scouts with special needs.

> See the appendix for an extensive list of resources, including catalog numbers and internet references.

But troop leader support only begins with paper and ink. The BSA website, www.scouting.org, offers extensive resources that complement the Troop Leader Guidebook, many of which we will highlight in these pages.

You also have access to training opportunities, both online and in person. Chapter 18 covers adult leader training in detail. Training is critically important to your success—and satisfaction—as a leader.

Finally, you can count on support from other Scouting volunteers. In fact, you are not alone in your district. One is your unit commissioner; an experienced volunteer whose sole responsibility is to support your unit. The other is your district executive, a professional who oversees the whole Scouting program in your area.

We offer these resources because we want you to be successful. We know that you are uniquely positioned to make a difference in the lives of the youth in your community; youth who will one day make a difference in their communities, our nation, and the world.

Thank you, and welcome to Scouts BSA!

## Troop Leader Essentials

These BSA publications are troop leader essentials that provide valuable information and insight:

• **Scouts BSA Handbook for Girls**—The primary resource for Scouts (and for Scout leaders who need to learn basic fundamentals.

• **Fieldbook**—A companion volume to the Scouts BSA handbooks that covers advanced outdoor skills

• **Troop Leader Guidebook (Volumes 1 and 2)**—Resources for Scoutmasters and assistant Scoutmasters.

• **Troop Committee Guidebook**—The primary resource for troop committee members.

• **Program Features and Outing Ideas (Volumes 1, 2, and 3)**—Complete monthly program features, 16 per volume, that include meeting plans, outing ideas.

• **Scouts BSA Requirements**—Requirements for ranks, merit badges, and special awards.

• **Guide to Uniforms and Insignia**—A guide to wearing uniforms and badges correctly.

• **Guide to Advancement**—The official source for administering advancement in all Boy Scouts of America program phases.

• **Guide to Safe Scouting**—The primary source for information on conducting Scouting activities in a safe and prudent manner.

• **Patrol Leader Handbook**—The official guide for patrol leaders.

• **Senior Patrol Leader Handbook**—The official guide for senior patrol leaders.

• **Scouting magazine**—The official online magazine for Scout leaders and parents; scoutingmagazine.org

• **Scout Life magazine**—The BSA's official youth magazine, published 10 times a year; scoutlife.org

• **Scouts.bsa**—www.scouting.org/programs/scouts-bsa

# SCOUT BASICS

Knowing the basics of Scouts BSA helps keep troop leaders focused on how to apply those basics and how the program works.

## Scouts BSA Defined

Scouts BSA serves youth aged 10 or 11 through 18. Its mission is the same as the larger organization: to prepare young people to make ethical and moral choices over their lifetimes by instilling in them the values of the Scout Oath and Scout Law. Scouts BSA achieves this mission through its interdependent methods.

## The Aims of Scouts BSA

The aims are character development, citizenship training, leadership, and mental and physical fitness.

### Character Development

Character has been defined as what someone does when no one is looking. It is the set of ethical and moral principles that guide a person's every decision. As a leader, you have an opportunity to study for the soul of a friend, whether to suit the world or whether to suit God's truth. It takes time to step backward or backward in a time of crisis.

"Youth don't inherit character as they inherit height or hair color. Even if they can learn character in a classroom as they might learn algebra or grammar. Instead, they develop character in the crucible of experience. Character is not developed in ease and quiet. Only through experience of trial and suffering can the soul be strengthened, ambition inspired, and success achieved."

---

# CHAPTER 1
## Mission, Aims, Methods, Ideals

> "Knowledge without character is mere pie-crust."
> —Scouting Founder Robert Baden-Powell

### Citizenship Training

Citizenship training. Citizenship is more than residency. True citizens accept the responsibilities of citizenship as quickly as they claim the rights that citizenship confers. They agree that "this will not be a good place for any of us to live in unless we make it a good place for all of us to live in." Scouts may be too young to vote, but through Scouting they can help make America a better place to live through service projects and environmental stewardship.

What is more, a Scout troop is a laboratory of democracy. By learning to lead and follow in Scouting, youth prepare themselves to be leaders and followers in their communities, religious institutions, and families.

> "Passive citizenship is not enough. He should uphold in the world the virtues of freedom, justice, and honor. Only active citizenship will do."
> —Robert Baden-Powell

### Leadership

Leadership. A leader steps up in times of crisis and is able to influence others to accomplish a specific goal. A good leader has a clear vision. Him shares that vision with others so they can willingly follow. Scouting encourages youth to learn and practice leadership skills. Every Scout has the opportunity to participate in both shared and individual leadership roles. Understanding the leadership positions available helps Scouts learn to be effective in the roles of others and guides them toward better citizenship.

### Mental and Physical

Childhood obesity has tripled since the 1980s, and the percent testing positive for drugs among patients for mental health issues has been steadily rising compared to previous generations. Schools are dropping an classes and recess for academics, yet young people in America are falling behind their peers in other developed countries in physical fitness and nutrition.

Against that backdrop of bad news, Scouting promotes mental and physical fitness through a rigorous program of outdoor activities and a varied array of merit badges and other awards that encourage youth to stretch both their muscles and their minds. As Baden-Powell said,

> "In a sound body is a short but full description of a happy state in this world."

And:

> "...you should...make it so the individual boy understands that he is responsible for his own health and strength. It is a duty to himself, to the nation, and to his Maker."
> —Robert Baden-Powell

---

# The Methods of Scouts BSA

If the four aims are our destination, the eight methods are our modes of transportation. In fact, you can think of the methods as the different gears on a car. When all eight pistons are firing, you're moving efficiently toward your destination. When a few pistons get fouled, the car lurches happily along. When only one or two pistons are firing, you feel the difference.

Here is a quick introduction to the methods and what they mean. They are presented here in numerical order, but they could be listed in any order because they are all equally important. None of the methods is more important than the aims of Scouting.

*Most of the methods will be covered in detail later in this book.*

## Adult Association

During adolescence, youth looking for guidance naturally turn to the adults in their lives to grow less intelligent by the second. Scouts BSA young men model in the form of Scoutmasters and assistant Scoutmasters, merit badge counselors, other adult members, and other caring adults who have earned the respect and affection of the youth. These adults become their colleagues, assisting them in running the program.

**Advancement.** Scouts BSA's ranks, merit badges, and special awards recognize Scouts for learning skills and taking on increased responsibility. As Scouts advance, they learn more skills and take on greater responsibilities. Scouts teach the biggest unit, Scouts demonstrated their competency level by earning merit badges in a broad array of subjects. Others who short of Eagle Scout rank their skills in such areas as first aid and cooking, and these skills are ones that they may pursue for a lifetime.

The real heart of Scouting is laid out in the Scout Oath, the Scout motto, and the Scout slogan (see tool box).

## Leadership Development

The ideal troop is led by Scouts who are picked by adults. Through formal training, informal coaching, and practical experience, young leaders train others and manage projects. Every Scout has the opportunity to lead in some way, whether as part of a team, as a leader of a small group, or as the leader of the troop. Young people are selected to positions of rank advancement requirements. Adults understand that their role is critical in both providing positive feedback and encouraging youth leaders who demonstrate leadership training.

**Outdoors.** Youth don't see Scouting to have their character shaped by badge work and skills. To have fun. The program is built around outdoor activities, to a large extent. All troop meetings should be devoted to learning outdoor skills and getting ready for the next outing (or occasionally washing dirty clothes from the last outing).

---

# The Ideals of Scouting

## Scout Oath

On my honor I will do my best
To do my duty
To God and my country
and to the Scout Law;
To help other people
at all times;
To keep myself
physically strong,
mentally awake,
and morally straight.

## Scout Law

A Scout is trustworthy, loyal, helpful, courteous, kind, obedient, cheerful, thrifty, brave, clean, and reverent.

## Scout Motto

Be Prepared.

## Scout Slogan

Do a Good Turn Daily.

---

## Patrol Method

Each troop is made up of one or more patrols: groups of about eight Scouts who camp, cook, play, and learn together. In patrols, Scouts learn citizenship and practice leadership at the most basic level, and strong patrols are the foundation of a strong troop.

## Personal Growth

While personal growth sounds like an aim of Scouts BSA, it is actually a method—assuring that the troop's leaders ensure opportunities for growth. Those opportunities include individual Good Turns and patrol and troop service projects, with the aim of expanding their horizons through a new Scouts who are receiving recognition for growing in their faith.

## Uniform

The Scout uniform does several things. It demonstrates unity within the troop and the larger Scouting movement. It minimizes the racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic differences among Scouts. And it is a sort of wearable trophy case where individual Scouts can display the badges that symbolizes what they have achieved in Scouting.

---

# The Scouts BSA Methods and Cub Scouting

If you are a former Cub Scout leader, you may look at the methods and think they are a lot like the methods you used in Cub Scouting. While there are plenty of similarities, there are some important differences, too.

**Adult Association.** In Cub Scouting, adults serve as den leaders. In Scouts BSA, adults are more likely to be Scoutmasters or assistant Scoutmasters at Scoutmaster conferences, patrol leaders' council meetings, and committee meetings along the trail.

**Advancement.** Unlike Cub Scouts, youth in Scouts BSA with an advancement at their own pace, especially after the first few ranks. Cub Scouts who advance quickly in their first year in the troop. How fast (or even whether) Scouts advance is up to them.

**Outdoors.** Especially as they enter their teens, youth in Scouts BSA live in a world that is far less clear-cut than Cub Scouts. As a result, outdoor activity is more intensive. By the end of review, you can help your Scouts learn to apply clear moral philosophy to situations that they can seem ambiguous.

**Leadership Development.** In Cub Scouting, denners and den chiefs are appointed by the den leader, carrying out the den leader's plans. In Scouts BSA, youth leaders make and carry out their own plans as they lead the patrol and troop.

**Outdoors.** Youth in Scouts BSA camp and hike much more frequently than Cub Scouts. Some troops camp at least one weekend plus a week in the summer. Pack-style family campouts are rare, however, and parents who do attend troop and patrol outings should be there as "chaperones," their personal behavior seen in both tent and dining hall.

**Patrol Method.** Unlike Cub Scouts, Scouts demit dens and run their own leaders, and those leaders hiking with troop-level leaders like the senior patrol leader run the troop. As a result, there are more campouts and outings, although they are encouraged to do so.

**Personal Growth.** As with advancement, personal growth happens more on an individual basis in Scouts BSA than in Cub Scouting. At one extreme are new Scouts who are still in their first year, and at the other end of the spectrum are Eagle Scout candidates who run service projects that guarantee their abilities to plan.

**Uniforming.** Unlike most Cub Scouts, some Scouts BSA troops are not embarrassed to wear the uniform in public. The savviest leaders promotes uniforming by example and encouragement, not by edict.

---

# Maintaining Your Balance

In terms of the aims and methods, Scouts tend to make a couple of mistakes. The first mistake is forgetting that the methods are the means to reach the aims. Your goal is not to become Eagle Scouts or lifelong campers, but it is much more important that they become good citizens, good friends, and good neighbors.

The second mistake is focusing on some of the methods to the exclusion of the others. Yes, outdoor meetings might be more fun than indoor winter campfire skills. Yes, your outdoor program might be stronger if you spend less time on rank advancement. And yes, merit badges, but that eliminates personal growth and advancement.

> **Thought you were using the methods to reach the aims, think about your last meeting or campout. Did the specific ways each method contribute to achieving the aims of Scouting apply?**

---

# Balancing the Methods: A Parable

Troop 14's Scoutmaster Scrum Smith is a strong leader and sets a great example for the youth. That is good. Adult association is one of the eight methods—but it is also possible to use too much of a good thing.

As a strong leader, Scoutmaster Smith has a hard time stepping back and letting boys learn from mistakes in the first place. Yet he doesn't develop the skills leadership development would have fostered, and as a result the troop's future is in jeopardy.

Meanwhile, Troop 7 Scoutmaster Jones is an excellent planner as well as a good chef, and creates a good experience by improving structure and improving experience for the weekend.

---

# Scouting As a Safe Place

Throughout this guidebook, we lists Scouts, policies and guidelines designed to keep the Scouts physically safe. But safety goes wider than just physical safety in a secure environment where youth can grow with confidence, face challenges without fear of ridicule, and move ahead in a clear, non-threatening setting with their mistakes, and therefore can adversity.

Scouts spend more time praising and less time criticizing.

# CHAPTER 2
# Troop Structure

Scouts BSA happens in the context of a troop with the nine elements that define the Scout arrangement. Today, some 22,000 troops serve more than 400,000 youth throughout the Scouts BSA program. Whether large or small, urban or rural, brand new or well-established, most troops share some familiar characteristics. Each also has its own distinct personality.

In this chapter, we will look at the typical troop structures and discuss membership requirements for youth and adults. First, we will address the troop committee's and Scoutmaster's role and the troop's impact on its ability to deliver the Scouting program.

## Form and Function

As an architect who has a house at a studio apartment can attest, you do not always need the same form to achieve a function. A bedroom, a bathroom, a kitchenette, and a sitting area will suffice. Move to a three-bedroom house, however, and you have room for more people and more activities. The form has changed, but not necessarily yourself with more house than you can afford or maintain.

People often say that form follows function, but the opposite also can be true. Form can define function, preventing you from doing things you might otherwise do.

Architect Frank Lloyd Wright argued that "form and function should be one, joined in a spiritual union." As you think about the structure of your troop, it is important to think about how that structure supports or restricts its function and whether some rearrangement might serve you better.

## Troop Basics

Every troop has four basic elements: the chartered organization, the Scouts, the leaders, and the troop committee. The Scoutmaster and the troop committee work closely with Scouts. Here is an introduction.

### The Chartered Organization

The Boy Scouts of America grants annual charters to local religious institutions, schools, civic organizations, and similar groups that wish to provide the Scouting program to youth in their communities. Some chartered organizations use Scouting as their official youth program. Others use Scouting as an adjunct to their youth program. Still others use Scouting as an outreach tool or as a way to fulfill their commitment to youth development. A school's parent-teacher council may serve as the chartering organization.

Each chartered organization agrees to:

- Provide a safe place for Scouts to meet and establish guidelines as well as those of the BSA.
- Include Scouting as part of its overall program for youth and families.
- Appoint a chartered organization representative—a member of the organization who coordinates the organization's Scouting units, represents the organization to the Scouting district, and generally keeps the organization informed about the Scouts and about districts and councils (in chapter 4).
- Select a troop committee of parents and members of the chartered organization who meet the organization's standards as well as the leadership standards of the BSA. The committee includes a chairperson and at least two members.
- Provide adequate facilities for the troop to meet on a regular basis for troop meetings and activities.
- Encourage the troop to participate in outdoor experiences.

> Note that financial support is not required. That said, many chartered organizations help cover expenses of running the troop, and membership in the organization members are usually happy to support troop money-earning projects.

### The Chartered Organization Representative

The chartered organization representative heads the organization's Scouting department and serves as a liaison between the organization and its Scouting units. The chartered organization representative is a vital link that keeps the organization's leadership aware of the Scouting program. A good chartered organization representative will represent the interests of both the troop and the organization.

Scouting unit is an umbrella term that refers to Cub Scout packs, Scouts BSA troops, Venturing crews, and Sea Scout ships.

The chartered organization representative may serve as a member of the troop committee. At a minimum, the chartered organization representative should always be invited to troop committee meetings and should be kept aware of what is going on in the troop. The chartered organization representative is also a voting member of both the district committees and the local council in their annual business meetings.

## The Troop Committee

The troop committee is a cross between a board of directors and a parent support group. It sets troop policies and handles administrative functions, allowing the Scoutmaster and patrol leaders to focus on Scouts.

The troop committee can be as large as needed, but at a minimum it consists of one chairperson and two committee members. The members can be parents or other relatives, former Scouts, or leaders from the chartered organization who are interested in serving young people.

Some committee members do not have assigned functions, but most take on specific duties. A typical committee may include but is not limited to these positions:

**Chairperson**—presides and leads the committee

**Advancement coordinator**—oversees advancement records and budgets

**Chaplain**—provides spiritual support to Scouts and leaders

**Chartered organization representative**—acts as a liaison between the troop and the chartered organization

**Equipment coordinator**—manages the troop's camping equipment

**Membership coordinator**—coordinates recruiting and retention efforts

**Outdoor/activities coordinator**—supports and promotes the outdoor program

**Parent communication coordinator**—works on communication with troop families

**Training coordinator**—promotes leader training

**Treasurer**—handles troop funds and financial records

**Parent coordinator**—recruits and orients new Scout parents and provides them with information about the troop

**Insignia coordinator**—encourages Scouts to wear their emblems and insignia

**Other positions**—may depend on the troop's needs. If your troop has a strong high-adventure program, you might have both an outdoor/activities coordinator and a high-adventure specialist. If your troop is responsible for maintaining its meeting place, you might have a facilities coordinator.

While the committee and the Scoutmaster corps have different responsibilities, they have the same goal: to provide a quality Scouting program. An atmosphere of collegiality is essential. The Scoutmaster should keep the committee chair informed, and the committee should offer assistance, support, and encouragement. This relationship between the Scoutmaster and the committee chair should be one of genuine mutual trust.

For more information, see the Troop Committee Guidebook, No. 34502.

Having in-depth, one-on-one discussions at troop or troop committee meetings can be difficult. If you are the Scoutmaster, you might consider meeting your committee chairperson for coffee or lunch every couple of weeks to touch base.

## The Scoutmaster Corps

The Scoutmaster and assistant Scoutmasters work directly with the Scouts and carry out most of the book will discuss what they do. At this juncture, two points are important.

First, as with troop committee members, some assistant Scoutmasters will serve in long-term roles that have assigned responsibilities. Others will help out on an as-needed basis. Some may take charge in the Scoutmaster's absence—the more smoothly the troop will function. If you have assistant Scoutmasters, orient them to your patrol system and discuss what adult mentoring and supporting junior leaders. The more effective the senior patrol leader, for example, the much less the Scoutmaster needs in the background.

While having assistant Scoutmasters is not required to obtain a charter, only the smallest troops can get by with only a Scoutmaster. Having one or more assistant Scoutmasters is strongly recommended. Adult leaders should also comply with the BSA's policy of two-deep leadership at meetings and on all troop outings.

## The Scouts

A troop must have at least five registered members, although most troops have considerably more. (The average troop has about 20 members, but some have 50 or even 100 members.) Troops are organized into patrols of 6 to 8 Scouts. In larger troops, as we discuss in chapter 3, each patrol can have a senior patrol leader who reports directly to the Scoutmaster. The youth must secure a copy of the Scout BSA Handbook.

Scouts with qualifying permanent addresses may be eligible to register beyond the normal registration age. The chartered organization and the local council are the authorities whether to request a waiver, which must be approved by the local council.

## Youth Membership Requirements

Youth can join Scouts BSA if they are at least 11 years old, are in the fifth grade, and register on or after March 1, or have earned the Arrow of Light Award and are at least 10 years old and are age 18 or under. The youth must secure a copy of the Scout BSA Handbook.

## Scout Rank Requirements

Requirements for the Scout rank, which became effective Jan. 1, 2016, must be completed as a member of a troop registered with the BSA. The requirements as part of the Arrow of Light Adventure Trail must simply demonstrate the required knowledge or skills to the Scoutmaster or other designated leader.

For the full Scout rank requirements, see the Scout BSA Requirements book (current year) or go to scoutbsa.org/programs/scouts-bsa/advancement-and-awards/.

---

## Adult Leadership Requirements

To serve as a registered Scouts BSA, an adult must:

- Be active in the Scout Law and Scout Code of Conduct. The Scout Code of Conduct can be found at scoutbsa.org/scouting-/bsa/rank-and-advancement/.

- Subscribe to the precepts of the Declaration of Religious Principle

- Reside within the USA or a U.S. territory, or be a U.S. citizen residing outside the USA.

- Be 21 or older for primary leadership positions.

- Be 18 or older for assistant leadership positions.

- Complete Youth Protection training (YPT) before application is processed and renew it every two years thereafter.

- Review the disclosure information related to the BSA's background check process and complete and sign a Background Check Authorization form.

- Take leader-position-specific training at my council's Scout University and provide verification of training through my local council.

All leadership positions are open to men and women and to both citizens and noncitizens. However, a chartered organization may impose additional leadership requirements. See its charter organization.

In the first part of the religious element in the training of the member, but it is absolutely nonsectarian in its attitude toward that religious training. Any member may give his own interpretation of the precepts of religion within the group with which the member is connected shall give definite attention to religious life. Only persons willing to subscribe to these precepts from the Declaration of Religious Principle shall be leaders in the Boy Scouts of America."
