# CHAPTER 3
## Patrol Method

In chapter 2, we said that Scouts BSA happens in the context of a troop. It might be more accurate to say that Scouts BSA happens in the context of a patrol within a troop. You see, the patrol—that subgroup of eight or so Scouts—is more than an organizational convenience. The patrol method is the fundamental organizational plan of the place where young people learn skills, take on leadership responsibilities, try new skills for the first time, and develop friendships that will last a lifetime.

## The Patrol Defined

A patrol is a semi-permanent group of compatible Scouts who elect their own leader. Each patrol has its own name, uniform, emblem, and yell. Members of a patrol help one another during troop meetings and patrol outings, while patrol members often sitting happily!) as troop meetings, compete together as a team in troop competitions, and go camping together. Patrols sometimes work on advancement together.

Once a troop reaches a designated program point, leadership within the opening and closing ceremonies, while a designated service patrol sets up chairs and cleans up afterward. When the senior patrol leader, troop committee, or Scoutmaster sees a sign is held up, and patrol leaders corral the members of their respective patrols. When a message needs to get to the troop between meetings, the senior patrol leader contacts the patrol leader, who, in turn, calls on the message.

## Types of Patrols

Historically, patrols were made up of Scouts of all ages and abilities, with each patrol having its own leadership.

- Each patrol always has members who excel in basic Scouting skills and members who are able to teach those skills.
- Each patrol always has experienced leaders and members who need leadership experience.
- Patrols thus are fairly evenly matched for games and skill competitions.
- All members don't "age out" at the same time.

The theory of this mixed-age approach is that Scouts who are new to the troop can be well-rounded Scouts with life in common. Moreover, Scouts making the transition from an Arrow of Light patrol might benefit from a more gradual change.

A better approach is to have three types of patrols: new-Scout patrols, regular patrols, and older-Scout patrols.

## New-Scout Patrols

Scouts who have just joined the troop, usually at the start of the school year, Scouts often have just joined the troop. The patrol elects its own leader, just like other patrols, but usually for a shorter term—perhaps one month instead of six months. The troop committee should call a "troop guide" to walk in the Scouts BSA handbook and to help with First Class rank advancement. Backing up the troop guide is an assistant Scoutmaster whose main responsibility is to work with new Scouts.

Scouts typically remain in a new-Scout patrol for their first year in the troop, but when they reach First Class rank, they can choose to move on or stay together as a regular patrol. At that point, members can either choose which regular patrol they want to join or stay together as a regular patrol.

## Regular Patrols

Members of a regular or traditional patrol have similar interests and perhaps spend time together outside the troop, and they may well be friends outside of Scouting. Ideally, they have chosen to be in the same patrol.

Traditional patrols typically serve Scouts who have reached First Class rank or completed the seventh grade. However, a new-Scout patrol that stays together can, as its members advance and move toward Boy Scout ranks, also stay together and become a regular patrol.

Many troops assign an interested Scoutmaster to a patrol, who advises and supports the patrol leader. Each patrol may or may not have a youth leader assigned to the patrol's main responsibility.

## Older-Scout Patrols

Older-Scout patrols serve older Scouts who want to pursue more challenging high-adventure outings and sports activities while maintaining their connection to the troop. They have a more relaxed attitude, so their patrol activities differ from others while keeping them involved in troop leadership.

You might think of the older-Scout patrol as a safety valve; it lets older Scouts blow off steam and then willingly re-engage with the troop.

An assistant Scoutmaster can be assigned to the older-Scout patrol to help the members turn their outing ideas into action. This adult member works to ensure that patrol activities don't distract their regular troops, build friendships, and may also glorify the members' youth rank.

---

## Scout Patrols vs. Cub Scout Dens

### A PATROL...
- Is led by a patrol leader who is supported by an assistant Scoutmaster
- Can include Scouts of all ages
- Has eight or so members
- Is identified by name (e.g., the Pedroni Patrol)
- Meets as a group as often as or less often than once a month
- Typically focuses on activity planning

### A DEN...
- Is led by an adult den leader who is supported by a denner (Cub Scout) and a den chief (Scout from a troop)
- Typically includes Cub Scouts in the same grade
- Has six to eight members
- Is identified by name (e.g., the Webelos dens or Arrow of Light patrols)
- Meets as a group more often than the pack
- Typically focuses on advancement work

---

## Patrol Size

The ideal patrol size is eight members. Eight Scouts can hike and camp together efficiently while minimizing their impact on the environment. They can share work without anyone feeling left out. If someone gets hurt or sick or someone brings a friend, the group can still function.

Size directly affects how new Scouts can shift patrol size based on that troop's makeup. A new-Scout patrol might need to be smaller since new Scouts need more personal attention and supervision. An older-Scout patrol might be bigger so that enough members are available for activities and experience can with duties and activities that affect Scouting participation.

Determining patrol size and structure is a good task for the patrol leaders' council. They are most affected by such decisions and understand it would be well as they can explain if regularly scheduled positions.

---

## Building Patrol Spirit

What captures are members, the members have the same devotion to their patrols that sports fans have to their favorite teams. And when patrols are strong, the troop is strong.

Patrol spirit starts with the patrol name, which the members choose and invest in. Some patrols name themselves after historical figures (like Daniel Boone and Davy Crockett) or forest creatures (like Eagle or Fox). Once members have chosen a name, they take their patrol name seriously and wear their patrol emblems proudly on their uniforms.

Just as the patrol has a patrol name, every member has selected its own equipment. It should emblazons it name and emblem there as well.

Names, tags, and emblems are just the beginning. To deepen patrol identity among its members in many other ways:

- Pitting patrols against each other in games and competitions rather than creating all-boy teams each time)
- Recognizing patrols that do the best in uniform or complete inspections
- Rewarding the patrol with the best overall attendance
- Giving them a special patrol award (like a High Honor Patrol Award)

It's easy to praise patrol spirit, but it is even easier to unintentionally destroy it. How? By frequently reorganizing patrols, by creating temporary patrols on outings where troop members are assigned randomly to units for a time. As Robert Baden-Powell said, "Patrols should be kept intact under all circumstances."

---

# Patrol Meetings

Patrols work best when they meet regularly to get their work done. Most troops set aside part of the weekly meetings for patrol activities. Patrols can be as creative as they want. One troop schedules patrol meetings after the troop meeting or at a different time and place, perhaps on a Sunday afternoon, with their patrol leader's family.

Whenever and wherever the patrol meets, each meeting should be well-planned and businesslike. Typically, the patrol leader calls the meeting to order, reviews upcoming activities and outings, the patrol leader reports on advancement, and the patrol leader reports what happened at the last patrol leaders' council meeting. Some meetings might focus on preparing for upcoming outings. Other work could include designing a new patrol flag, or teaching the patrol box with cooking gear, or practicing for the patrol's presentation at the next troop campfire. Allow time for advancement requirements. It is also a good idea to save some time for fun; cooking an activities tournament can keep the meeting lively.

On Saturday, the traditional patrols take a 10-mile hike toward completing a Hiking merit badge requirement. The instructor and patrol leader make periodic stops to teach short lessons on first aid, outdoor skills or songs.

Besides doing their own thing during troop activities, all three types of patrols can conduct activities separate from the troop if they follow these guidelines:

- The Scoutmaster and the participants' parents give permission.
- The activity does not conflict with troop calendars.
- The policies in the Guide to Safe Scouting are followed.
- The activity is supervised by at least two registered adult leaders 21 years of age or over.

# Patrol Activities

Most patrol activities take place in the context of troop activities, but that does not mean that every patrol must do its own thing. Several Scouts from the patrol might go on a nearby state park hike. The troop travels together to the park on Friday afternoon. On arrival, the Scouts meet with the two adult leaders leave for an over-night backpacking trip, while the other patrols set up camp in adjacent campground.

---

**Patrol Leader.** The patrol leader's responsibilities are discussed in detail in the *Patrol Leader Handbook*. In short, a patrol leader should:

- Take a leading role in planning and conducting patrol meetings and activities.
- Keep in touch with each member of the patrol leaders' council and communicate the PLC's decisions back to the patrol.
- Keep patrol members informed about upcoming events and deadlines.
- Encourage patrol members to complete their advancement requirements.
- Set a good example by living up to the Scout Oath and Scout Law.

**Assistant Patrol Leader.** The assistant patrol leader runs the patrol in the patrol leader's absence. An assistant patrol leader may carry out special assignments given by the patrol leader, such as leading the patrol on a hike or instruction.

**Patrol Scribe.** The scribe is the patrol's secretary, checking attendance, maintaining the Patrol Record Book, collecting and recording patrol dues (if any), and helping to prepare budgets for outings.

**Patrol Quartermaster.** The patrol quartermaster takes care of the patrol's equipment the patrol owns, checking out troop gear from the troop quartermaster and ensuring that it is returned in good condition.

**Patrol Grubmaster.** The grubmaster oversees menu planning, shopping, and cooking for the patrol on outings. Note that not everyone needs to plan menus or cook to complete merit badge requirements.

**Patrol Cheermaster.** The cheermaster leads the patrol in songs, yells, and stunts during meetings and campfire programs.

> In chapter 13 we will discuss more about working with youth leaders. Suffice it to say that most youth leaders, especially at the patrol level, need plenty of ongoing support to succeed. Remember that most are leading their peers - which is not easy - and no one is leading them for the first time.

---

# CHAPTER 4
## The Troop in Context

To paraphrase the poet John Donne, the troop is an island entire of itself. Although you may sometimes feel isolated in the church basement on Tuesday night or at the local Scout camp on a Saturday afternoon, a host of groups and individuals stands ready to support your efforts. This chapter explores those organizations.

## The Chartered Organization

As we said in chapter 2, each troop is owned and operated by a community-based chartered organization—typically a religious institution, school, or civic group. The importance of understanding your relationship with these organizations more in chapter 22, but here is an overview of what your chartered organization can do for you.

While your chartered organization agrees to provide the troop a meeting place, chartered organization representatives, and some financial support from the chartered organization, it is a natural extended family for your troop. You may even be able to get chartered organization members or leaders to help with Scouting activities or to demonstrate needs like summertime scholarships.

The key is to demonstrate how your mission aligns with the organization's mission. Emphasize that your troop is a program of the organization, not an outside group.

### The Local Council

To administer Scouting effectively, the BSA works through some 350 local councils across the country. These councils vary in size, geographic scope, and resources. Most councils exist to provide the following:

- Training and support for troop leaders and chartered organization representatives
- General liability insurance to cover chartered organizations
- Camping facilities
- A service center
- A professional staff to assist you

> **Local councils extend beyond America's borders.**
> The Far East Council, headquartered in Japan, serves U.S. Scouts in several Asian nations; the Transatlantic Council supports U.S. Scouts in most of Europe; and the Orient Service Council, headquartered in Texas, supports U.S. Scouts in the Pacific.

> **You are not limited to using your own council's camping facilities. Check with nearby councils to find out what they offer.**

## The District

Most local councils are divided into geographic districts, each usually serving a city or a section of a large city or county. The district is the council's arm closest to the unit level, and the district is responsible for these four functions:

**Membership.** The district seeks to grow Scouting by organizing new Scouting units, recruiting new members and adult volunteers to join existing units, and sharing current members' experiences.

**Program.** The district supports units by promoting camping and community service and by challenging and supporting Scouts to advance.

**Finance.** The district works to provide its share of funds to support council operations.

**Unit Services.** The district provides direct coaching and consultation to help units succeed.

Carrying out these functions are two groups of volunteers: the district committee and the commissioner staff, and leaders of troops, packs, and ships.

### District Committee

Led by its chair and including committee members, the district committee oversees the membership, program, and finance functions. It typically works like the following diagram:

**District Membership Committee.** The membership committee works throughout the year to organize new units, support the year to organize new units, support the year to organize new units, support existing units, and increase membership. It encourages activities that culminates in the spring, and maintains healthy relationships with chartered organizations. It organizes leadership recruitment drives that can give your troop an infusion of new members.

**District Program Committee.** The committee promotes camping and other outdoor activities. It works closely with the Order of the Arrow (Scouting's national honor society) to encourage Scout attendance at the council's summer camp.

> **Committee members are often experienced Scouts who are well versed in camping techniques and know the best camping spots in your area. If you want to know about the best places to camp, want to learn about skills like backpacking, or have questions about BSA policies or procedures involving camping, these are the people to ask.**

**District Activities and Civic Service Committee.** The activities and service committee plans districtwide events like camporees, Scouting shows, recognition dinners, and other activities designed to enhance the success of troops. The committee also plans special events like Scout-O-Rama and Founder's Anniversary Week, the week that contains February 8, Scouting's birthday.

As the name implies, the committee is also responsible for service projects. Often they are looking for creative ways to help the community.

**District Training Committee.** The training committee is responsible for ensuring that adult and youth leaders receive the training needed for their positions. The committee offers basic and supplemental training throughout the year. It also promotes more advanced training events, such as the Wood Badge course or university of Scouting) and at the same level sponsors at the Philmont Training Center.

Start here if you are not sure what training you and other troop leaders need. The committee can help you determine next step in your professional development and can help you resolve scheduling conflicts. Trainers may even be able to bring training to your troop.

**District Advancement and Recognition Committee.** The advancement and recognition committee promotes recognition and encourages district boards of review for those Scouts working toward Eagle Scout badge counselors and maintains a list of those counselors to assist Scouts. It recognizes those who will conduct Eagle Scout advancement procedures. If you need a merit badge counselor for a particular merit badge, if you need advice about advancement policies and procedures, if you need details about religious emblems program, or if a Scout needs to visit a board of review, contact this committee.

While you probably won't have much interaction with the finance committee, it is a good resource for information on troop finances. It may also be able to help you find funding for special causes like scholarships for needy Scouts.

---

# Commissioner Staff

The district's fourth function, unit success, is the responsibility of the commissioner staff. Most commissioners are experienced Scouters who have been there, done that, and gotten more comfortable with public speaking. Still, commissioners are the face and are dedicated to helping you meet those challenges.

The commissioner staff is headed by the district commissioner, who may be supported by one or more assistant district commissioners who oversee specific geographic areas. You will see these volunteers frequently, as they are partners with your unit commissioner and the roundtable commissioner.

## Unit Commissioner

A unit commissioner is responsible for supporting three or so units, either at the unit or at a chartered organization or several units in the same area. The position combines the best practices necessary to help assigned units succeed.

Depending on your troop's situation, the unit commissioner can play several roles.

**A representative.** Your unit commissioner can bring you up to date about upcoming activities, new programs, or changes to BSA policies.

**A teacher.** Your unit commissioner can provide on-the-job training to equipment leaders who have learned in other settings.

**A coach.** If your troop is facing challenges, your unit commissioner can help you and others think through solutions.

A *paramedic:* If your troop faces a serious crisis, your unit commissioner can bring in bear whatever council and district resources can help get things moving.

Most importantly, you should get to know your unit commissioner as a friend of the unit. Make sure your commissioner is on the troop mailing list and receives invitations to troop courts of honor and other key events.

Besides offering general support, your unit commissioner can help your troop with two important tasks.

## Charter renewal.

Each year, your troop must renew its charter with the BSA, a process that involves conducting a membership review. Your unit commissioner can guide you through this process, help collect subscription fees, and submitting paperwork to the local council, including paperwork for the Journey to Excellence performance recognition program. This process is led by the committee chair.

## Charter planning.

Charter renewal is a good time to emphasize and celebrate the chartered organization's good citizenship with Scouting. This can be done by having uniformed Scouts present the new charter at the head of the chartered organization at a full meeting of the organization.

## Roundtable Commissioner.

The roundtable commissioner hosts monthly roundtable meetings that bring together leaders from troops, teams, crews, and ships to network, learn, and find solutions to problems.

# Getting the Most Out of District and Council Relationships

Your district and council exist to support you. Here are a few ways to maximize that support:

**Be present.** Roundtables are the most efficient way to communicate with all district volunteers, so make sure your troop attends.

**Be on time.** Meeting our hard deadlines for tasks like charter and merit badge processing follow-up ensure that everyone can serve you well.

**Be up to date.** Promptly turn in new Scout and adult applications and other paperwork to the advancement if meeting times or leadership roles change.

**Be involved.** Find ways to help out, such as by offering a color guard, hosting a district event, or serving on a committee at the district composite. You will find the help reciprocated.

**Be honest.** Every troop faces occasional challenges. Let your unit commissioner know when you need help.

> **To make roundtable meetings more valuable, try to get as many leaders from your troop to attend as possible. By traveling as a group or sharing a meal before the roundtable, you can spend time together—something fun leaders need—and you can discuss how things are going in the troop.**

# District Executive

All members of the district committee and professional staff should see Scouting as a local tradition they are likely to pass on to the next generation. The council Scout executive is a middle manager like a field director and works with the district committee and the Scoutmaster to facilitate youth progress. The executive has a vested interest in your troop and is well-versed in the resources available in your district. If you are not sure whom to call, contact your district executive.

# Other Units

Other Scouts units can play important roles in your troop's program and success. You'll read more in chapter 14.

For now, here is a quick introduction.

**Cub Scout Packs.** Most youth Scouting careers start as Cub Scouts. By building strong relationships with Cub Scout packs in your community, you make your troop the natural next step for Scouts bridging from Cub Scouting. Visit packs in your community and offer to provide an audience when your Scouts need to teach skills or an eager "victim" during first-aid simulations.

**Scout Troops.** If you share a community with another troop, you might enjoy doing things together. Also, you can learn a lot from other troops and probably teach them a thing or two. If your troop is small, you can pair up with another troop to ensure that you have enough adult leaders for summer camp or other activities.

**Venturing Crews and Sea Scout Ships.** Scouting's young-adult programs keep older Scouts interested in Scouting, making it less likely that they will leave the program altogether to participate in other activities. The Venturing achievement program in particular can help prepare older Scouts for the transition from Scouts BSA to other youth people.

# CHAPTER 5
## Uniforms and Insignia

Few elements of the Scouting program are as recognizable as the Scout uniform. But most people—including many Scout leaders—do not realize that the uniform is more than just clothing in support of the right methods of Scouting. In this chapter, you will find detailed information concerning a discussion of what uniforming accomplishes.

## The Uniform as a Method

When worn properly, the uniform does a couple of things that, frankly, no other method of Scouting can accomplish. It establishes uniformity across troop members, putting everyone on an equal footing regardless of present circumstances, race and ethnicity, or a family's socioeconomic status. The uniform also serves as a sort of wearable trophy case that shows what the wearer has accomplished. It is a strong tool you can use within your meetings and at special events to recognize what Scouts currently hold, and what special honors they have received. This uniform also supports the other methods of Scout BSA.

**Adult Association.** Adult leaders also wear the uniform, setting an example for Scouts showing they are part of the troop.

**Advancement and Personal Growth.** As described above, the uniform displays patches and emblems the wearer has earned.

**Ideals.** The Scout emblem, parts of which appear on each rank patch, serves as a reminder of the ideals; the American flag on the right sleeve emphasizes duty to country.

**Leadership Development and the Patrol Method.** The badge of office represents the wearer's position of responsibility; the patrol emblem has each of the patrols in the patrol.

**Outdoor Skills.** The uniform, with its practical pockets, zip-off pants legs, and other features, is designed for action. (Note, however, that most troops switch to an activity uniform, as described below, for hiking and camping.)

## Uniform Basics

For Scouts and Scouters alike, the full uniform (also called the field uniform) consists of:

- Scout long-sleeved or short-sleeved shirt
- Scout pants or shorts
- Scout socks or knee socks
- BSA cap or campaign hat (troop optional)
- Scout belt and buckle (although pants are self-belted)
- Scout neckerchief, slide tie, or neckwear (troop optional)

The troop can choose its headgear and neckerchief or slide tie, or can also choose to use no neckerchief. These are good decisions for the patrol leaders' council to revisit occasionally. Beyond those options, troops are not allowed to modify the uniform by, say, adding a colored stripe down the pants leg.

Troops typically wear the field uniform during troop meetings, courts of honor, evening meals at summer camp, and perhaps while traveling. For physically active outdoor events or informal activities, most troops switch to an activity uniform by removing the neckerchief and changing to shorts or an activity shirt.

Wherever the uniform is worn, it should be worn correctly and neatly. Each troop determines the proper wearing of the uniform; see the short for its members.

**Many troops have adopted the "Class A" patch policy as a method to assist in their uniforming by allowing Scouts to wear dress shirts make them easier to spot during campfires, museum visits, and other activities where the Class B uniform.**

New uniforms can be purchased from official Scouting distributors located in selected retail stores, from local council service centers and Scout shops, and from the BSA Supply Group website. Many troops establish a uniform bank of "experienced" uniforms that they loan out to Scouts in need. A request to troop families, troop alumni, and members of your local community may yield uniforms and patches. Consider taking donations to start a uniform bank.

Here are a few more things you should know about the Scout uniform:

- Only registered members are allowed to wear the Scout uniform.
- Older versions of the uniform are still considered official and appropriate for wear.
- A Scout is not required to have a uniform to participate in Scouting or to pass an advancement board of review.
- No alterations of, or additions to, the official uniform may be made by any Scout, leader, or Scouting official without written approval from the Boy Scouts of America.
- In accordance with the provisions of the BSA's congressional charter, any imitation of United States Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps uniforms is prohibited.

## Encouraging Uniforming

The best way to encourage proper uniforming is to make sure adult leaders in the troop set a good example. Wear your complete uniform to all troop meetings and inspections and recognize the best-uniformed patrol. Take care, however, to ensure that your Scouts view uniforms as tools of the program rather than as an exercise in fund-raising. The Uniform Inspection Sheet is a handy tool to use during uniform inspections.

## Insignia

Somebody could write a book about how to wear Scout patches and other insignia. In fact, somebody has. The Guide to Advancement and Insignia tells you everything you need to know about patch placement. A more concise guide is also available online. Here are a few points about Scouting insignia that are frequently misunderstood:

- Members wear only the insignia that show their present status. Previously earned badges and insignia may be displayed on a uniform sash or frame, or they may be retired.
- Members may wear only temporary patches on badges of merit on the back of their merit badge sash.
- Members should wear only one temporary patch at a time and should remove the patch when it is suspended from.
- Adults should not wear youth insignia. However, some youth leaders in the Eagle Scout rank may wear the Scout Quartermaster Award, and youth religious emblems are considered youth insignia. Do not wear them on the adult uniform.
- The Order of the Arrow sash and merit badge sash should not be worn at the same time, and neither should be worn folded over the belt.

> It is against BSA policy to sell or trade merit medals and patches; prohibits patches and other earned awards as training rewards.

## When Not to Wear the Uniform

Do not wear the uniform:

- While selling a commercial product or service, even for Scout purposes. Note, however, that Scout uniforms may be worn during Scout-sponsored fundraisers, like selling Scout show tickets.
- While engaged in any activity that could dishonor or discredit the BSA, the uniform, or the person wearing it.
- As a costume or disguise for any reason.
- In situations that might mistakenly imply an endorsement by the BSA of a political candidate, or philosophy.
- While participating in any form of public demonstration or protest process as private individuals but not while wearing the uniform. It is OK for Scouts to participate in a ceremony and not remain on stage where their presence might imply an endorsement.

**Some people incorrectly refer to the field and activity uniforms as Class A and Class B uniforms, respectively.**

The accompanying graphic shows the placement of basic insignia, as does the Uniform Inspection Sheet. The Adventure Scout BSA Handbook and the Guide to Awards and Insignia in the Scouts BSA Handbook for Girls also includes these graphics to help Scouts make informed decisions about shoulder patches. Keep in mind that neatness, not perfection, should be the goal when uniforming. It is better for Scouts to sew on their own patches and get them a little crooked than for a parent or scoutmaster to do the job.

# THE SCOUTS BSA PROGRAM

> Youth don't join Scouting to develop their character.
> They don't join Scouting to wear uniforms.
> In fact, they don't join Scouting to do much of anything we discussed in section 1.
> 
> Instead, they join Scouting to have fun, and fun is what happens on hikes, at summer camp, and in the backcountry.

# CHAPTER 6
## Program Overview

This chapter will discuss the basics of troop programming; the following chapters will discuss various program elements in detail.

### The Annual Program Cycle

If you are a scoutmaster or have become involved in youth sports, you know that seasons exist for a reason. When teams are formed, training camps where players practice and sharpen their skills, a handful of preseason games, the bulk of actual competition follows through and—if your team makes the playoffs—a postseason cycle to conclude the season. An annual program cycle in Scouting follows much the same pattern.

The annual program cycle in Scouting looks much different:

- Scouting is a year-round program, so the only "off-season" is perhaps a short break after summer camp or a couple of weeks around the winter holidays.
- Learning the game and playing the game are intertwined. During each month troop meeting, Scouts learn and practice skills they will use on that month's outing.
- Scouts don't play just one game. They might go backpacking this month, rock climbing the next, and white-water canoeing the month after that.
- Awards are given throughout the year. Scouts receive badges as soon as they earn them and then are formally recognized at courts of honor that take place three or four times a year (often at summer camp for Scouts, at winter camp for Cub Scouts and Eagle Scouts).
- Youth can go Scouting throughout the year, although most come from Arrow of Light patrols and join in the winter or spring.

> Troops that take the summer off lose valuable momentum.

Although the Scouting cycle looks different from the sports cycle, it still has a natural rhythm. The year typically looks at when certain activities naturally take place—outdoor activities—summer camp for all Scouts and a high-adventure trip for older Scouts. In between, the troop meets each week, camps each month, participates in service projects and fundraisers. Courts of honor provide deadlines for Scouts to complete advancement requirements and an opportunity to celebrate their achievements. The scoutmaster and committee chairs work with boards of review. An annual planning conference ensures continuity from one program year to the next.

### Typical Annual Program

Here is a typical troop's annual calendar. It includes weekly meetings (on Thursdays in this example), monthly outings (which might be day hikes early in the year and backpacking trips later), patrol leaders' council and troop committee meetings of the patrol leaders' council and troop committees, courts of honor, two service projects, and two fundraisers. Some troop calendars also include major holidays, school breaks, and community events.

You don't have to limit your troop to one outing per month. Some large or solidly established troops hold two outings per month. A troop that offers a wider variety of activities, offers more opportunities for leadership, and helps ensure that every Scout can go camping or hiking every month if desired.

As you review the calendar, note these features:

- The troop misses its regular Thursday meetings when holidays occur.
- The patrol leaders' council and the troop committee have designated meeting days (in the example, the first Monday and the second Sunday, respectively).
- Outings and other weekend events are spread out as evenly as possible.
- The calendar takes advantage of long weekends and school breaks (such as in March and May, for example).
- The calendar avoids potential conflicts like camping during Mother's Day weekend and, in this example, avoids a day outing into the weekend of Scout Sunday in February. (Note that some faith-based organizations may ask for a day outing instead on a Sunday of Scouting.)

Some troops set aside one regular meeting night each month for patrol meetings or patrol leaders' council meetings. Attendance tends to be better; the arrangement provides continuity for advancement work and is a great way to use a second night that week. Nights like that also give Scouts a chance to catch up on advancement work and to handle troop business under adult guidance.

When scheduling monthly meetings, earlier in the month typically works better. If you shoot for, say, the fourth Sunday of the month, you may run into conflict with the Memorial Day, Thanksgiving, and Christmas holidays.

In chapter 2 we discuss how to help your patrol leaders' council craft a calendar. On the next page is a typical troop calendar.

# A Program With a Purpose

Scouting can be called a game with a purpose, but you can actually apply that description to every element of the program. Everything the troop does should be directed toward reaching some sort of objective. The troop's monthly program calendar offers a variety of activities and outings. Scouts learn knots and lashings at troop meetings so they can use these skills on the next campout or service project. Hiking and camping skills are learned so Scouts can go on backpacking trips and service projects are required for advancement. They lead Scouts to pay for equipment or outings.

Scouts need to see that the things they do are leading somewhere. If nothing has changed and if they have learned the same skills they knew before, Scouts will quickly lose interest in the program.

## The Monthly Program Cycle

While the overall program cycle can be a month long, the actual program is typically a weekly troop meetings, monthly patrol leaders council and troop committee meetings, and a bimonthly troop activity. If your troop's activity committee meetings fit in for now, we will focus on the troop meeting and monthly outing.

To see how these pieces fit together, consider the month of May in the troop calendar shown on the previous page. The meeting for the first Monday would be a troop meeting. The meeting for the first Monday would be an easy trip on flat water that is suitable for new Scouts.

According to the BSA's Safety Afloat guidelines, several things need to happen before the trip can take place:

- The troop must secure qualified supervision (suitable for the Scoutmaster and troop committee).
- Participants must wear a personal flotation device.
- Participants must take the BSA swimmer classification test.
- Participants must demonstrate skill proficiency or following or have a minimum of three hours of training and supervised practice.

---

In addition, as with all trips, patrols need to plan menus and prepare duty rosters. That is a lot of work, but the troop has four weeks to get it all done. Each meeting might focus on different skills or what each meeting might feature.

- **May 9:** At a nearby city park's lakes, hold swim tests and teach how to enter and exit the canoe; collect health forms and fees.

- **May 16:** At a nearby park's lake, practice paddling techniques and what to do in an emergency; have patrols prepare duty rosters.

- **May 23:** On a nearby lake, each meeting focuses on the month's outing. That means Scouts will have fun at the meetings and be prepared to run the canoe trip.

Scouters say you can't take the sitting out of Scouting, but every do so unintentionally by always meeting inside. Whenever possible, move all or part of your troop meetings outside.

## Modifying the Monthly Cycle

Every month won't be straightforward as the one described here. Sometimes you will have more or fewer weeks between activities, or the meeting place might not be suitable for outdoor preparation. You will usually have other business you need to conduct at troop meetings. That is all OK. The key is to ensure that there is a logical connection between the meetings leading toward those outings. Just as business will fill with outdoor practices. When Scouts will put away their notebooks and get outside during the meeting when they have a great weekend outing ahead.

# CHAPTER 7
## Troop Program Planning

Without effective planning, meetings will be boring, outings will be unorganized, Scouts will be disappointed, and the troop will fail to achieve the aims of Scouting. To put it another way, prior proper planning prevents pitifully poor performance—not to mention lower advancement rates and unhappy Scouts.

Planning is important for another reason. Since program planning requires input from the patrol leaders' council, the process teaches Scouts a valuable skill they will use for their Eagle Scout service projects and throughout their lives.

### Two Types of Planning

To be an effective leader, use both *short-term planning* and *long-term planning*. Short-term planning results in a calendar like the one shown in chapter 8 and is used to plan events for the year. Short-term planning yields detailed schedules for campouts, meetings, and special events. Long-term planning takes a little more. Since most new youth leaders have not planned anything more comprehensively than a trip to the movies, breaking this down into the steps outlined in the pages that follow should help.

> Planning is one of the core skills taught in *Introduction to Leadership Skills for Troops*, which we discuss in chapter 13. Planning is a critical skill for you to master and to help youth leaders master in building a successful troop.

### Long-Term Planning

Long-term planning happens at the annual planning conference, which is typically held in late spring or early summer, as soon as possible after you have confirmed and secured calendars have been submitted to your local council office. By holding it early in the year and scheduling it well before the break and district compromises, you avoid having to change the troop calendar any more than necessary. By not waiting any longer, you are more likely to have an enthusiastic group willing to plan the year.

In the case of high adventure, the patrol leaders' council may actually need to look more than 15 months out. For example, if you are planning a trip to the Philmont Scout Ranch in New Mexico, you must sign up during the fall two years *before* the actual trip.

**If you elect youth leaders in the fall and spring for six-month terms, only those Scouts elected in the spring will have a say in the troop calendar. That is why we recommend holding your annual planning conferences where they fine-tune the upcoming six months' plan and develop the following six months' plan.**

#### Preparing for the Annual Planning Conference

Before you schedule your planning conference, the committee chair, Scoutmaster, and senior patrol leader should perform the following steps a month or two before the formal meeting:

**Step 1—Gather necessary information:**

- Key school dates, such as holidays and exams
- Community event dates, including events the troop might want to participate in or donate its services to
- The chartered organization's key dates, open considering opportunities for additional or innovative fundraising opportunities that might reduce scheduling conflicts
- Personal dates that may affect the troop's activities, such as the Scoutmaster's anniversary cruise
- Key district and council dates, including summer camp, winter camp, council-sponsored meetings, and dates of the Arrow Lodge functions
- Data collected from the troop resource survey
- Last year's troop annual plan, if you have one
- Troop priorities and goals
- Scouts' advancement records
- General outline of next year's program

**Step 2—Discuss the planning process with your senior patrol leader, explaining the importance of the process and the senior patrol leader's role in planning meetings and activities and your troop goals. Show your draft outline for next year's program, and ask the senior patrol leader for input and suggestions. Also share your outline with your chartered organization representative and ask for input and planning conference guide (available at www.scouting.org/programs/scouts) so the senior patrol leader will understand the agenda and can work ahead.**

---

> As you develop your draft plan, focus more on likely dates for outings than on outing themes or destinations. Leave those details for the Scouts at the planning conference.

#### Setting Goals

Here are possibilities for types of troop goals your draft plan might include:

- Have an outdoor adventure at least once a month.
- Strengthen hiking and expedition skills by providing at least two backpacking trips, supplemented by increasing your proficiency on Scout Sunday or on the Sabbath.
- Earn the National Camping Award and a gold arrowhead in Excellence rating.
- Conduct a fundraiser to help pay for troop expenses such as new tents and other camping gear.
- Have each patrol earn the National Honor Patrol Award at least once.

**Step 3—Your senior patrol leader shares the draft plan with patrol leaders. Patrol leaders schedule a meeting to gather information and ideas. Besides reviewing that draft plan, patrols could evaluate the current year's plan. A useful tool is the Scout's Troop Method, which uses these simple questions:**

1. What should we start doing that we are not on it?
2. What should we *stop* doing that is not working?
3. What should we *continue* doing that is working well and helping us succeed?

**This is useful for Scouts to have input on the current year's calendar available for review, as well as your draft plan. They should out Plan Preferences in Troop materials, to note any significant improvements or advancement during troop meetings or to build more excitement into the calendar for patrol outings, possibly you accomplish all the goals you set.**

**Step 4—Invite the following people to attend the conference to maximize the discovery of your calendar:**

- Your troop's youth leaders
- Troop committee members and other adult leaders
- Chartered organization representative
- Your unit commissioner
- Anyone else who might be helpful, such as other parents

---

> Keep in mind that these people will play very different roles at the conference:
>
> - In an active role—senior patrol leader, assistant senior patrol leader, patrol leaders, troop guide
> - In a support role—Scoutmaster, committee chair, chartered organization representatives, junior assistant Scoutmasters, and any other adults
>
> To keep the planning conference as purposeful and efficient as possible, invite only those who understand the need for active participation and decision-making.

### Holding the Annual Planning Conference

You can hold your annual planning conference at your regular meeting place on a Saturday morning or afternoon or at a park, school, or other suitable facility. If you have a troop retreat center, you might consider bringing the conference into an overnight retreat to allow more time for discussion and team building.

Most adults should not play active roles in the planning conference. Instead, they should give their finished ideas to the Scoutmaster or assistant Scoutmaster for consideration in the final planning. Besides the materials described above, you might choose posters for your work and the troop's work. For suggestions, see the office supplies and laminated wall planners with write-on/wipe-off surfaces. Pencil in the dates from your draft plan, as well as other important dates, like council meetings, school, and chartering community events.

Another useful tool is the Troop Program Planning Chart shown in chapter 8. You might want to display your program features, activities, counts of honor service provide, leadership meetings, and more. The worksheet can help you and the patrol leaders coordinate which month's different activities occur each month and that your courts of honor and service projects are properly spaced throughout the year.

The outcome of the conference should be an annual calendar, listing all monthly program features (e.g., hiking, canoeing, skiing), dates for all scheduled activities, and a brief description of the program. The calendar should include one or two familiar and worthwhile activities and institutions. A few traditional activities—a particular campsite, a special hike, or a particular activity—help make the program feel repetitive. Even traditional outings can benefit from occasional tweaks, such as doing a favorite activity at a new location.

## The Annual Planning Conference, Step by Step

**Step 1 –** The Scoutmaster leads a discussion on the troop's goals for the next 12 months. Base your goals on a report or master board and agree to a list of goals.

**Step 2 –** Show with the aid of the meeting attendees the draft printed calendar that shows the dates you researched for review. Take any dates that should appear on posted calendars around the community.

**Step 3 –** Take a few minutes to discuss these dates and events. Once you feel comfortable with all the dates at this stage of the calendar, you might take a vote to approve the dates you have so far.

**Step 4 –** Base your discussion with your leaders about what Scouts want to do. This can be the most challenging exercise in your program planning conference, so take as much time as you need. You could use Program Features for Troops and Crews described later in this chapter to help you with this step. After all, the activities you plan will take it one month at a time. Don't forget to add in advancement opportunities. The flow of your troop's program is very important if one of your goals is for the troop to take a wilderness trip to Alaska, some of your program could focus on traveling to and backpacking skills. Incorporate fun activities and vary skills with variety and salinity.

Again, as you agree on a monthly feature or program theme, write it in a flipchart or board and take a vote. Designate someone to write all this in a master calendar and take it home.

**Step 5 –** Add other important dates such as:

- Troop meetings and patrol leaders' council meetings
- Boards of review
- Courts of honor
- Troop and patrol elections
- Troop open house
- Service projects
- Arrow of Light Scout joint outings and transition ceremonies
- Any other troop and patrol activities that can be scheduled this far in advance

**Step 6 –** Hold a final discussion on the plan, calendar, and goals, and then take action for approval. Once you approve your annual plan, it will go to the troop committee for final approval. Work with the committee to make any necessary changes to facilities reservations at your chartered organization. As soon as possible, begin making campsite reservations for the dates and locations you have approved.

**Step 7 –** To make this plan a truly valuable tool, it must be shared with each troop family, your chartered organization, and all other interested parties. You must follow your plan, share it with everyone, and review it regularly to see if modifications have become necessary.

> While discussing ideas at the conference, use these ground rules:
> - It is important to respect each other's views. Listen and don't interrupt.
> - Keep focused on your task to plan your annual program. Don't get sidetracked.
> - Write out ideas so everyone can see them.
> - Be in agreement.

> Robert Baden-Powell once said **"a boy is not a sitting-down animal." Be sure to take frequent breaks during the planning conference for stretching, refreshments, or a quick game.**

## After the Planning Conference

Your planning conference is over, and you are ready for distribution. Here are some features to consider:

- Schedule as much detail as possible, including meeting start and end times.
- Indicate when regular meetings won't be held or will be held at different times.
- Code events for easy reference. For example, you might use different colors or symbols for meetings and outings or even for meetings that don't affect all members (such as patrol leaders' council, troop committees, boards of review, rifle shooting, and swimming merit badge classes).
- Be sure to list summer camp and other trips, including national Scout jamborees, roundtables, and training courses.
- Add a notation date at the bottom of every page if the calendar is longer than one page. You must update the calendar, change the revision date and highlight the new events.
- Share the finalized calendar with your Scoutmaster, committee members, adult leaders, members of the troop committee, and chartered organization's leader, secretary, and webmaster. Post it on your troop website and in your meeting place.

# Program Features for Troops and Crews

The three-volume *Program Features for Troops and Crews* may be your next best friend. Each volume is separated by grade level and includes information on health and safety, citizenship and personal development, STEM (science, technology, engineering, and mathematics), and outdoor skills. You can use these to plan fun, exciting, and challenging events suitable for the experience level of their group.

Each program feature includes general information about the topic, advancement and awards-related information, and complete meeting plans. All activities use the EDGE method (Explain, Demonstrate, Guide, Enable). Four meeting plans, outlines of three main events, and den meeting ideas are included. Activities can be tied to the liters used in the revamped Venturing program, plus related print and online resources. Keep in mind that outdoor activities have special leadership and safety requirements, as described throughout this guidebook.

The plans were made to be adaptable; use them as a starting point and to spur creativity. The patrol leaders' council can select and modify a plan from each volume to create its own monthly theme and activities. The innovative format—designed to support Scout troops and Venturing crews—offers feedback from volunteers who were surveyed. Best of all, the fresh ideas come from actual Scouts and Venturing crew members who represented each program area.

Their efforts have led to adaptable and easy-to-follow meeting plans, outdoor activity ideas, and the four-meeting format.

# Short-Term Planning

No matter how comprehensive and well planned your troop calendar is, it is simply a skeleton on which to build your troop program. Short-term planning puts flesh on the bones.

## Patrol Leaders' Council Meeting

At each regular monthly meeting, the patrol leaders' council plans the activities for the following month's troop meetings. If the PLC really is on the ball, it actually looks at three months' programming each time.

- It briefly reviews the month that is just ending using the Start, Stop, Continue model.
- It plans in detail everything the troop will do in the month that is just beginning for all troop level.
- It briefly previews the following month and makes assignments for who will be planning companies.

> See the Senior Patrol Leader Handbook for complete information on leading patrol leader council meetings.

That is a lot to accomplish in a 60- to 90-minute PLC meeting that also includes other business, so the senior patrol leader may want to delegate parts of the planning process. For example, a senior or an assistant senior PLC member and an assistant Scoutmaster might work on campout details for the assignment made at the previous month's PLC meeting at the troop program planning conference. By using different planners, you allow each group to better focus on their assigned event.

Some planning details can be automated. Rather than let the PLC argue for 15 minutes over who should do the opening next week, have the senior patrol leader set up a duty roster that rotates responsibilities. Create a simple checklist for troop setup and cleanup, and the interpatrol activity.

The Scoutmaster's role before and during the patrol leaders' council meeting is to

- Coach the senior patrol leader about of time on the agenda and what needs to be accomplished during the meeting.
- Be familiar with the troop's annual program plan and what is happening in the council and the chartered organization.
- Provide resources gleaned from Program Features for Troops and Crews and perhaps other sections of this book and other sources.
- Help the PLC get in the group makes that will call for special physical demands or require resources or the neighborhood troop committee support.
- Help the senior patrol leader keeps the meeting on track and on schedule.
- Share a Scoutmaster's Minute and perhaps a brief training module.

After the patrol leaders' council meeting, the Scoutmaster should inform the troop committee of decisions made so committee members can provide the necessary support. (Given this sequence, it makes sense for the patrol leaders' council to meet prior to the troop committee each month or, if they meet at the same time, the PLC should ensure the monthly plan is distributed to the Scouts, their families, other adult leaders, and members of the chartered organization. Distribution could be in the form of a troop newsletter, a responsible adult posted PLC minutes, outing fliers, or some other form of those methods.

> As an adult leader, you can serve as the conscience of the group. If you see the group is skipping over details on troop meeting plans or is not being thoughtful about them to decide, if they try to defer to a Scout who is absent, encourage someone to call the Scout. If they are not sure of something that should be done, pull out this book or see if a PLC member can look up the information on a smartphone or tablet.

## Patrol Leaders' Council Huddles

Before each meeting or troop activity, the senior patrol leader should communicate with each individual responsible for a part of that meeting or activity. A brief five-minute huddle should take place at the meeting or activity earlier than other Scouts for a quick huddle.

After each troop meeting or activity, the senior patrol leader should hold a brief meeting of the patrol leaders' council to discuss what went well and what could be improved at the next event on the troop's calendar, and modify any parts of the monthly plan that require adjustment.

# CHAPTER 8
# Troop Meetings

Over the course of a year, the typical troop will hold dozens of troop meetings. In fact, the number of these meetings will probably exceed the number of outings, service projects, and holiday programs combined. Troop meetings need to be very deftly planned.

They also give you plenty of opportunities to fail. When troop meetings are not organized and executed well, Scouts get frustrated, and every other part of the troop program suffers. The most important thing adults need to understand about troop meetings is that adults are to

**Exercising leadership.** The Scouts themselves plan and conduct troop meetings. This autonomy lets them develop skills that can only be learned through practice and demonstrated by they can set high standards and stay accountable. Often, the only time Scouts will see an adult leader actively involved is during advancement requirements, earning badges and gives the Scoutmaster's Minute.

**Sharing with the group.** Patrol leaders gather together in breakout sessions, compete together in interpatrol activities, and develop Scouting elements like flag ceremonies.

**Promoting advancement and personal growth.** Individual Scouts have the chance to get advancement requirements signed off by troop may offer classes for popular merit badges although the Scouts themselves are not completely skill before the troop meeting happens. The Skills for Life troop advancement emblems program, an excellent opportunity for the chaplain aide to demonstrate leadership.

**Inspiring Scouts.** The opening and closing ceremonies and the Scoutmaster's Minute emphasize the ideals of Scouting.

**Having fun.** Games and activities the Scouts anticipate most give troop meetings fun. A Scoutmaster who are in school for days or more hours a day, do not let your meetings feel like drudgery.

> The best troop meetings incorporate all eight methods of Scouting, as described in chapter 1, without overshadowing any of them.

Beyond these big-picture purposes, every Scout should accomplish something specific at every meeting. Each troop meeting should help each Scout learn a new skill, complete an advancement requirement, get prepared for an upcoming outing, enjoyed fellowship with other Scouts or just have a good time. At the end of the meeting, there is a risk that the Scout may decide not to come back next week. (The same goes for adult leaders, the best volunteers want to do more than drink coffee and share stories.)

## The Parts of a Troop Meeting

Troop meetings include these eight parts:

1. Preparing
2. Opening
3. Group instruction
4. Skills development
5. Patrol meetings
6. Interpatrol activity
7. Closing
8. After the meeting

Parts 2 through 8 take 60 to 90 minutes total. Parts 1 and 8 each add 15 minutes.

## Scheduling Troop Meetings

For a general rule, troop host meets once a week at the same day and at the same time for 60 to 90 minutes. Weeknight meetings are most common, but some troops find that weekends work better for them.

Some troops meet every other week, perhaps for a longer period, while others do troop meetings or a campout if they meet on Friday, Saturday, or Sunday. These scouts and work as long as you communicate the schedule clearly to the parents. I know that some troops have found that meeting biweekly meeting may have only one contact with the troop during an entire month.

Another variation is to hold three troop meetings per month and leave one week open for games, meetings, patrol leaders' council for clear communication is essential.

Most troops need the chartered organization's location, and some are even lucky enough to have dedicated space. But don't let your meeting space limit where you can do. Whenever possible, get outside. Even a small patch of grass in a lot or a nearby city park—to play games or practice Scout skills.

## The Purposes of Troop Meetings

Troop meetings serve many purposes, including the following:

**Advancement.** Scouts work on advancement requirements, receive instruction, check equipment, choose tent mates, and handle other details related to upcoming outings.

**Learning and practicing Scouting skills.** Scouts spend part of the troop meeting learning and practicing skills that they will need on upcoming outings or that will help them advance in rank.
