## 1. Preopening (15 minutes)

Several things happen during the preopening:

* The designated service patrol prepares the meeting place by setting up equipment, moving chairs, getting flags and flag stands ready, and doing whatever else must be done ahead of time.

* The senior patrol leader huddles with those in charge of specific parts of the troop meeting to review the tasks they were assigned at the patrol leaders' council meeting.

* An assigned youth, usually a Scout, conducts a flag ceremony. Leaders like to see Scouts learn from them and perhaps teaches them for themselves by allowing the honor of leading the ceremony. For instance, if they will be teaching lashings, since the activity might be to make sure each patrol can tie a clove hitch and a timber hitch.

## 2. Opening (10 minutes)

The senior patrol leader calls the meeting to order—on time!—with the Scoutmaster present. The designated program patrol conducts a flag ceremony and leads troop members in reciting the Scout Oath and Scout Law. Scout for variety in opening ceremonies to keep things fresh.

> **Troop Resources:** A BSA website at www.scouting.org/scoutresources/ offers suggestions for creative openings and closings.

## 3. Group Instruction (10 minutes)

This part of the meeting focuses on a few aspects of the monthly outing that all Scouts should know before the outing. When a winter camping trip is planned, for instance, an expert in cold-weather camping might lead it.

## 4. Skill Development (35 minutes)

This part of the meeting focuses on specific skills Scouts need for upcoming outings or advancement. If all your Scouts are about the same age or rank or if you are about to undertake a long-term high-adventure trip, you might want to work on a specific skill as a whole troop rather than in patrols.

* The new Scout patrol might work with this troop guide to learn basic skills.

* Experienced Scouts might work with the instructor using the teaching skills to learn more advanced skills.

* Some Scouts might spend the time planning a special patrol outing, or they might meet with the troop's Life-to-Eagle coordinator about Eagle Scout service project ideas.

> **Whoever is teaching should use Scouting's Teaching Edge. First, explain the skill, then demonstrate it while the Scouts watch carefully. Have them practice the skill. Finally, enable them by creating an environment where they can continue to practice it. Repetition is key. Remember, practice so they can eventually use the skill on their own. You can do this by removing any obstacles to success, enabling the learner to succeed.**

You might also split the group along different lines. Scouts who plan to attend an upcoming campout could practice the skills they'll need. Scouts who are working toward a rank could work on advancement requirements. Leaders like the quartermaster and scribe might opt out altogether to work in their offices.

As you can see, this part of the meeting will look different every single week. One thing should not change: Any instruction should be relevant to the participants and be as hands-on as possible. Do not teach knot-tying for its own sake, and don't spend 15 minutes talking about Leave No Trace principles. Instead, weave this wisdom from the 1947 Handbook for Scoutmasters: "Young people have a thousand muscles to wiggle with and only one dozen to sit still with. That dozen gets mighty sore mighty quickly."

## 5. Patrol Meetings (15 minutes)

At the conclusion of skills development, the patrols break off for their own short meetings. These meetings can be held simultaneously in different areas of the meeting place—in corners of rooms or outdoors. The patrol leader runs the meeting, perhaps being backed up by an adult patrol advisor.

Business items can include taking attendance, collecting dues, planning the patrol's involvement in upcoming troop activities, and discussing the results of the last outing. You might use this time to sort out any other details required for the smooth operation of the patrol. The agenda should be clear, and the patrol leader should be responsible for making sure all the necessary tasks are accomplished.

> **See the Patrol Leader Handbook for more information on patrol meetings.**

## 6. Interpatrol Activity (15 minutes)

After patrol meetings, the troop comes back together for an activity the six patrols interact with one another in a friendly competition. You might organize a relay race or a scavenger hunt, or perhaps a game using lashings. If you ran a lashing activity during the skills-development period, you might run a Norman chariot race, where patrols build and race "chariots" (wooden frames lashed together) in the gymnasium. You might do a game that is designed for fun.

> **See Troop Resources at www.scouting.org/scoutresources/troop/activities and Belay On for more activities, including the Roman chariot race.**

## 7. Closing (5 minutes)

The closing is the Scoutmaster's time. Any necessary announcements are made and recognition is given to Scouts who have advanced since the last meeting or who have excelled in some way. This is the time to challenge Scouts and perhaps a brief opportunity to share a story based on Scouting's values.

Troop Resources [www.scouting.org/programs/scouts-bsa/] includes examples, but the best Scoutmaster's Minutes stem from the Scoutmaster's personal experience or from areas, like conservation or world brotherhood, that connect meaningfully to a Scout's life. The key is to make a direct connection to a Scouting value. "You have all promised on your honor to be loyal. Wait, let me tell you about a Scout I know who took that promise seriously and did what was right even when it was tough."

---

## Using Adult Leaders at Troop Meetings

Adult leaders should be at troop meetings much the same as at outdoor outings. Adults should take an active role, not just be more involved, doing a hard job. They should take on specific responsibilities. Here are some suggestions:

* Give the Scoutmaster's Minute.

* Assist with skills instruction if you have advanced.

* Keep track of what should be happening at the moment (checking by the troop meeting plan) and quietly remind the senior patrol leader when things are off schedule.

* Help the patrols with specific skills that group leaders are qualified to teach.

* Touch base with the youth leaders during the next part of the meeting to see how the Scout is progressing.

* Sit in on patrol meetings and skills instruction to provide feedback to the youth leaders. (By quietly standing nearby with a reassuring presence, adults support youth leaders without usurping authority.)

* Check in with youth leader counters. For example, the equipment coordinator and quartermaster might meet to discuss upcoming purchases.

* Counsel on merit badges and sign-off on advancement requirements.

* Watch for Scouts who are not involved or who wander away from the meeting place.

Adults should never do these things:

* Run the meeting. (That's the senior patrol leader's actual emergency.)

* Criticize or call out Scouts. A good rule is "Praise in public, criticize in private."

* Jump in and help. ("Nice try! Let me show you the right way.") For getting, avoid it if the group leader is the step leader's responsibility.

---

## Understanding Troop Meetings

As you can tell from the previous section, a troop meeting is not like a Scout den meeting, where everyone has the same birthday. Instead, it is more like a legislative session. The whole group comes together during the opening, then breaks apart into smaller groups, which will be operating somewhat independently by themselves. There are also plenty of ad hoc mingling around the room. Disagreement happens, and the youngest Scouts might feel out of place in groups.

The feel and result of good troop meetings leads to a couple of important points:

* Parents, former Cub Scout leaders, and other outsiders can best understand how meetings are run as an accomplishment. It is important to help these people understand how troop meetings work and what roles can do to be helpful.

* Some Scouts—particularly those who are used to very structured environments—might see a Scout who is not doing anything. They must see Scout engaged in what is going on.

> **Be wary of letting your youth leaders fall too often as they plan and lead troop meetings. While a few Scouts dropping the ball won't cause the troop to suffer, it is better to work behind the scenes to help them succeed. It is better to work behind the scenes to help them succeed rather than letting them—or their troop—suffer. It is better to work behind the scenes to help them succeed rather than letting them—or their leaders miss the details of their predecessors.**

---

<!-- Location 44 of 149 + 28% -->

---

# Tips for Effective Troop Meetings

- Start on time and end on time.
- Include variety, action, and purpose.
- Vary your routine on occasion by having a special visitor, planning a round-robin game tournament, or attempting a special project.
- Get outdoors whenever possible.
- Encourage youth leaders to use the Scout sign to get attention—and to wait until everyone is quiet before talking.
- Make the meetings fun to enjoy. If the troop does a favorite game, long things tend by alternating it with other games so scouts stay interested.
- Mix up your games so that scouts rely on skill, rather on speed, some rely on dexterity.
- Keep games moving and try to complete them in the time planned.
- Plan your Scoutmaster's Minutes well and keep them to a minute roughly 120 words.
- Always leave the Scouts wanting more.

# Evaluating Troop Meetings

How do you know whether a troop meeting was successful? You can have the patrol leaders' council discuss the Start, Stop, Continue questions:

- What are we not doing that we should start doing?
- What is not working that we should stop doing?
- What are we doing well that we should continue doing?

You can also evaluate a meeting by looking at how many Scouts attended, how many completed their advancement requirements, at how well I met the needs of new Scouts, experienced Scouts, and older Scouts. But the real proof may come later. Is the next meeting better attended? Are more Scouts working on advancement? Does the Scoutmaster receive a minimal number of phone calls from confused parents between meetings? If so, you can congratulate yourself and your youth leaders on a job well done.

---

# CHAPTER 9
## The Outdoor Program

The outdoor program is where Scouting—*real Scouting*—happens. "This is do Scouting without the outdoor program," is like reading a cookbook but never cooking, joining a gym but never exercising, or predicting a sport but never competing.

"*Camping is the boy's Elysium and the Scoutmaster's opportunity*" is a motto worth remembering.

When Scouts hike through pristine wilderness, they discover the beauty of nature and learn to accept responsibility as stewards of the environment. When they cook together as a patrol, they discover what it really means to be part of a team. When they set up camp in the rain and everything still gets done, they discover their own leadership. And when they climb or cycle or swim just a little farther than they ever imagined they could, they discover their own capacities.

Even so, the outdoor program is a method of Scouting, nothing more and nothing less. While outdoor skills may never be perfected, camping is not the end-all of Scouting. It is only one of the steps—though perhaps the most potential of the steps—toward our aim of building up happy, healthy, helpful citizens.

As a result of the outdoor program, some of our Scouts may discover a passion for the wilderness. Some may go on to hike the Appalachian Trail or stand atop Mount Everest. Still others may hang up their hiking boots forever the day they leave Scouting. None of that matters so long as they have the lessons of Scouting with them wherever they go.

## Scouting's Continuum of Outdoor Activities

Outdoor activities are important at every level of Scouting, but the specific change as young people mature and progress through the ranks. Part of the Scoutmaster's responsibility is ensuring which activities are age-appropriate and which may be a little too challenging. The point is not to withhold fun activities from younger Scouts, but rather to make sure the challenge level is right. A typical who enjoys hiking five miles into camp, sleeping under a tarp, and using a cathole latrine. Similarly, a 16-year-old Life Scout won't get very excited about camping in the backyard.

As the Troop Program Planning Chart in chapter 7 shows, new Scouts might do activities like hiking to a nearby campsite or attending a council campfire—activities that are a step above what they did in Arrow of Light Scouting and that mostly take place in the homecounty. Experienced Scouts might be ready for a backpacking trip to a more distant location, backpacking trip, or even a canoe trip and backpacking hike. In keeping with this philosophy, the BSA's national high-adventure bases set minimum age requirements for participation, as do many council-run programs.

The task for troops is to develop activities that combine the right amount of fun and challenge for Scouts of all ages. Here are some ideas you could use to do:

- On a weekend campout, younger Scouts could ride to the campsite while older Scouts backpack in.
- At summer camp, younger Scouts could work on merit badges, and older Scouts could participate in COPE or an older-Scout program that takes them away from camp for a week or more.
- During the spring, you could hold a special orientation campout for new Scouts, bringing along a handful of older Scouts to teach skills.
- Over the summer, you could take your older Scouts on a trip to one of the BSA's national high-adventure bases.
- During the fall, you could set aside one month for older-Scout advancement.

Also keep in mind that Scouts like challenges in more than just physical activities. A day of campsite competitions can be as challenging for the patrol leader who must prepare and lead Scouts as it can be for the Scouts themselves.

## Planning Patrol Activities

With the proper training, guidance, and approval by the troop committee, patrols can plan and lead short hikes and service projects as long as they follow two rules:

- The Scoutmaster must approve the patrol activity.
- The patrol activity cannot interfere with any troop function.

A patrol day hike or service project can be allowed only when it has been thoroughly planned and the Scoutmaster is satisfied that a qualified adult will be present and responsible. If the Scoutmaster has any doubts, the patrol should revise or reconsider its plans until those concerns are satisfactorily addressed.

Patrol activities require the same adult coverage as any other Scouting activity. All registered adult leaders 21 years of age or over are required at all Scouting activities, including all meetings. There must be a registered female adult leader 21 years of age or over present if female youth are participants. A registered male adult leader 21 years of age or over must be present for any activity involving female youth or female adult program participants.

Scouting's Barriers to Abuse, found in the Guide to Safe Scouting and at www.scouting.org/training/youth-protection, should always be followed.

---

# Activities

## Penalty Planning and Risk Assessment

No organization, including the Boy Scouts of America, can operate every possible activity that could be conducted as part of a unit, district, or council event. As such, it is neither the intent nor the duty of this guide to provide specific guidance on every conceivable activity. Instead, we have provided tools and resources so the Program Hazard Analysis, SAFE safety checklists, and the PAUSE cost.

For those activities that support the values of the Boy Scouts of America, there are several tools available to participants that will help them plan for a fun and safe tour, activity, or event. Good planning and comprehension prior to executing the activity is key to understanding risk. Two of those tools are the Program Hazard Analysis and SAFE safety resources are the Program Hazard Analysis, SAFE safety checklists, and the PAUSE cost.

As you use these tools, reflect on the words of Robert Baden-Powell: "The most important rule is: Do the right thing and do it right, so the result of that is Scout from having an accident or getting hurt. You want to know how to act on any accident or emergency so that he is never taken by surprise."

## Program Hazard Analysis

This is a process to identify program areas within camps or during activities that have inherent risks. This tool is defined by assessing probability and severity of loss. This tool identifies risks initially, as if there are no protective measures in place, then looks at the risks again with protective measures.

## Safety Checklists

These tools are used to inspect a vehicle or meeting place when taking Scouts on trips to campgrounds. Checklists are a "body of knowledge" for running Scouting activities safely. Like an airline pilot who uses a checklist before takeoff, these tools help to ensure your unit has taken proper measures to address risks in your Scouting activity. Many safety-related organizations include SAFE checklists, Safe Sport Defense, Safety Afloat, and Climb on Safety are examples.

## Safety PAUSE

The Safety PAUSE process stresses the importance of a last-minute safety check in the field. By encouraging each Scout or adult to PAUSE and think about potential hazards before beginning, you have an opportunity to take necessary precautions to prevent any present or potential hazards.

# Outdoor Ethics

### Ethics Discussion

Scouts out have when Scouts out have been taught to tread ground beds and ditched their tents to keep out the rain. Today's Scouts pride themselves on taking nothing but pictures, leaving nothing but footprints. To that end, the BSA created the Outdoor Code, which sets forth Scouting's aspirations in the outdoors. As you follow the principles of Leave No Trace and Tread Lightly!, described below.

### The Outdoor Code

*As an American, I will do my best to—*
- *Be clean in my outdoor manners.*
- *Be careful with fire.*
- *Be considerate in the outdoors.*
- *Be conservation-minded.*

## Leave No Trace

Leave No Trace principles are just as applicable to backcountry activities at Scout camps, state parks, and private campgrounds as they are in the backcountry.

### 1. Plan Ahead and Prepare.

Proper trip planning helps you accomplish trip goals safely, be self-sufficient, minimize your impact on the natural and cultural resources, and ensure a better experience for your troop and other groups.

### 2. Travel and Camp on Durable Surfaces.

Stay on established trails—even when they are wet or muddy—and use existing campsites whenever possible.

### 3. Dispose of Waste Properly.

Pack it in, pack it out. This simple saying motivates visitors to take their trash home with them, from food scraps to human waste.

### 4. Leave What You Find.

Leave rocks, plants, animals, archeological artifacts, and other objects of interest as you find them. Removing items, including historic buildings or take photographs. In some places, it is illegal to remove artifacts.

### 5. Minimize Campfire Impacts.

The naturalness of many areas has been degraded by overuse of campsites and removal of deadwood. To minimize campfire impacts as well as the potential for brush and forest fires, use backpacking stoves for cooking and flashlights for lighting. When you do build a fire, use established fire rings, and remove all unburned materials from the ground that you can break by hand.

### 6. Respect Wildlife.

Camp quietly, and observe wildlife from afar, giving animals a wide berth. Store food securely and keep garbage and food scraps away from animals.

### 7. Be Considerate of Other Visitors.

Thoughtful campers travel and camp quietly and in small groups, select campsites away from other groups, wear clothing and use equipment in earth-tone colors, and keep noise levels low. Keep gates open or closed as they were found, and be considerate of other campers, and they will likely return the favor.

> The member-driven Leave No Trace Center for Outdoor Ethics teaches people how to enjoy the outdoors responsibly. The principles described above have been reprinted with permission from the Leave No Trace Center for Outdoor Ethics, www.LNT.org.

## Tread Lightly!

**Tread Lightly!** provides promote safe and responsible shooting, boating, and use of motorized vehicles.

- **Travel Responsibly.** Stay on designated roads, trails, and recreation areas. Avoid wet, muddy trails. Launch and use watercraft only in designated areas.
- **Respect the Rights of Others.** Allow others to enjoy the outdoors in solitude and safety. Stay out of posted lands. On trails, yield the right-of-way to those who are coming uphill. Move to the right side of the trail to allow those on horseback to pass.

---

# Educate Yourself

Study maps and regulations of places you plan to visit. Do not have to know how to operate a chainsaw or other equipment safely.

- A nature hike to identify native animals and plants for Second Class requirement 6
- A night hike that ends at an observatory for star study
- A fitness hike with stations where Scouts complete activities
- An orienteering hike, where Scouts navigate by map and compass
- A geocaching hike, where Scouts navigate from cache to cache in a large city park
- A service hike along the shoreline, where patrols complete a service project while enjoying a hike
- A "big Scouts" hike, where the troop eats lunch at various places of worship in the community to learn about the ways people worship

As you can see, several of these options incorporate other methods of Scouting, including advancement, the patrol method, and personal growth. When you think creatively, a hike becomes more than just a walk; it becomes a vehicle for achieving many of the aims of Scouting: outdoor skills, leadership, and mental and physical fitness.

---

# Being Conservation-Minded

The Outdoor Code encourages Scouts to be "conservation-minded." This means thinking about protecting the environment rather than just ground to be hiked over and rivers to be conquered, but as a large component of soil, plants, and animals to care for and maintain as part of Scouting for many years. Keeping this tradition alive is an important part of Scouting leadership.

## Types of Outings

Scouts groups can be categorized into one type of activity, like backpacking, but most offer a wide variety of outings throughout the year, each with its own benefits. Each outing offers your Scouts an opportunity to excel, and a source of personal challenge.

What kinds of outings can troops take? It is easier to discover what they can't take. The Guide to Safe Scouting includes a short list of activities that are unauthorized, including boxing, skydiving, and rappelling more than 20 feet from the ground or target shooting. A longer list of activities requires special training and/or certification.

Avoid the temptation to pursue an unauthorized activity as a "rite of passage"—all of which should be the Scouts and adult leaders in the same troop. Doing so sends the wrong message about what is acceptable in Scouting and creates a distrust of non-Scouting activities with their friends, which each adult decision about what activities to pursue should be based on whether they will advance your program's objectives and help Scouts and adult leaders grow.

---

## Planning for the Camping Merit Badge

To earn the Camping merit badge, a Scout must complete at least 20 days and 20 nights of camping and do at least two of the following:

- A backpacking trip of at least 1,000 vertical feet
- A backpack, canoe, or cross-country ski for at least 4 miles

# The Ninth Method of Scouting

Back when you first attended Scout leader training, you learned that the ninth method of Scouting encompasses advancement, ideals, leadership development, outdoor programs, patrol method, personal growth, and the uniform. Now that you're a seasoned leader, however, you realize that there's a ninth, unstated method, too.

The activities you plan—whether a monthly campfire, a quarterly camping trip, a winter campout or a week at summer camp—is not enough to transform a Scout into an adult. But when you weave those weeks into months, and the months turn into years, incredible transformations happen.

As an experienced troop leader, you've watched those transformations firsthand. You've seen the shy, awkward machines we call Arrow of Light Scouts grow into responsible young adults. You've seen Scouts who couldn't get through a night of camping without a meltdown head 12-day treks in the wilderness. You've been to your Scouts' high-school graduations, their colleges, and their weddings.

You've also discovered that your troop, like its members, always has room to grow. That the troop needs a unifying vision. Perhaps you're struggling to retain older Scouts or serve youth with special needs. Perhaps you want to do a better job with your troop's leadership development or improve your patrol method.

This guidebook is for you. For the first time, Scouting America has created a publication that focuses exclusively on the needs of leaders who've moved beyond the basics—who've been there, done that, and gotten the t-shirt (and the patches). We hope you'll use it often.

# Troop Leader Essentials

These publications are trusted resources that provide valuable information and insight:

- **Scout BSA Handbook for Boys and Revised BSA Handbook for Girls**—The primary resource for Scouts (and school leaders) on skills needed to learn Scouting skills
- **Field Book for Boys and Girls**—A resource with handy how-to's and handbooks that covers advanced outdoor skills
- **Troop Leader Guidebook (Volumes 1 and 2)**—The primary resource for assistant Scoutmasters
- **Troop Committee Guidebook**—The primary resource for troop committee members
- **Program Features for Troops and Crews (Volumes 1, 2, and 3)**—Complete troop program features, 16 per volume, that include meeting plans, outing ideas, and much more
- **Scouts BSA Requirements (current year)**—Requirements for ranks, merit badges, and special awards, updated annually
- **Uniform and Insignia Guide**—A comprehensive guide to wearing uniforms and badges correctly
- **Guide to Advancement**—The official source for advancement in all Scouting America program phases
- **Guide to Safe Scouting**—The primary source for information on conducting Scouting activities in a safe manner
- **Patrol Leader Handbook**—The official guide for patrol leaders
- **Senior Patrol Leader Handbook**—The official guides for other troop-level youth leaders
- **Scouting magazine**—The official online magazine for Scout leaders and parents, scoutingmagazine.org
- **Scout Life magazine**—Scouting America's official youth magazine, published 10 times a year, scoutlife.org
- **Scouts.bsa**—www.scouts.org/programs/scouts-bsa

# Support Resources

Scouting America has created a network of resources at your fingertips, including roundtables and training courses, Scouting magazine, the Scouting America website, www.scouts.org, and this guidebook and its companion, volume 1, which offers an introduction to the troop leader role for leaders who are relatively inexperienced leaders.

Scouting America also offers resources that are of particular interest to experienced leaders, including Wood Badge and Powder Horn training described in chapter 18 of volume 1), Philmont Scout Ranch and backcountry experiences offered by the Paul R. Christensen, and the conferences held each summer at the Philmont Training Center and the John D. Tickle National Training and Leadership School (at the Philmont Training Center). These conferences let you meet and share ideas with other seasoned leaders from across the country.

Of course, the longer you continue in Scouting, the more you become a resource yourself. Serving on roundtable staff or training courses, mentoring newer leaders, sharing your skills and keep up with changes in the Scouting program. Your district executive, district commissioner, or district training chair can let you know more about these opportunities.

*Thank you for your continuing service to Scouts BSA!*

# Overview of the Troop Leader Guidebook

## Volume 1

**Section 1: Scouts BSA Basics.** An introduction to Scouts BSA, including mission, aims, methods, ideals, troop structure, and membership and leadership requirements.

**Section 2: The Scouts BSA Program.** An introduction to the elements of the troop program and program planning, including camping and other outings.

**Section 3: You and Your Scouts.** Guidance for every Scout from the new recruit to the senior patrol leader.

**Section 4: All About Adults.** Working with every adult from the new parent to the troop committee chairperson.

**Section 5: Advancement and Awards.** An introduction to the youth advancement program and the array of awards Scouts, adults, and units can earn.

**Section 6: Troop Administration.** An introduction to troop administration from a Scoutmaster corps perspective, including record keeping, finances, troop meetings, member relationships, and communications.

**Section 7: Health and Safety.** A comprehensive overview of Scouting America's safety and risk-management policies.

**Appendix: Resources, Scouting websites, and glossary.**

## Volume 2

**Section 1: Assessment and Improvement.** Developing a framework for continuous improvement, laying out the steps to reach each goal.

**Section 2: Effective Leadership Today.** Exploring Scouting's toolbox of leadership techniques and how they can help you make your troop better.

**Section 3: The Power of Program and High Adventure.** Adding excitement to every activity and making high adventure accessible and achievable for all Scouts.

**Section 4: Service and Stewardship.** Powerful tools for infusing direction, excitement, responsibility, and achievement into your troop's program.

**Section 5: Keeping Scouts Involved and Interested.** Ways to keep Scouts of all ages engaged in Scouting.

**Section 6: Responding to Special Challenges.** Tried-and-true approaches to understanding pressures your Scouts may be experiencing and what to do about them.

**Appendix: Resources, Scouting websites, and glossary.**

---

# ASSESSMENT AND IMPROVEMENT

When you are a new troop leader, everything can look fresh and exciting: Troop meetings abound with energy, troop outings take you to places you've never been, and veteran leaders seem to have all the answers. As you become more seasoned or move into positions of increased responsibility, you will probably discover that there's room for improvement.

Figuring out what should change—and how it should change—is the purpose of this section.

If your troop is firing on all cylinders, don't skip over this part. In Scouting, as in life, the only constant is change. Today's great success may flop tomorrow. Keeping your troop humming means continually evaluating every aspect of troop operations.

## SECTION 1

---

# CHAPTER 1
## Troop Vision

Volume 1 of the Troop Leader Guidebook introduced the mission statement of Scouting America:

*The mission of Scouting America is to prepare young people to make ethical and moral choices over their lifetimes by instilling in them the values of the Scout Oath and Scout Law.*

Scouting America also has a vision statement:

*Scouting America's vision is for Scouting America to become a responsible, participating citizen and leader guided by the Scout Oath and Scout Law.*

Like all vision statements, this one is a picture of future success as envisioned by the organizational leaders at national. Vision statements are important because they define the direction toward which organizations are moving. When your vision is top of mind, it's easier to see where to go at crossroads and easier to avoid dead ends and rabbit trails leading nowhere. Conversely, without your goals, after all, if you don't know where you're heading, you won't know whether you're on the right path.

### Criteria for a Meaningful Vision

- A vision engages the heart and spirit.
- A vision aims toward a worthwhile goal.
- A vision gives meaning to an effort.
- A vision is simple.
- A vision is attainable.
- A vision can change over time.

Crafting a troop vision is a good first step in assessing and improving your troop. It can also help unify the Scoutmaster committee, and patrol leaders' council around a common set of priorities.

Here are some examples:

- Troop X will turn ordinary youth into adults of extraordinary character. The troop welcomes Scouts at all stages toward adulthood, providing along the way the training and support they and their families need.

- Troop X will prepare Scouts for success with lifelong skills in leadership, teamwork, service to others, a love for the outdoors and adventure, respect for themselves and others in the world.

- Troop X will develop tomorrow's leaders through a fun, exciting program with many opportunities for Scouts to learn the skills and excel in a team-based environment.

- Troop X will produce youth and adult leaders with strong personal character in family and community service by equipping them to live by ethical values.

- Troop X will develop outstanding youth leaders who seek to serve and who will forever hold their Scouting experience dear, knowing that leadership on and adventure will be the backdrop of this development.

- Troop X will help young Scouts explore new places and activities, advance in rank, and achieve the aims of Scouting America through teamwork.

- Troop X will instill servant leadership and living by ethical values while offering fun and adventure.

Different troops may have similar visions in common but also differ in what they emphasize. If all these troops were achieving their visions, you might well be able to visit their meetings and guess which statements apply to which troops.

### Crafting a Troop Vision

So what is your troop's vision? If you don't have one, it may be helpful to include your Scoutmaster, New Youth Leader Training (NYLT) and adults who have been to Wood Badge will be familiar with the concepts, enlist them to lead the visioning process. Here's a process you could follow at a PLC or troop committee meeting.

**Step 1: Review basic concepts.**

Some leaders may be unfamiliar with the concepts of vision, mission, and values. If that's the case with your group, these concepts in their professional lives but may not understand how they apply to Scouting. Take a few minutes to review the basic concepts from NYLT and Wood Badge. If members have attended those trainings, they will be familiar with the concepts, so enlist them to lead the visioning process.

Also review the mission, vision, and values of Scouting America, as well as the aims and methods of Scouts BSA. It would obviously be counterproductive to develop a troop vision that goes counter to what Scouting America is trying to accomplish.

---

# CHAPTER 2
## Evaluating Your Troop

Having a vision of future success is not enough, of course. Consider, for example, how many high school basketball players have a vision of playing professional basketball and end up in other careers. According to the National Federation of State High School Associations, about 5.8 percent of male players and 4.5 percent of female players in high school basketball end up playing in the NCAA. Of those who do play college basketball, far fewer make it to the pros. This means, at most, a tiny 0.03 percent of all high school players will ever advance to basketball professionally.

Many Scouts separate the 0.03 percent from the 99.97 percent through sheer determination and hard work, of course. But perhaps the biggest factor is the willingness to do what it takes to become successful. They make it to the pros by playing up their strengths but also identifying and overcoming their weaknesses. Other players might work on game fundamentals, for example, or might hit the weight room day after day after day.

Scouting is no different. To achieve your troop vision, you need to identify your weaknesses and develop a plan to overcome them. In this chapter, we'll discuss three important tools: troop self-assessment, annual planning, and outside input.

## Journey to Excellence

A great way to start evaluating and improving your troop's performance is the program called Journey to Excellence (JTE) program. The successor to tools such as the Quality Unit program, JTE uses a balanced scorecard approach to help leaders assess troop performance across multiple areas. It's a fancy way of saying JTE focuses on a small number of performance areas that are measurable and contribute to overall troop success. Better yet, JTE measures performance and, more importantly, improve future results.

For focus, JTE organizes performance in these 11 areas:

- Planning and budget
- Building Scouts BSA
- Retention
- Advancement
- Arrow of Light-to-Scout transition
- Recruitment
- Outdoor camping
- Long-term camping
- Service projects
- Patrol method
- Leadership and family engagement
- Trained leadership

### Using Journey to Excellence in Your Unit

If you look at Journey to Excellence as nothing but extra paperwork or online busy work to get a new ribbon for your troop flag, you're missing out on the new tool to strengthen your troop. You should complete your JTE scorecard at charter renewal time, but you should do more. Here's what JTE can bring to your unit:

### A Method for Evaluating Troop

The JTE program isn't based on things you likely are tracking already, such as advancement. It offers a simple way to gauge your troop's performance based on benchmarks for each year. A group of volunteers and professionals from across the country will look at actual data from the past three years in each area. The group then sets benchmarks, such as this one for short-term camping:

- **Bronze:** Conduct four short-term overnight campouts.
- **Silver:** Conduct seven short-term overnight campouts.
- **Gold:** Conduct nine short-term overnight campouts.

Based on your troop's performance across all 11 measures, you can achieve bronze-, silver-, or gold-level JTE status for the year by doing the following:

- **Bronze:** Earn at least 525 points by earning points in at least 8 objectives.
- **Silver:** Earn at least 750 points by earning points in at least 8 objectives.
- **Gold:** Earn at least 1,000 points by earning points in at least 8 objectives.

Bronze level indicates satisfactory Scouting, silver indicates excellent Scouting, and gold indicates outstanding Scouting. Essentially, the goals you should aspire to reach at least bronze status.

JTE benchmarks are subject to change each year. As achievement improves across Scouting America, the benchmarks will become more rigorous. You can access the current troop scorecard at www.scouting.org/awards/journey-to-excellence.

---

# SCOUTS BSA TROOP LEADER GUIDEBOOK, VOLUME 2

performance. You also end up with an objective score rather than a subjective opinion.

Here are some ways to use JTE as a framework for planning:

- Review the troop scorecard with your patrol leaders' council and determine what your goal for the next year will be, such as achieving silver status.

- At the annual planning conference, ensure that the PLC schedules the number of outings, courts of honor, and service projects necessary to support the patrol method, and whether they meet the needs of Scouts of various ages and abilities.

- Set numerical goals for benchmarks that aren't related to activities, such as advancement and trained leadership. If some. The benchmarks for increasing youth membership and growth of junior Scout transition are a good example. Without reaching these kinds of goals with your troop, it can be easy to overlook such future problems.

In other cases, JTE can highlight weaknesses masquerading as strengths. Let's say, for example, that your troop regularly reaches gold rank on the long-term camping and long-term outing benchmarks. That might suggest you're planning activities that don't interest members or that those activities are spaced too far apart. It could also mean that you need to enhance your efforts to help new Scouts reach First Class rank in the first 12 to 18 months so they can participate in your program.

Exactly what your JTE results mean depends on your individual troop. Once you've completed your JTE scorecard each year, share the results with the troop leaders' council, the troop committee, and the Scoutmaster corps. Within each group, discuss the results and make concrete plans to do better next year.

## Getting Help With Shortcomings

If your JTE scorecard reveals a weakness, it's important to find resources in those areas. Here are some starting points:

- Talk with your unit commissioner.
- Talk with the leaders of troops in your district or council that excel in the areas where you struggle.
- Seek out training specific to the problem area for your Scoutmaster and assistant Scoutmaster. Your outdoor program, while the Philmont Training Center offers conferences on specific topics like expedition behavior and wilderness skills.
- Research available online and supplemental training courses at www.scouting.org/training/adult.

## A Framework for Planning the Year

Achieving success in JTE requires short-term camping, long-term camping, outing, and other activities. It also requires activities that foster advancement and recognition. Beyond the calendar doesn't include these sorts of activities, you won't qualify for JTE recognition and, more importantly, you won't make progress toward achieving your troop vision.

All the same time, success in JTE depends on achieving goals that don't directly relate to the calendar, such as advancement. Those goals should be part of your annual plan, too.

Here are some ways to use JTE as a framework for planning:

- Review the troop scorecard with your patrol leaders' council and determine what your goal for the next year will be, such as achieving silver status.

- At the annual planning conference, ensure that the PLC schedules the number of outings, courts of honor, and service projects necessary to support the patrol method, and whether they meet the needs of Scouts of various ages and abilities.

- Set numerical goals for benchmarks that aren't related to activities, such as advancement and trained leadership. If some. The benchmarks for increasing youth membership and growth of junior Scout transition are a good example. Without reaching these kinds of goals with your troop, it can be easy to overlook such future problems.

## A Cause for Celebration

When you reach bronze, silver, or gold status, celebrate! Here are some ideas:

- Make a big deal of adding the JTE ribbon to your troop flag.
- Distribute JTE patches to Scouts and leaders.
- Add the appropriate JTE logo to your website.
- Share your achievement with everyone from your troop's leadership, the chartered organization, and the community at large. Remember, however, that reaching bronze, silver, or gold JTE status is about more than achieving a score. It's about creating a troop program that truly accomplishes the aims of Scouting. And that's definitely cause for celebration!

# When in Doubt, STOP!

If you're not sure whether your troop is on track—say you're momentarily lost during an outing. The landscape might have looked familiar, but it really looked beyond where you'd hoped to be by now. Or you realize you're not quite sure where you should head next. At that location. That can happen with troop leadership, too. You look around and realize you're not quite sure where you should head next. As you conduct this exercise, use the troop vision as a guidepost.

The STOP technique can't be lost to STOP: Stop, think, observe, and plan. The same technique works when navigating their way through the challenges of being troop leaders.

**S** – Stop where you are.

**T** – Think. See if you can figure out how your travels have gotten you to the place where you are and what you know about how to get to where you want to go.

**O** – Observe. Pay attention to what is all around you. Take advantage of the ideas of those who are with you; there can be plenty in numbers.

**P** – Plan. Settle on a coherent plan of action to get you to the next place on your journey. Then, even as you carry it forward, recognize that you'll probably need to adjust your plans according to new information and situations.

Even if you are certain you know where you are, there is great value in going through the STOP exercise. It helps you be thorough in developing as a leader and improving your troop.

> Between the JTE scorecard, the troop self-evaluation, and input from your unit commissioner or other Scouters, you should be able to gain a clear understanding of what your troop is doing well and exactly where it could improve. Or you might put information in a sidebar you put into action. We'll help you understand how to discuss ways to evaluate your troop's leadership team—including yourself.
>
> Consider meeting with other local troop leaders, perhaps in conjunction with district roundtable meetings, to share best practices.

# Outside Input

One challenge of the troop assessment is that it can be hard to evaluate your own troop honestly. Having multiple leaders rate the troop has a blind spot? As Robert Burns famously wrote, "And would some Power the smallest gift gie us / To see ourself as ithers see us!"

Your unit commissioner can give you the gift of unbiased feedback. The unit commissioner should be as willing to share feedback as you are willing to accept it. Remember that feedback is a gift.

---

# Evaluating by Walking Around

The JTE scorecard, the troop self-assessment, and commissioner input are useful, but don't discount the value of your own observations. A scoutmaster shouldn't be so busy that he or she can't spend some time observing troop meetings and activities to see how things are going. Boards of review and boards of review are great opportunities to get feedback from troop members.

Just be careful not to confuse anecdotes with evidence. The opinions of one or two Scouts or parents, whether positive or negative, don't necessarily reflect troop-wide reality.

---

# The Troop Down the Street

Another useful, albeit indirect, way to evaluate your troop is to visit another troop in your district or council and observe how they operate—especially in areas where you'd like to make improvements. There are quite a few Scout BSA leaders who've never been to another troop's meeting. By observing how another troop operates, you may pick up tips to improve your own troop operations. You may also discover that things aren't as bad as you thought.

# CHAPTER 3
## Evaluating Your Leadership Team

By definition, leading a Scout troop is a team effort. You can't charter a troop without a Scoutmaster, a troop committee chair, two troop committee members, and a chartered organization representative. But running a troop—really making it successful—can get by with so few leaders.

> A good time to evaluate the troop's leadership team is over the summer, in advance of the new program year.

Once a year, the troop Key 3—the Scoutmaster, committee chair, and chartered organization representative—should conduct a confidential, forward-looking evaluation of the troop's leadership team. One effective way to do that is to do a SWOT analysis. Popular in business circles, SWOT stands for strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats. In the context of your troop, it helps you focus on various leaders:

- **Strengths:** Eagle Scout; Wood Badge-trained; excellent rapport with Scouts
- **Weaknesses:** swing shift job makes regular attendance difficult; works better with adults than Scouts; yet is in an assistant Scoutmaster role; hasn't been trained
- **Opportunities:** plans to attend Powder Horn and could help build high-adventure program; would be a good candidate to move into Scoutmaster role; has a younger family so could commit for the long haul
- **Threats:** child is close to aging out, so may become less involved; health issues make participation on outings difficult; job situation is uncertain—may have to relocate

> If you're not careful, your SWOT analysis can veer into personal considerations. Be discreet, stay positive, and remember that you're evaluating people's roles and contributions to the troop's success. And remember, every other leader (like you) is a volunteer with strengths, weaknesses, and responsibilities beyond Scouting.

Every troop is different, of course, but this evaluation is likely to reveal a few areas where you need to make changes—or where change is coming whether or not you like it. First, you may identify some changes in members' interests, abilities, or current life situations. Second, you may be able to foresee changes you'll need to make in the near future. Third, you may be able to plan ahead so that some adults happily continue in troop roles long after their own Scouts have moved on to college and career. Others prefer to reduce or change their involvement. Developing a plan now for replacing those leaders can prevent a crisis later.

## Terms of Service and Term Limits

When will your time in your current position end? In many troops, the only way to leave a position is to quit, die, or recruit your own replacement.

Other troops take a more thoughtful approach. Rather than allowing leaders to continue indefinitely, they recruit for one year at a time and reassess assignments annually. (Summer is a good time for this.) While most leaders will continue in their current positions, this approach gives individuals—and the troop—a graceful way out.

Some troops go a step further and set term limits, at least on key positions like Scoutmaster. The logic being that fresh energy comes into the position every few years, knowing that a new demanding (if rewarding) assignment may go to the retiring Scoutmaster. Some troops consider naming a Scoutmaster-designate who can shadow the Scoutmaster and seamlessly move into that role when the time comes.

# CHAPTER 4
## Evaluating Yourself

As an adult leader, you play a critical role in the success of your troop. If you are the Scoutmaster or troop committee chair, virtually every aspect of troop operations depends on your leadership. Even if you focus primarily on one Scout's advancement or the new Scout patrol, your impact is more focused but still quite meaningful.

It's important, then, to evaluate yourself and how you are contributing to the troop's success. Self-evaluation is a process that should recur from time to time as your circumstances change.

## A Self-Assessment Exercise

Here are five questions to think about. Take plenty of time thinking about your answers with other adults inside and beyond your troop can help you understand the importance of your answers.

> ### Five Questions About You
> 1. Why did you become a troop leader?
> 2. Why do you want to be a leader now?
> 3. What does Scouting America expect of you?
> 4. What do you expect of Scouting?
> 5. What do you expect of yourself?

### 1. Why did you become a troop leader?

To begin understanding where you are now, think back to the reasons you became a troop leader in the first place, whether that was last year or many years ago when you were one of the original Scouts were here. Here are some possible reasons:

- Perhaps you have a child who wanted to be a Scout, and you signed up so you could spend time together or to help your child have the best possible experience.
- You may have been a Scout yourself as a young person and were eager to see today's young people get to enjoy the same kind of experience.
- You may have wanted to take part in the adventure of Scouting, and becoming involved in a troop offered the challenge and fun. Perhaps you wanted to recapture the feeling of belonging to a group of people doing something interesting and worthwhile together.
- Some people take positions as troop leaders because they were assigned that responsibility or were asked by a group leader or committee chair to fill a vacancy in troop leadership. Some are willing; others are reluctant.

For other people, leading a Scout unit can be a form of service to one's community, to the future of young people, and to the nation.

Still others, much like entrepreneurs in the business community, embrace the challenge of starting something new, developing it, and watching it grow.

### 2. Why do you want to be a leader now?

Look back once again whether your motivation has changed. Which concerns are still relevant? What other reasons keep you involved?

Consider these questions:

- If you became a troop leader because your child joined the troop, what will you do when their time with the troop is completed and they move on?
- Has your interest in the adventure of Scouting increased or waned?
- Are you now enjoying Scouting as much as you did when you accepted your first role as a troop leader? If not, what's different now and what might you do to improve the situation?
- What role does public acknowledgment of your contributions play in your willingness to continue as a troop leader?
- Do your fellow Scouts constitute a large part of your friendship circle or do you feel that Scouting pulls you away from activities that are important to you?
- How satisfied are you with the balance between your time, abilities, and schedule?

Because no one will stay in the same position forever—even those Scoutmasters with 30 years' tenure—also think about these questions:

- Has your troop developed pathways so you can move upward to a position of greater responsibility and satisfaction?
- Is there a succession plan for the time when you will move out of your current leadership position or perhaps leave the troop altogether?

**Scouting should be fun and rewarding for Scouts and adults alike. If you aren't getting something out of the program, it's probably time to think about finding a different role.**

Sharing your thoughts, both as a way to improve your own leadership and your troop's opportunities and as a way to improve district, council, and national efforts to offer the best Scouting program possible.

## 5. What do you expect of yourself?

That's an important question to ask yourself. If you don't expect much of yourself as a troop leader, you probably won't have many expectations for your Scouts either. If, however, you are committed to strengthening your abilities as a leader, adding to your skills, taking your troop members advance, and finding plenty of fun and satisfaction in the process, then Scouting America is there for you.

In chapter 1, we discussed the concept of developing a troop vision—a concept that will be familiar to you if you've attended Wood Badge or sent Scouts to NYLT.

Consider this personal vision as an important factor in developing your vision and Scouting America are there for you.

Consider these examples:

* My vision as Scoutmaster is to create a robust adult leadership structure that includes trained leaders at every position and a clear succession plan.
* My vision is to see all youth members of the Sidewinder Patrol—including my child—become Eagle Scouts.
* My vision is to create an environment in which the adult leaders are nothing but unlock the Scout ten for Thursday nights and drive on weekend outings.
* My vision is outdoor-activities coordinator is for our troop to have enough equipment, financial resources, and adult volunteers to take 50 Scouts on high-adventure youth leaders when they dream big.
* My vision is to create a troop environment that is welcoming to Scouts of all races, races, abilities, and economic backgrounds. I want our troop to be a true team of Scouts committed to service and to each other.

A personal vision can bring meaning and focus to the time you devote to Scouting. It can also clarify what short-term goals you need to set and what you should be doing aside from your week-to-week routine.

## Getting Assessed by Others

Share your assessment of yourself with others who are familiar with your role and responsibilities in Scouting and can provide valuable insights. Ask them what they see as your strengths and areas where you can improve. Pay attention to what they are telling you, but don't discount what they are saying based on their position. Consider feedback to be a gift. It may be that what you discover ways to make the troop's program better as it creates a Thinking through what others tell you is an important way to improve.

Be willing to serve in the same role when the troop's other Scouts need their own assessments and plans for improvement if you've already self-assessed and had those discussions among you. Nothing creates continuity in leadership like knowing that another adult leader in the troop and some of the changes begin with me. Here's what I'm going to do to work on them.

you. These are all "stakeholders"—people who are affected by your success and who are supposed to support you during the course of your self-assessment. Your stakeholders may be fellow unit leaders, parents of Scouts, your district executive, unit and roundtable commissioners, and even your council Scout executive and your local Scouters who have direct knowledge of your leadership.

Based on their feedback from the assessment, you can develop a course of action that will more effectively move you toward your goals.

Here are step-by-step instructions for developing and using a 360-degree assessment:

1. Define the purpose of the assessment. Ideally, the goals need to be SMART—specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-based.

2. Join with a facilitator to help with the assessment process. The assessor should conduct a two 360-degree assessment.

3. Develop an assessment survey that will provide the kind of feedback that will help you move more effectively move toward your goals.

4. Identify the way to ask your stakeholders who can offer you their candid opinions. Provide each with a copy of the survey and a stamped envelope addressed to you as the facilitator. Note that only the facilitator will see the surveys.

5. Finally, make assessment an ongoing part of your Scouting responsibilities and, when appropriate, in other leadership roles. Use it as a tool during any major troop event but rather, a constant tool for gaining a clearer perception of your strengths and the areas where you can improve.

## Tips on Giving Feedback

For feedback to be effective, it should be helpful; otherwise, there is no reason to offer it.

1. Feel all the other people involved are acutely aware of feedback. Listen carefully, then rephrase what they say to be sure you understand them.

2. Deal only with behavior that can be changed.

3. Deal with specifics, not generalities.

4. Describe the behavior; do not evaluate it.

5. Let the person know the impact the behavior had.

6. Use an "I" statement to accept responsibility for your own perceptions and emotions.

7. To make sure the recipients of feedback have understood your message in the way you intended, ask them to rephrase what you said.

You can give caring feedback without a good technique, but the sharing lacks substance. The worst will not help the lack of caring.

## Tips on Receiving Feedback

1. Seek out feedback. It will nearly always provide you with information that, in some ways, will help you understand yourself.

2. Listen carefully. Receiving feedback requires a heightened awareness of yourself and the person offering the feedback.

3. Listen actively. Prepare the feedback in your own words and, if possible, verify that you are receiving is the same as the one the speaker intended to send.

4. Listen unsympathetically. Put feedback in its proper context by observing the speaker's body language, tone of voice, and other nonverbal cues that might help you understand the reasons for offering feedback.

5. Note how you hear the feedback when someone offers criticism or defensive can cloud your ability to listen effectively. Consider feedback to be a gift. It may be.

---

## Pointers on Developing a 360-Degree Assessment Survey

1. The assessment survey should be clear and concise, explaining what stakeholders are taking part in the assessing, how it will be used, and why the facilitator needs their honest answers.

2. Questions must be developed based on the goals you are striving to reach. Feedback should come from the questions on the survey about their perceptions of your progress toward your goals.

3. Feedback comes from what those completing the form actually have observed and can measure. There is nothing to be gained in asking them to assess what they've not observed.

4. Answers to the questions can be simple and straightforward. Offer stakeholders such as the following:

---

## 360-Degree Assessment

Aside from informal evaluations, consider asking for a 360-degree assessment in which feedback comes from all directions. In a business setting, such feedback comes from supervisors, peers, subordinates, and sometimes customers. In a troop, you might ask for feedback from your Scouts, parents, fellow leaders, other adults in your troop, and those who work for or with you.

# CHAPTER 5
## Charting a Course

Once you've evaluated your troop, your leadership team, and yourself, you may be able to identify dozens of things you'd like to change all at once. But trying to do too much too soon can leave you frustrated and your troop confused. Rather than disrupt your current program, select the five to 10 issues that are the most pressing or would have the most positive impact on troop operations. For example, filling a vacant Scoutmaster position would be more important than planning a money-earning project so you could buy a troop trailer would fall farther down the list. Call this list your troop-improvement list.

## Setting Priorities

If you're overwhelmed by the number of issues you need to address, try this technique:

Divide your list of issues into A, B, and C categories.

- **A priorities** are things you must do.
- **B priorities** are things you should do.
- **C priorities** are things that would be nice to do.

Sort your A priorities by importance: A-1, A-2, A-3, etc. Consider your A priorities to be your troop-improvement list.

| Priorities | A (Must accomplish by Scoutmaster and committee) | B (Should accomplish; project to fund trailer) | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | | | |
| 2 | | | |
| 3 | | | |

Keep in mind that priorities can change over time. If your Scoutmaster is stepping down in two years, replacing him or her becomes an A priority immediately. Once you've identified a replacement by the time he or she leaves, you'll need an organized transition process and establish a timeline for addressing it. In some cases, you and your fellow leaders may be able to work on two or more issues at the same time; in other cases, you may need to handle issues one at a time. To use a house analogy, it would make little sense to paint your kitchen if you're planning to tear out walls soon for a major expansion.

Next, use the team-based project planning process described below to set and work toward goals in each area. The result will be a clearer sense of direction, greater satisfaction, and, most importantly, an enhanced ability to achieve the aims of Scouting.

## Five Stages of Team-Based Project Planning

> Wood Badges is a great place to learn more about and practice project planning.

A good way to tackle your troop-improvement list is to have each team leader work with them as a discrete project using the five-stage planning process below:

1. Prepare a project overview that summarizes the specific goals and objectives of the project.

2. Develop a work breakdown structure that identifies the tasks to be completed and the order in which they should be done.

3. Assign responsibilities for specific activities or tasks to each project team member.

4. Put the plan into action and track its progress.

5. Prepare a closeout report that compares the original goals and objectives to the final outcome.

If this process seems overwhelming, look among your leaders or troop parents for someone who works as a project manager in his or her job. He or she might volunteer to serve as the project person—not to do the actual work but to oversee and track progress.

## Stage One: Project Overview

The team's initial planning should be summarized on a one-page document called the project overview. The overview is the "big picture" for the entire project. It should be clearly written—so much so that someone not involved in the team will understand what the project is all about. It should clearly state what the project is and what you hope to achieve. Develop the project overview with the help of the project team.

---

# Project Overview

The project overview should state the reason of the project, what is the team trying to accomplish? How big will the project be? How long will it take? What are the goals and a limited number (three or four) high-level objectives. These goals and objectives will drive the team.

In addition, the overview should state how the team will measure project success. If the team is successful, what will success look like? How can it be measured? The overview should include the types of approach the team will take in completing the project. Finally, the overview should list assumptions or risks in place that might prevent the success of the project.

> ## Project Overview
> 
> **Problem, opportunity, or situation to be changed**
> 
> **Objectives**
> 
> **Measure of success**
> 
> **Approach or method**
> 
> **Assumptions and risks**

## Project Goals and Objectives

Before focusing on the details of a project, it is important to have clear, concise and reliable objectives. These define the project and influence how you measure success. To be sure that goals and objectives are clearly written and achievable, it is important to subject them to the "SMART" test:

- **S**—Specific: Is it specific in targeting an objective?
- **M**—Measurable: What are the measurable indicators of progress or success?
- **A**—Attainable: Is it attainable by someone on the team?
- **R**—Relevant: Does it support the troop's project and overall vision? Is it an appropriate goal within the current scope of the project?
- **T**—Time-based: When will the project be completed?

## Measure of Success

The goals and objectives should be measured to help determine success. The team must choose some measurable criteria. It might be the number of participants, a change in the frequency of a given activity, or a change in behavior. When developing goals and objectives, be sure to include some form of assessment or test. Goals that use qualifying words like "all" or "never" also are acceptable.

## Approach or Method

Before selecting the best possible approach or method, it is important to consider the assumptions and risks to success before making this decision. Often, the approach or method chosen is a direct result of what the team thinks will work best in those roadblocks to success. In addition, the team needs to devise some way of doing this type of project in the most effective.

---

You may sometimes work on a project that has never been done before. This situation may cause the team to consider creating a new approach or method. When members "think outside the box" come up with novel approaches to the situation.

When picking an approach on something they have done before, they tend to use the same approach that worked in the past. But just because something worked for you in the past, does that mean it will work this time? If you have always done it, you will keep on getting what you've always got? Clearly "what we've always got" is not what you are trying to accomplish. This is where it becomes important to consider some alternatives.

The selection of the best possible approach or method may well be the most important decision you make during the early stages of project planning. The approach or method influences everything that follows; therefore, special consideration must be given when choosing the direction.

Here are some guidelines to consider when choosing an approach or method:

- Focus on project goals and high-level objectives.
- Challenge assumptions regarding previously employed approaches and methods.
- Explore out-of-the-box solutions.
- Do not hesitate to invent new methods when appropriate.

## Assumptions or Risks

The team should identify assumptions and risks associated with the objectives and the chosen approach or method. Identify the possible implications and facilitate planning and completion. Assumptions are attitudes or beliefs that are widely held concerning the project, but may not be true and valid. Others may not be true, causing the team to select a wrong plan of action.

Risks are those things that could undermine the success of the project. To help the team identify assumptions or risks, answer the following questions for each objective:

1. What resources are required—realistically—to complete this objective?
2. What risks are associated with obtaining these resources in a timely manner?
3. What problems or delays are likely to occur in completing this objective?
4. What effect will delays have on the overall project plan and schedule?

When you think you have identified all the major reasons. Perhaps the most significant one is that it helps ensure success. In project planning, there are two big traps you seek to avoid by using a project coordinator and using a project manager. One trap is when the team jumps into action and assumes that it can work out what it needs to do after the first task. Such activity often wastes time and resources, to say nothing of the frustration and disappointment team members feel. The other trap is when the scope of the project is not clearly defined and someone says, "Oh, while you're at it, do this, too." Such add-ons can dramatically alter the project, thereby throwing off your schedule and distracting you from achieving your stated goals and objectives. A clearly defined project helps the team determine when add-ons are not a part of the project.

---

# Stage Two: Work Breakdown Structure

The work breakdown structure is used to break all the work into a teams level of detail so that planning can proceed. Break down each objective into separate activities that will be necessary to accomplish the project. Then, put the activities in order—what must be done and when.

The work breakdown structure should have a definite start and stop time. The activities may be linked to one another, and they should all contribute to the achievement of your objective. Every activity should be assignable to a team member and accomplished by that team member. If not, the activity probably should be broken down further.

Doing careful thought to the order of activities can save a great deal of time and sometimes prevent things from getting mixed up. Scheduling activities in random order often makes the project last too long and doesn't utilize the power of the critical path. The longest one of these is sometimes called the "critical path." This is a set of linked activities that the team needs to complete before they can start others, and there is likely to result in delaying the overall project.

## Network of High-Level Objectives

| High-Level Objective 1 | High-Level Objective 2 | High-Level Objective 3 | High-Level Objective 4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activity 1 | Activity 1 | Activity 1 | Activity 1 |
| Activity 2 | Activity 2 | Activity 2 | Activity 2 |
| Activity 3 | Activity 3 | Activity 3 | Activity 3 |

---

# Stage Three: Activity Assignments

We are now on familiar territory—this is the "you do this, and you do that" part of the project. Keep in mind the following guidelines:

- Each team member should gain a clear understanding of the purpose of the project.
- Each member must understand exactly what his or her responsibility is, to the details of each assigned activity, and the availability of resources (including how much time) available to complete the activity is acceptable.
- It is important to match each team member's skills and strengths to the activities—be sure you have the right people for the job.
- Establish milestones or specific activities to be accomplished, as well as regular reporting times, so that the team leader stays informed on the project's progress.

---

# Stage Four: Putting the Plan Into Action

This is the stage where "do" happens. Once the project is in the hands of the team leader, it is important that the team leader or his or her primary role: provide team leadership. There may be times when it is important for the team leader to share information with the rest of his or her committee or project. He or she may need to offer support to team members or offer guidance when problem solving seems to become stalled.

As you can see, project planning progresses through the five stages (described in chapter 4). With each new project, the team may begin differently—they may change its position on the development scale, or the team leader may decide that certain portions of the plan should proceed differently, and then provide additional resources if needed to help ensure timely completion.

---

# Stage Five: Project Closeout

Once the project is complete, there still are two important things to do. One is to recognize your team members. Remember, celebration is an important part of a high-functioning team. The other is to evaluate the project and prepare a final report. This evaluation should be written and include an evaluation of several things:

- Were the goals and objectives met?
- Was the project completed on time?
- How might we make things run better next time?

This written report will provide you with the information you need to overcome obstacles and solve some of the pitfalls you may have encountered.

> ## A Better Future
> 
> Help us succeed like all of our work, that's because it is. Even though we may not do the work, we must address and resolve the serious issues you identified within the troop. Remember, the end result should be a better-organized, better-motivated troop that is ready and willing to deliver on the promise of Scouting.

# EFFECTIVE LEADERSHIP TODAY

Adult leaders in Scouting teach skills, supervise activities, serve as role models, maintain equipment, and drive Scouts to campouts.

Their most important contribution, however, is leadership. But, unlike the leaders in youth activities like sports, Scouters lead through their Scouts. When their work is done, their Scouts will say, "We did this ourselves."

And they'll be right.

Anyone can become an effective leader provided he or she masters basic leadership skills. In this section, we'll introduce some of those techniques. We'll also explore the youth leadership method and introduce advanced skills for making the patrol method work more effectively.
