--- asin: B0BJ7JQ774 extraction_date: '2026-03-19' ocr_backend: claude-haiku-4-5 (2-pass corrected) page_count: 119 source: kindle-cloud-reader-screenshots title: Scouts BSA Program Features Volume 2 --- # SCOUTS BSA # PROGRAM FEATURES ## FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ![Fleur-de-lis logo] # A Guide to Program Planning ## Volume 2 Scouting America Location 1 of 259 • 0% --- # PROGRAM FEATURES FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## A Guide to Program Planning **Volume 2** --- ## Volume 1 - Camping - Climbing and Rappelling - Communication - Cycling - First Aid - Games - Geocaching - Hiking - Outdoor Ethics - Paddle Sports - Pioneering - Rifle Shooting - Safety - Science - Snowboarding and Skiing - Spectator Sports ## Volume 2 - Archery - Backpacking - Citizenship - Engineering - Fishing - Fitness and Nutrition - Living History - Mathematics - Mentoring - Music - Orienteering - Scuba Diving - Soccer - Special Needs and Disabilities - Wilderness Survival - Wildlife Management ## Volume 3 - Caving - Cooking - COPE - Duty to God - Emergency Preparedness - Ethics - Financial Management - Multimedia - Nature and Environment - Project Planning - Shotgun Shooting - Skateboarding - Sustainability - Swimming - Technology - Winter Camping --- ## Key - **Green** = Outdoor program features - **Orange** = Sports program features - **Red** = Health and safety program features - **Blue** = Citizenship and personal development program features - **Teal** = STEM program features - **Purple** = Arts and hobbies program features --- 33711 661800 ISBN 978-0-8395-3111-1 ©2024 Scouting America/Boy Scouts of America 300071302 Location 3 of 259 • 0% In order to view this proof accurately, the Enterprise Preview Option must be set to Always in Acrobat Professional or Adobe Reader. Please contact your Customer Service Representative if you have questions about hiding this option. --- # CONTENTS Introduction-1 Introduction 17-1 Archery 18-1 Backpacking 19-1 Citizenship 20-1 Engineering 21-1 Fishing 22-1 Fitness and Nutrition 23-1 Living History 24-1 Mathematics 25-1 Mentoring 26-1 Music 27-1 Orienteering 28-1 Scuba Diving 29-1 Soccer 30-1 Special Needs and Disabilities 31-1 Wilderness Survival 32-1 Wildlife Management Appendix-1 Appendix Appendix-2 Blank program planning chart Appendix-3 Blank meeting plan Appendix-4 Blank main event plan Appendix-5 Budget Worksheet Appendix-6 Acknowledgments Volume 2 — iii Location 4 of 259 • 1% > In order to view this proof accurately, the Overdrive Preview Option must be set to Always in Adobe Reader. Please contact your Customer Service Representatives if you have questions about hiding this option. --- # PROGRAM FEATURES ## FOR TROOPS AND CREWS As Scouting America's mission statement says, Scouting exists "to prepare young people to make ethical and moral choices over their lifetime by instilling in them the values of the Scout Oath and Scout Law." There is nothing in the mission statement about meetings, outings, or other activities. However, it would be hard to achieve the mission without a solid plan—which requires active program planning. The goal of *Program Features for Troops and Crews* is to make that planning a little easier for you, the unit leader. These three volumes of program features bring together 48 features for use by Scout troops and Venturing crews. That's four full years of suggested programming. While your unit may not use the material here exactly as presented, it offers a launching point for you and your youth members to use in planning a program that will have your Scouts coming back, facilitate advancement and personal growth, and help you achieve Scouting's mission. ## How To Give a Safety Moment In Scouting, a Safety Moment serves as a brief yet important introduction to the topic of safety in activities. It provides a quick tip to help us enjoy our time while staying cautious. During each Scout meeting, one Scout is designated to deliver a Safety Moment. Here are some tips to assist the designated Scout in delivering an engaging and safe topic. **Introductions:** Greet everyone and introduce yourself as the Scout giving the Safety Moment. **Use a Relevant Safety Topic:** Present the Safety Moment provided for the meeting or choose one pertinent to an activity that the unit might be planning to do on an upcoming activity or outing. When possible, focus on preventing injury. Remember to make it brief! **Explain the Safety Tips:** Use clear and simple language to explain the safety tip. For example, when using a pocketknife, always cut away from yourself. Use any props or visual aids to help people understand. **Encourage Questions:** Invite questions from the group to ensure everyone understands the safety tip. **Conclusion:** End by thanking everyone for listening and reminding them to stay safe. ## Annual Program Planning In Scouting, planning is a two-phase process. - **Long-term planning** results in an annual calendar and a set of unit goals for the year. - **Short-term planning** yields detailed plans for one month's meetings and outings—and sometimes a little more. ### The Annual Planning Conference Long-term planning happens at the annual planning conference, typically held in late spring or early summer, as soon as possible after school, community, and council calendars have been published. A planning conference should include the committee chair, unit leader, and senior patrol leader or crew president should perform the following steps. **Step 1—Gather the necessary information:** - Unit and family events, like holidays, homecoming, and exams - Community event dates, including those the unit might want to participate in and dates you should avoid - The chartered organization's key dates, again considering opportunities to collaborate as well as potential conflicts - Personal dates, such as family vacations, that may affect the unit's activities - Key district and council dates - Data collected from the troop resource survey or activity interest survey - Last year's annual plan. If you have one or use visual aids to help everyone understand. - Unit priorities and goals - Advancement records for each member - A general outline of next year's program **Step 2—Discuss the planning process with your top youth leader,** explaining the importance of this process and his or her role in it. Discuss options for unit meetings and outings. Be flexible and creative at this point. You should both review the program planning conference guide, available at www.scouting.org/programs/scouts-bsa for troops and www.scouting.org/programs/venturing for crews, to be on the same page with the agenda and conference setup. ### Setting Goals What sorts of unit goals should the draft plan include? Here are some possibilities. - Attend summer camp - Have an outdoor adventure at least once a month - Strengthen relations with the chartered organization by providing a service project to benefit the organization or increasing the unit's presence, such as on Scout Sunday or Scout Sabbath - Earn the National Camping Award and a gold Journey to Embrace rating - Conduct a fundraiser to help pay for expenses such as new tents and camping gear - Plan and conduct a service project that helps the National Honor Patrol Award at least once - Plan an outdoor skills weekend to learn new skills **Step 3—Have the top youth leader share the draft plan** with other youth leaders, who then share it with other members to get their input and ideas. Based on feedback from the youth members, evaluate the current year's plan. A useful tool is the "start, stop, continue" method, which is based on three simple questions: - What should we start doing that we are not currently doing? - What should we stop doing that is not working? - What should we continue doing that is working well and helping us succeed? Keep last year's annual plan and rent year's calendar available for review, as well as the draft plan. They should use these materials, however. They might, for example, see a need for a theme or focus that did not appear in last year's plan and want to add opportunities into the calendar for patrol outings or social events—perhaps trips they did not think of earlier. **Step 4—Invite the following people to attend the conference to brainstorm the efficiency of the following:** - The unit's youth leaders - Unit committee members and other adult leaders - The chartered organization representative - The unit commissioner (optional) - Anyone else who might be helpful, such as parents A Scribe should be assigned to take notes in case you play very different roles at the conference. A Scribe should be assigned to take notes so you can reference back later, in case planning takes a while, ensuring that all members are contributing. ### Holding the Annual Planning Conference The annual planning conference can be held at any regular meeting place or there is a lot of room. For example, a parish hall, school gym, or community center. If you have access to a cabin or retreat center, you might consider hosting the conference into an overnight experience at your building. If the conference follows closely after unit until the introductions to Leadership Skills for Troops and Troops Introduction to Leadership Skills for Troops continuum. Adult adults should not play active roles in the planning conference. Instead, they should give their input ahead of time to the unit leader for program planning, and then allow the youth to lead them in charge of cooking and cleanup, and allow the youth leaders to focus on the task at hand. Besides the materials described above, you might prepare one item each month that consists of a calendar of activities and a list of products that will work, including wall planners with reusable stickers, allowing dates in the draft plan, as well as other important district, council, community, school, and organization dates. Another useful tool is the program planning chart, a worksheet that helps you record the conference's accomplishment. Use this chart every month and that count of honor and service projects are evenly spread throughout the year. --- Volume 2 | Introduction | Introduction-1 --- Introduction-2 | Introduction | Volume 2 Location 6 of 259 • 1% --- # The Annual Planning Conference, Step by Step The purpose of the conference should be an annual calendar; a list of monthly program features (e.g., hiking, trips, service projects, outdoor skills), and a set of unit goals. The amount of detail in the plan can vary. For some months, the group will decide on a specific activity, meeting place, and outing. For other months, the group may choose either the destination or the outing's focus. Ideally, the calendar should include a mix of familiar and unfamiliar activities and destinations. A few fixed outings each year add fun; more a few can make the program feel repetitive. Also, include some flexibility to allow for occasional tweaks, such as doing a favorite activity at a new location. While discussing ideas at the conference, use these ground rules: • It is important to respect one another's views. Listen and don't interrupt. • Keep focused on the task to plan the unit's annual program. Don't get sidetracked. • Write out ideas so everyone can see them. • Be in agreement. Don't let an issue go unresolved; if members of the unit can't agree on a destination or program theme, write it on a flip chart or board and take a vote. Designate someone to record all this on a master calendar, and take good notes! ## The Annual Planning Conference, Step by Step **Step 1—** The unit leader leads a discussion on the unit's goals for the coming year. Write the goals on a flip chart or easel board, and agree to that set of goals. **Step 2—** Share with the rest of the meeting attendees the printed draft of the calendar that shows the dates of all planned program features. Post poster-size calendars around the room and events planned. Ask if anyone has other dates and events to add. **Step 3—** Take a few minutes to discuss these items and dates so you both comfortable with them. **Step 4—** The troop youth leader shares updates from members about what they want to do. This exercise can be the most challenging at the program planning conference, so take as much time as needed. Let the youth express what activities they would like as a foundation for the group's desired programs or themes. You might take it one month at a time. Don't let an issue go unresolved. If members of the unit can't agree on a destination or program theme, write it on a flip chart or board and take a vote. Designate someone to record all this on a master calendar, and take good notes! **Step 5—Add other important dates such as:** • Unit meetings and leadership meetings • Boards of review • Courts of honor • Elections • Open house • Service projects • Arrow of Light Scout joint outings and transition ceremonies • Any other activities that can be scheduled this far in advance (for example, fundraising events) **Step 6—Hold a final discussion on the plan, calendar, and goals, and then take a vote.** If the group approves the plan and calendar in the annual plan, it will go to the unit committee for final approval. Work with the chartered organization representative to make any facilities reservations at the chartered organization to secure dates for events by making campsite reservations for the dates and locations selected. **Step 7—To make this plan a truly valuable tool, it must be shared with each unit family, the chartered organization, and your community partners. The unit must follow the plan, share it with everyone, and review it regularly to see if modifications are needed.** --- # After the Planning Conference Prepare the calendar in an easy-to-use format for distribution. Here are some features to consider: • Include as much detail as possible, including meeting start and end times. • Indicate when regular meetings won't be held or will be held at different times. • Include a list of items that members could bring to meetings—such as food for a pie, you might use blue for unit meetings and outings, green for activities that don't fall within regular meeting times (such as patrol leaders' council, unit committee, courts of review), red for special events (with color coding use black for external events (such as national Scout jamborees, roundtables, and troop courses). • Add a revision date at the bottom of every page. If the calendar must change after printing, a stamped date can indicate when the change was made. Distribute copies of the final plan to youth members and their families, adult leaders, members of the unit committee, and representatives of the chartered organization. Be sure to include copies for your district executive, the district executive's assistant, and the chartered organization's leader, secretary, and facilities manager. Also be sure to post the calendar on the unit website and at the meeting place. --- # Monthly Program Planning No matter how comprehensive and well-planned your annual calendar is, you'll still need to plan what to do in detail for each month's outing and meetings. If the group is on the ball, it actually looks at these months of granularity each time. • Briefly review the month that's just ending (using the "start, stop, continue" mode. • Plan in detail everything that will do in the month that is just beginning for self-scout begins. • Briefly preview the following month and plan. As with the annual plan, be sure to communicate the results of each monthly planning meeting with all members, leaders, and other stakeholders—especially if there are needs to the annual plan. --- # How to Use the Program Features Each program feature in this book provides a month's worth of program ideas for Scout troops and Venturing crews. You will find here: • General information about the topic • Related advancement requirements and awards • Games related to the topic (or that are just fun) • Ideas for using the EDGE method (Explain, Demonstrate, Guide, Enable) to teach topics • Four weeks' worth of meeting plans • Outings or overnight trip ideas (bringing that skill to real-world situations) • Print and online resources related to the topic, including a list of related program features You will find these icons in each program feature: 🟢 **Essential (Tier I):** Basic skills-development information and beginner level activities 🔵 **Challenging (Tier II):** Skills-development information and activities for those with some experience ◆ **Advanced (Tier III):** Learning topics and activities for those who are ready for the ultimate challenge --- # A NOTE TO VENTURERS For the purpose of earning the Discovery and Pathfinder awards, the main event examples used in program features are "the main focus of months of programming that follows." **Essential (Tier I): Beginner level activities** Including skills training, planning; events often last less than a full day (not overnight); activities can be beyond the comfort zone for most participants. **Challenging (Tier II): Intermediate level activities** Requiring moderate preparation; events typically last less than four days; some prior skill development may be desirable or required; activities extend beyond the allocated time for most participants. **Advanced (Tier III): Higher level activities** Engaging more preparation; events will last four days or longer and be physically challenging, requiring skills development prior to participation; such adventures are highlights of the program year and may take place once or twice annually. --- Location 8 of 259 • 2% Volume 2 Introduction Introduction–3 --- # SCOUTS BSA PROGRAM FEATURES VOLUME 2 FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## CAMPING ### Meeting Plan: Campsites **Week 1 Date_____** | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | Preparation (5 minutes before meeting) | Describe special checks of tents, cards taking various items of camping gear, one item per card. Have small groups sort the cards in order of importance, setting aside items that might be forgotten. | | | | Opening Ceremony (12 minutes) | Flag presentation
Oath and Law
Uniform inspection
Safety Moment | | 7:00 p.m. | | Group Instruction (15 minutes) | Introduce campsites using a diagram of model campsites as point of reference.
• Youth leaders show how to set up a dining fly. | | 7:12 p.m. | | Skills Instruction (20 minutes) | • Learn to tie two half-hitches and a taut-line hitch.
• Learn how to tie a clove hitch over an open-ended object.
• Review the above skills.
• Learn how to join two poles with two round lashings.
• Using the above skills, erect a dining fly as quickly and efficiently as possible. | | 7:30 p.m. | | Breakout Groups (15 minutes) | • Discuss plans for the main event.
• Review what personal and group equipment will be needed. | | 7:50 p.m. | | Game (10 minutes) | Play Sloppy Camp, Taut-line Hitch Race, or Dining Fly Race (described earlier). | | 8:10 p.m. | | Closing (5 minutes) | Announcements
Leader's minute
Closing | | 8:25 p.m. | | | **Total 90 minutes of meeting** | | | | After the Meeting (15 minutes) | Leadership team reviews plans for the next meeting and for the main event. | | | *All times are suggested. --- Location 10 of 259 • 3% In order to view this proof accurately, the Unstyled Preview Option must be set to Always in Adobe Professional or Adobe Reader. Please contact your Customer Service Representative if you have questions about hiding this option. --- # Parts of a Program Feature 1. A thorough description of the meeting content 2. A breakdown of the parts of the meeting with suggested times 3. Note who is in charge of each segment of the meeting 4. Write down the actual times for each segment of the meeting 5. A fun activity for members to do as they are gathering 6. Start the meeting right with a formal opening ceremony 7. A quick or not so quick introduction to the topic for all members 8. Specific instruction for all three skill levels; note the colored icons 9. Time for patrols or other small groups to practice skills and prepare for the main event 10. A game that tests what members have learned or just lets them blow off steam 11. Take care of business at the end of the meeting 12. Get ready for the next meeting and the main event --- # CAMPING ## Main Event: Overnight Car Camp **Date:______________________** ### Logistics | Field | Details | |-------|---------| | Location: | | | Departure time: | | | Return time: | | | Duration of activity: | Overnight | | Budget: Completed _____ Approved _____ | | | Camping: Duty roster _____ Menu _____ | | | Transportation: Group _____ Staff _____ | | ### Essential (Tier I) > Find a campsite that you can drive to and that offers plenty of opportunities for outdoor adventures. Camp overnight and have your Scouts cook meals. ### Equipment List - Camping gear (individual and group) - Backpacks, canoes, or bicycles to get the group to and from the campsite (optional) - Tents - Sleeping bags - Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Activity - Establish a safe campsite following Leave No Trace principles. - Plan camping duty roster and meal plan. - Prepare a scheduled campfire with plenty of time for setting up camp the right way, including establishing defined sleeping, social, and cooking areas. - Practice camping skills Scouts need to work on (the building, cooking, knots, etc.). - Plan activities for the evening and next morning (games, skits, songs, etc.). - On departure, be sure you are leaving no trace of your presence. ### Safety - Review the buddy system and all appropriate safety systems; cell phones are a good idea as appropriate. Have a first-aid kit handy. - Two-deep adult leadership is required for all activities. ### Notes > For an added challenge, plan for all Scouts to reach camp under their own power—by hiking, cycling, or canoeing. If possible, have them carry their own gear. All could travel by the same means, or different groups could choose different options. --- # Parts of a Main Event Plan 1. A quick description of the main event 2. Basic information such as times and transportation plans 3. A quick reference to the event's ability level (essential, challenging, advanced) and more in-depth description of the main event 4. Key equipment you will need to take, including specialized gear for the type of event 5. A thumbnail planning worksheet 6. Safety concerns you should keep in mind 7. Special notes about the activity, including variations you could consider --- Volume 2 — Introduction — Introduction–9 Location 14 of 259 • 4% --- # ARCHERY ## On Target Movies like *The Hunger Games*, *The Avengers*, and *Brave* have brought a renewed interest in archery. As a result, archery has become one of the fastest growing sports in the United States. So what does it take to be a great archer? Have you ever given thought to the many different types of archery shooting that are available? Target shooting at summer camp is just the beginning. Have you ever wanted to try archery but didn't know where to start? This program feature will describe the equipment and techniques used in archery and will help you understand what it takes to become a proficient archer. As you become more accomplished, there will be several ways to progress in the sport. First, you will need to learn how to handle a bow and arrow safely. Then, you will need to work on various fundamentals so you become a skilled archer. But even if you are just a beginner, archery is a whole lot of fun. You can get started right now with the Archery program feature. ### Objectives This month's activities should: - Teach Scouts how to handle bows and arrows safely - Explain the different types of bows and how to take care of each type - Teach Scouts about the parts of different bows and arrows - Instruct Scouts in the fundamentals of archery as they apply to the bow that is being used - Introduce Scouts to the various styles of archery that are available to create a lifelong hobby ### RELATED ADVANCEMENT AND AWARDS - Archery merit badge - Ranger: Shooting Sports elective - Venturing Shooting Sports Outstanding Leadership Shooting Sports Outstanding Advancement Award - USA Archery Junior Olympic Archery Development Awards --- ## Leadership Planning As a leadership team, you may want to discuss the following items at planning meetings when choosing archery as your program feature: 1. What is our unit's current skill level? 2. Who do we know who is an archery instructor? 3. What materials and equipment (bows, arrows, quivers, etc.) do we need, and what are Scouting America's rules for handling these? 4. Do we have a copy of Scouting America's current National Range and Target Activities safety rules? 5. What location is needed for meetings—our normal meeting place or a shooting range? 6. Is there any cost factor involved? 7. What will we do for our main event, and where will we go? 8. To meet our needs, what should we change in the sample meeting plans? ### LEADERSHIP All archery instruction and archery range management for the Archery merit badge and all Archery must be conducted by trained, qualified on-site range masters who are certified in archery range safety instruction. To qualify as an archery range master, the range master must be at least 18 years old. > **PARENTS CAN HELP WITH THE ARCHERY PROGRAM FEATURE BY:** > 1. Sharing their expertise > 2. Providing transportation and supervision for the main event > 3. Providing materials for the different games and activities > 4. Mentoring Scouts as they work on advancements > 5. Maintaining current Youth Protection certification, which is required of all adults providing transportation for or participating in outings. --- ## THE HISTORY OF ARCHERY The advancement of civilization was enhanced by the use and discovery of bows and arrows, which provided a much safer way to hunt. Spearheads have been discovered from many thousands of years ago. A recognizable bow, made of wood, horn, and sinew, dates back to around 2800 B.C. Egyptians are considered the first to use the bow in battle, sometime around 3500 B.C. to 2800 B.C. This bow, known as the composite bow, gave them superiority over their enemies. Around 1500 B.C., Assyrians developed a shorter recurve bow that provided more power and easier handling. The English longbow was one of the deadliest weapons of the medieval period. The English gave archery another boost by putting archers on chariots. This mobility allowed the Egyptians to defeat the Hittite army. Crossbows were invented around 500 B.C. to 800 B.C. A new application for the crossbow was developed—a device for shooting backward while riding a horse. The term "Parthian shot" is still used today in archery. Around A.D. 1000, bow and arrow technology spread throughout America. Archaeological studies indicate its use was adopted by most prehistoric American Indians. Over time the wooden bow was made larger. The British were famous for employing long on the bow and their version eventually became known as the longbow. The most famous battle of the longbow was the battle of Crécy in 1346. In 1500, to meet the demand for the longbow, crossbows were introduced and later replaced all bows with muskets. However, archery has remained a popular sport activity in England. In 1808, the first archery club in the United States, the United Bowmen of Philadelphia, was founded. The National Archery Association was established in 1879, and the first national tournament was held in White Stocking Park in Chicago the same year. In 1900, archery became part of the Olympic Games. It was dropped after 1920 because the wide range of rules could not be standardized. In 1931, archery became a National Camping School–trained shooting sports director or a USA Archery/NFAA instructor. --- # GENERAL ARCHERY SAFETY RULES • Keep all arrows in their quivers until ready to shoot. • The safest way to transport an arrow safely is in its quiver. If you must carry arrows in your hands, hold them securely with both hands around all of the arrows and with your palms facing down. • Be sure the area around and beyond your target is clear before you shoot. Never draw a bow if anyone is in front of the shooting line. • Always aim and shoot at a definite target; never point a bow at anyone. Be absolutely sure of your target and that it is safe to shoot at it. If you are not sure, take a closer look. If, after a closer look, you are still not sure, do not shoot. • Always have an arrow on the string when shooting a bow at a target. Dry firing (shooting a bow without an arrow) can seriously damage a bow and possibly injure the archer. Never dry fire a bow. • Always use proper safety equipment, including an arm guard and a finger tab or glove. A bow sling is not required but is recommended. • Always inspect your equipment before shooting. Repair or replace damaged equipment. Replace the bowstring when it becomes worn. • Never shoot an arrow straight up in the air. Do not shoot if there is any chance your arrow might ricochet from (bounce off) the target or another object and hit someone. • Use arrows that are the proper length for you. Arrows that are too short can cause serious injury. • Never shoot an arrow up into the air. • Walk, do not run, on the archery range. If you run, you might accidentally cross in front of another archer's shooting line, trip on the ground, or fall and trip into a target and be injured by the arrows sticking out of it. • When retrieving arrows from behind a target, particularly on a field range or at an isolated target, get, lean your bow against the face of the target or stick an arrow in the top of the target with the bow attached to it, rather than let other archers that you are behind the target. # TYPES OF BOWS Archers have several types of bows from which to choose. ## A compound bow has a system of strings and cables connected to cams (pulleys) of various designs. When the bowstring is drawn back, the cams multiply the force the archer exerts on the bowstring and limbs, storing more energy. When a compound bow is shot, the arrow is aided by energy stored in the limbs, and it releases the arrow with much more "compounded" energy—hence the name. Most modern compound bows have a let-off at full draw and use sights to aid in aiming the bow. [DIAGRAM: Detailed labeled diagram of a compound bow showing: UPPER LIMB, CABLE GUIDE SLIDE, RISER, ARROW REST, CAM, LOWER LIMB, SHOOTING HAND, BOWSTRING, and other components] ## Most traditional bows have no sights and are shot instinctively. There is less stored energy in a traditional bow than in a compound bow. There are several types of traditional bows, with the recurve and the longbow being the most common. Longbows are quite long and can have draw limits for 2 inches or more. When a longbow is strung, the string will be approximately straight and the archer must hold it on the tips of the bow. There are several other types of traditional bows, including fiberglass. ## A crossbow is most often shot like a rifle. There is a stock that holds the bow horizontally rather than how the bow is cocked. It also holds the string back. The "prod" is attached to the front of the stock and is where the bow fires the bolt. There is a trigger called "twitts"—that are much shorter and stronger than regular arrows. The bolt is placed on the... [DIAGRAM: Labeled diagram of a crossbow showing: NOCK, NECK FLETCHING, FLETCHING, SHAFT, and PROD] --- # COMPETITION TYPES Archery offers different ways for people to get involved in competitions. Depending on where you live and the climate in your area, you might find indoor or outdoor competitions, or both. There are times of year where there are more competitions for each type that some archers might slightly to allow continuous archery competitions. **3-D archery** competition is an indoor or outdoor scenario that may be held in a gym or outdoors in a permanent facility set up around a course shooting at molded-foam replicas of different game animals. Some facilities also available (including dinosaurs and even Bigfoot). The season for 3-D archery is year-round. **The indoor archery season** lasts from late October through March. You aim at a multi-colored target face that is only a few meters away or at one target at 20 yards. **In safari archery**, you use a 3-D target or paper animal targets, aiming at bright orange dots that designate where you should hit. There are novelty targets for the children, too. The safari archery is short on a moving course. The season generally runs from February through May. **Field archery** competition includes three parts, all on a roving course. The "field round" is shot on a black-and-white ringed target with black in the center. The "hunter round" is shot on an all-black and-white ringed target with only one ring in the center. You will also shoot paper animal targets with a white dot where you are supposed to hit. The season generally runs from March through July. **Target archery** is the most familiar competition, and the type seen in the Olympics. Target archery involves shooting at multi-colored targets in an open field. The targets are at set distances, typically 20, 30, or 40 yards. For each competition type, there are different categories depending on equipment, age, and gender. --- Volume 2 | Archery | 17–4 --- Location 18 of 259 • 6% --- # ARCHERY GAMES ## Parts of a Bow Relay **Equipment:** Several bows of different types, blindfolds **How to play:** Before the game starts, blindfold all players and scatter them around the room. While in relay formation, one adult leader stands in front of the room holding the bows. On a signal, the first player on each team walks forward to an adult with the team leader serving as a guide. The player tries to identify the part of the bow where the adult has placed their hand. They then return to tag the next player. Continue until all players have had a turn. **Scoring:** Players score 1 point for identifying the type of bow and 1 point for identifying the specific part. **Variation:** Set a timer and let players continue competing, taking multiple turns, until time expires. ## Shooting Steps Shuffle **Equipment:** For each team, prepare two sets of shuffle cards, one set shows the 10 shooting steps (stance, nock, set, predraw, draw, anchor, transfer/hold, aim, release, follow-through), and one set of definitions mixed from the previous set. Create two sets of cards per team. **How to play:** Shuffle each team's cards (20 total). On a signal, each team must put its cards in order and match each step with its definition. **Scoring:** The first team to get the cards sorted correctly wins. **Variations:** 1) Add a relay element where one player on a team runs forward to retrieve a card. 2) Play reverse cards by placing cards face down on the floor in a scattered array and then begin the sorting process. ## Granny's Footsteps **Equipment:** None **How to play:** Assemble all players in a single line. To start the game, a leader acts as "Granny." Granny stands 20 yards in front, with her/his back to the group. Players must sneak up on Granny without being seen. Granny counts to themselves from 1 to any number up to 30. At any point they wish, they turn around and any person seen moving is sent back to the starting point. A Scout caught three times is out of the game. **Scoring:** The first Scout to touch Granny wins. **Variation:** Rather than move toward Granny, the players simply move around the room. Anyone caught moving when Granny turns around is eliminated. Continue until only one player is left. ## The Maze **Equipment:** None **How to play:** Each team forms a circle. Next, each member reaches across with their right hand and takes someone else's right hand. Each member then holds someone else's hand, but not the person next to them. On a signal, two players (appointed before the game) let go of their right hands only. No one else may let go. These two "loose ends" will attempt to straighten out the mass of hands into a line. --- # E.D.G.E. Ideas > **Explain** how it is done—Tell them. > **Demonstrate** the steps—Show them. > **Guide** learners as they perform—Watch them do it. > **Enable** them to succeed on their own—Have them practice/teach it. ## EXPLAIN - Explain the rules of archery safety. - Tell about local and state laws for owning and using archery tackle. - Describe the parts of different bows. - Explain the different arrow types. - Describe how to safely clean and store a bow. ## DEMONSTRATE - Demonstrate the fundamentals of shooting. - Show the different parts of a bow. - Demonstrate how to use a cord stringer. - Display the different arrow types and show how to determine which is right for different bows. - Give a presentation about safety cleaning and storing a bow. ## GUIDE - Help Scouts determine their dominant eye. - Have Scouts point out and describe the different parts of the bow and how they work. - As a coach/pupil team, string a bow using a cord stringer. - Have Scouts demonstrate proper archery technique while you watch. - Guide Scouts as they clean bows and explain how to properly store them. ## ENABLE - Have Scouts use the EDGE method to teach archery methodology to a friend. - Create coach/pupil teams where Scouts can help each other improve their shooting technique. - Have Scouts (under supervision) prepare an archery range for use. - Hold an open shoot where Scouts can practice the skills they have learned. - Let Scouts make their own arrows under the guidance of experienced archers. --- # MAIN EVENT SUMMARIES | | ESSENTIAL | CHALLENGING | ADVANCED | |---|---|---|---| | **Day Activity** | Day of archery range shooting— Visit an archery range and learn the fundamentals of shooting. | Overnight Activity Archery tournament— Participate in a local archery tournament or plan and organize one for your patrol. Please contact your Customer Service Representative if you have questions about hiding this option.* --- # BACKPACKING ## The World at Your Feet Many people today never stray far from a warm bed, a cozy bathroom, and a well-stocked refrigerator. While they enjoy all the comforts of home, they miss out on the wonders of nature, the fun of exploring new places, and the environment that comes with accomplishing more than they ever thought they could. When you go backpacking, you carry all the essentials of home (if not all the comforts of it) on your back. You might hike for miles along a remote mountain ridge, spend a night in a secluded valley, have lunch along a winding trail, and enjoy dinner by starlight on a mountaintop. Then you can bed down under the stars for a well-earned rest before beginning a new day of adventure. Backpacking can be a single-day activity of several miles in a local park or a weeklong trek of 50 miles or longer at Philmont Scout Ranch. No matter what the distance, you will need to be prepared to meet the challenge, and you will need to follow the principles of Leave No Trace so that those who follow you can enjoy the same trails for decades to come. ### Objectives This month's activities should: - Improve physical fitness. - Develop practical skills in living with nature and God. - Foster a greater appreciation for the outdoors and a determination to follow the Outdoor Code and the principles of Leave No Trace. - Offer opportunities to practice planning and teamwork. - Strengthen self-confidence and team building. ### RELATED ADVANCEMENT AND AWARDS - Scout requirement 1e - Tenderfoot requirements 1a, 1b, 5a, 5b, and 5c - Second Class requirements 1a, 1b, 3a, and 3b - First Class requirements 1a, 1b, and 1d - Backpacking and Hiking merit badges - 50-Mile Award - Outdoor Ethics awards - National Medal for Outdoor Achievement - National Outdoor Challenge Unit Award - Historic Trails Award --- # Leadership Planning As a leadership team, you may want to discuss the following items during your planning meetings when choosing backpacking as your program feature: 1. What will our main event be? 2. Where will we have our main event? 3. Do we have appropriate crew gear (e.g., stoves, tents)? 4. Do our Scouts have the appropriate personal gear (e.g., backpacks, boots)? 5. Who in the unit can teach backpacking skills? 6. Where could we find guest speakers who have worked at a high-adventure base or hiked trails like the Appalachian Trail and Pacific Crest Trail? 7. How proficient are our Scouts and leaders in backpacking skills? 8. Are we ready to tackle a trek at a high-adventure base operated by Scouting America or a local council? 9. How can we involve parents? 10. What parts of the Backpacking merit badge will we focus on? 11. How can we use backpacking to promote "team building"? 12. To meet our needs, what should we change in the sample meeting plans? 4. Supporting money-earning projects to raise funds for equipment 5. Maintaining current Youth Protection certification, which is required of all adults providing transportation or participating in outings. ## MAKING BACKPACKING FUN Scouts who are new to backpacking can find it boring. Scouts who are less physically fit may view it as little more than hard work in disguise. Fortunately, there are plenty of things you can do to make backpacking fun. Here are a few: - Hike to interesting destinations: a hidden waterfall, a scenic overlook, a historic site, or a spot to do some bouldering or horseback riding. - Plan activities for evenings at camp. Take along cards, a flying disc, and other game equipment. Conduct games with other activities. Play "kick the can" along the trail and have Scouts study the flora and fauna in a 3-foot square area. Practice splicing at trees. Use techniques from the Scouts BSA handbooks to measure the height of or distance to distant trees. - Don't just walk. Take a 10-minute water break every hour, ideally in an attractive location—never at the bottom of a big hill! ## PARENTS CAN HELP WITH THE BACKPACKING PROGRAM FEATURE BY: 1. Sharing their expertise 2. Providing transportation for the group 3. Loaning backpacking equipment to the group --- # Job Name: 48887764 ## BUDGET BACKPACKING EQUIPMENT Although it is possible to spend hundreds of dollars on equipment, including backpacks, sleeping bags, and tents, backpacking gear does not have to be expensive. Some outdoor stores rent backpacks and other gear, and many Scouting units collect donated gear that Scouts can use. That is especially helpful with backpacks, which can run anywhere from $100 to $300 or more. Scouts also don't need the latest, greatest gear. Rather than buying an expensive zero-degrees sleeping bag, you can add a fleece blanket to a less expensive bag rated to 20 degrees. Rather than purchasing a specialized backpack, use a day pack or a zipper bag from the kitchen. If you are hiking on easy terrain, you can probably make do with good athletic shoes rather than hiking boots. • Practice techniques that help reduce fatigue. When climbing a big hill, try the caterpillar technique. When the leader needs a break, they step to the side of the trail and let the others pass. Everyone else does the same thing after a few minutes, and so on. Eventually, the original leader, now rested, will end up in front again. Repeat as needed. • As you hike, play the sorts of games you might see on a long car ride, such as Twenty Questions. --- # Job Name: 48887764 ## The Principles of Leave No Trace Backcountry visitors have a responsibility to either guard or protect the land. Incorporate Leave No Trace principles into your activities. ### 1. Plan Ahead and Prepare. Comply with area regulations such as observing limitations on group size. Camp only in designated areas or areas of high use. Obtain all necessary permits. ### 2. Travel and Camp on Durable Surfaces. In high-use areas, concentrate your activities where vegetation is already absent. In less-traveled areas, spread out to avoid creating trails that cause erosion. Always choose the most durable surfaces available: rock, gravel, sand, and bare soil. ### 3. Dispose of Waste Properly. Carry out of the backcountry all trash, leftover food, and litter. Use biodegradable soap and properly dispose of dishwater at least 200 feet distant from water sources. Never bury trash or leave feces on the surface. Help prevent the spread of disease. Catholes 6 to 8 inches deep in humus and 200 feet from water are the most sanitary and often the most practical way to dispose of feces. ### 4. Leave What You Find. Preserve the sense of discovery, and preserve the past. Leave rocks, shells, plants, and other objects as you find them. Avoid introducing or transporting nonnative species. Do not disturb cultural or historical structures and artifacts. ### 5. Minimize Campfire Impacts. Many natural areas have been degraded by overuse of fires and the increasing demand for firewood. Lightweight camp stoves are ideal for cooking, so use wood, and make cleanup easier. After dinner, enjoy a candle lantern instead of a fire. ### 6. Respect Wildlife. Practice these safety methods: • Observe wildlife from afar to avoid disturbing animals, especially during sensitive birthing seasons. Quick movements and loud noises are stressful to wildlife. You are too large and quick to be entertaining to them. • Store food securely and keep garbage and food scraps away from animals. Never feed wildlife. ### 7. Be Considerate of Other Visitors. Protect the quality of their experience by respecting their privacy and following these practices: • Select campsites away from other groups. • Keep your noise level down. Leave pets and all music players at home. • Make sure the colors of clothing and gear blend with the environment. • Yield right-of-way properly and leave gates (open or closed) as found. > Blisters develop when skin is irritated by friction or heat and are often caused by footwear that does not fit properly or by wet feet. > > • Ensure that your footwear fits well and has been adequately broken in. > > • Change into dry socks wherever your feet become damp. > > • Wear two pairs of socks—a thin, close-fitting liner sock made of a synthetic material under a thicker wool or synthetic sock. > > If you notice a hot spot—the signal that a blister is starting to form—treat it immediately. Apply a gel or liquid bandage to the spot, place a piece of moleskin with a hole in the center, and fit it around the affected area. These steps will help reduce direct pressure and protect the blister from continued rubbing. Change bandages once or twice daily or as needed. --- The member-driven Leave No Trace Center for Outdoor Ethics teaches people how to enjoy the outdoors responsibly. One player from each team dribbles the ball around a course, then passes it off to the next player. The first team to finish the course without losing the ball wins. (This game is best played outside but could be done indoors—in a gym, for example.) ### Throw-In for Accuracy **Equipment:** One soccer ball per team, stopwatch or timer **How to play:** Divide into two teams. Establish a sideline where one team member will stand to throw the ball in. The next-handed overhead throw. Another player stands in the target area trying to catch the ball. If teams have varying skill levels, you can position the head-butting players closer to or farther from the sideline. **Scoring:** Each team scores 1 point for every ball that is caught successfully. ### Everybody Up **Equipment:** None **How to play:** This initiative exercise is a useful way to emphasize the idea of group cooperation. Ask two Scouts of about the same size to sit facing each other on the ground or floor with the soles of their feet touching, their knees bent, and tightly grasping each other's hands. From this position, they must try to stand up together without letting go of each other's hands. If they succeed, ask another Scout to join them and try standing with three people. Then add one more, then more, until you have the hands of another while maintaining foot contact with the rest of the group. Thinking is required to come up with ways to get everybody up. ### Scout Pace Contest **Equipment:** Watch with a second hand **How to play:** Players will complete a 1-mile course in exactly 12 minutes, traveling in pairs and using the Scout pace (60 steps running, 60 steps walking). Divide the distance in half so that each pair runs half a mile from the meeting place, or have players go as many times around the same area as needed. Timing can be done at any point along the course at 2-minute intervals. **Scoring:** The pair that finishes closest to 12 minutes (5 more or less) wins. ### The Loaded Backpack **Equipment:** Paper and pencils, various camp items to choose from, a small pillow out of a backpack, compass, map, flashlight, piece of fishing line, matchbox, soap, comb, socks, spoon, toothbrush, toothpaste **How to play:** Arrange the items not too conspicuously in a pile to one side of a pack. The entire group will walk slowly along the trail in single file, silently looking for stray articles but not stopping or turning to look for them. At the end of the hike, each team huddles together to make a list of everything they saw in the correct order. **Scoring:** The team with the most complete list of items (if desired, teams can then arrange their list in order of how important the items would be to have in a backpack). ### Walking Pace **Equipment:** A safe, long-distance walking area with enough room for all players to walk alongside each other on a trail or path. Mark a starting line and walk instead of run. **How to play:** A walking race differs from running in that one foot must be in contact with the ground at all times. **Scoring:** See which player can reach the finish line first without running. --- # Job Name: 4888764 ## E.D.G.E. This activity provides experience and builds the appetites for longer adventures.* ### Equipment List - Trail and topographic maps and compasses - Backpacks - Sleeping bags and pads - Backpacking food - Water - Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Activity - Find a location from which you can take two easy round-trip hikes of two or three miles. - After a brief shakedown, take the first hike. - Back at the starting point, prepare lunch. - Use the "start, stop, continue" method to discuss the morning hike. - Give Scouts time to recognize their packs, redistribute crew gear, and make adjustments for the afternoon hike. - Hike back to camp and prepare dinner. ### Safety - Use the buddy system. - Have a first-aid kit available. - Cellphones are a good idea. - Special concerns in backpacking include getting lost, blisters, dehydration, and the use of untreated water. - Two-deep adult leadership is required for all activities. ### Notes --- **Volume 2 | Backpacking | 18–11** --- ## Right Page ### Job Name: 4088764 # BACKPACKING ## Main Event: Backpacking Overnighter **Date** _______________ ### Logistics **Location:** _________________________________ **Departure time:** _________________________________ **Return time:** _________________________________ **Duration of activity:** Overnight **Budget: Completed __________ Approved __________** **Camping: Duty roster __________ Menu __________** **Transportation: Group __________ Self __________ ### Challenging (Tier II) *Plan an overnight campout to practice backpacking techniques and explore nature. This activity builds on the practice day for a hike of a week or longer.* ### Equipment List - Trail and topographic maps and compasses - Backpacks - Sleeping bags and tents - Backpacking food - Water - Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Activity - Determine a location for the overnight and secure any necessary permits. - Review and study the area using maps of the region. - Depart on Scouts on Friday evening and hike a mile or two to a campsite. - Make all cooking and camp preparations on the group at the end of the trail. - If drivers will also be hiking, ferry one or more cars to the pick-up point on Friday night. - Hike back along the way, such as picking up trash or repairing damage caused by previous hikers. ### Safety - Use the buddy system. - Have a first-aid kit available. - Cellphones are a good idea. - Special concerns in backpacking include getting lost, blisters, dehydration, and the use of untreated water. - Two-deep adult leadership is required for all activities. ### Notes --- **18–12 | Backpacking | Volume 2** --- **Location 40 of 259 • 14%** --- # BACKPACKING ## Main Event: Backpacking Trek ### Date _______________ | Logistics | | |-----------|---| | Location | | | Departure time: | | | Return time: | | | Duration of activity: Weekend or longer | | | Budget: Completed __________ Approved __________ | | | Camping: Duty roster __________ Menu __________ | | | Transportation: Group __________ Self __________ | | ### Advanced (Tier III) Take a backpacking trip lasting a full weekend or longer. You could travel to a Scouting America activity center, a national park, a state park, or even a destination like the Appalachian Trail or Pacific Crest Trail. ### Equipment List * Trail and topographic maps and compasses * Backpacks * Sleeping bags and pads * Backpacking food * Water * Scout Basics Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Activity * If the group is not experienced in backpacking, recruit an expert to lead the trek. * Work with your Scoutmaster or another adult leader and a local council. * Determine a location for the trek, and secure any necessary permits. * Acquire and study topographic maps of the area. * Look for opportunities for side hikes to points of interest. You are automatically a citizen of the country where you were born, but most would agree that being a good citizen takes more effort. People often talk about the rights they have as citizens, but citizenship also comes with responsibilities, such as voting and paying taxes. Every opportunity you have to provide service to your country, whether through the military, political office, or community projects, strengthens our nation. The more you learn about citizenship, the better you can do your duty to your country, as the Scout Oath requires. ### Objectives This month's activities should: - Bring Scouts give back to their community. - Give Scouts a better understanding of how the government works. - Introduce Scouts to local, state, and national government leaders. - Demonstrate how the election process works. - Let Scouts experience how Congress passes laws. - Let Scouts visit a national or state park, historical monument, state capitol, or the U.S. Capitol. ### RELATED ADVANCEMENT AND AWARDS - Tenderfoot requirements 7a and 7b - Second Class requirements 8a, 8b, and 8e - First Class requirements 8a, 8c, and 8d - Citizenship in the Community, Citizenship in the Nation, and Citizenship in the World merit badges - Discovery: Service requirement 6 --- # Job Name _4088764 ## Leadership Planning As a leadership team, you may want to discuss the following items during your planning meetings when choosing citizenship as your program feature. 1. What types of service projects can we do as Scouts? 2. What service project opportunities are available in the community? 3. Where can we find information on our government leaders? 4. What local national park, national monument, or capitol can we visit? 5. How can we involve parents? 6. To meet our needs, what should we change in the sample meeting plans? ## CITIZENSHIP BASICS ### Branches of Government The United States government has three branches. The legislative branch, which includes the Senate and House of Representatives, makes the laws. The executive branch, which includes the president, the cabinet, and various departments and agencies, is charged with enforcing and administering the laws. The judicial branch, which includes the courts (the most significant of which is the Supreme Court), interprets and applies the laws. These three branches work together to make the government function properly. ## How a Bill Becomes a Law [DIAGRAM showing the legislative process through House and Senate, with steps including Introduction, Committee Action, Floor Action, Conference Committee, Congressional Approval, and Presidential action with Veto option] ## PARENTS CAN HELP WITH THE CITIZENSHIP PROGRAM FEATURE 1. Arranging visits to government offices or meetings with government officials 2. Providing transportation for activities 3. Talking about how and why they vote 4. Teaching about various levels of government 5. Giving a firsthand account of major court cases that have affected their lives 6. Helping organize and lead the games 7. Maintaining current Youth Protection certification, which is required of all adults providing transportation for or participating in outings. --- Volume 2 | Citizenship | 19–1 --- 19–2 | Citizenship | Volume 2 Location 44 of 259 • 16% --- # Job Name: 4088764 ## Checks and Balances in the Federal Government State and local governments—and even nonprofit organizations—often use a similar three-branch structure for their own governance. ### Checks and Balances To make sure that one branch of the government does not become too powerful, a system of checks and balances exists in our country. This system allows for each branch of the government to limit the powers of the other branches and keep each branch balanced with the others. Some people think the president has the most power in the government, but the legislative branch is equally powerful and can call for a "check" on another branch at any time if it considers the actions of another branch unconstitutional. [Diagram showing the three branches of government and their checks and balances on each other] **Judicial Branch** - Justices are appointed for life from executive nominations, and the Senate approves them. The courts interpret the Constitution. **Legislative Branch** - Congress creates lower courts and can remove judges through impeachment. The Senate approves judicial appointments. Congress makes laws, creates agencies and departments, and controls funds to carry out items and provide for the common defense. **Executive Branch** - The President nominates judges and appoints cabinet members and other federal judges. Congress creates lower courts and can remove judges through impeachment. The Senate approves judicial appointments. Courts can declare acts of Congress to be unconstitutional. Congress makes laws, creates agencies and departments, and controls funds to carry out items and provide for the common defense. A two-thirds vote is needed to override a presidential veto. The President can veto laws created by Congress. Volume 2 | Citizenship | 19–3 --- # Job Name: 4088764 ## Community Service Community Service means service that benefits the people or area around you, and it is a key part of the Scouting program. Various advancement requirements involve doing community service, giving back to others and completing a project to help out. Think about others and service projects in your community. Think about others and are willing to help. Giving back and taking care of your community is part of being a good citizen. Don't take for granted the democracy and freedoms we have in this country—generations before us fought hard for the appreciation for the gifts of citizenship, freedom, and democracy we all enjoy as Americans. ### POLICY ON SCOUT PARTICIPATION IN POLITICAL EVENTS Uniformed unit members and leaders may participate in flag ceremonies at political events and may lead the Pledge of Allegiance; however, they should retire after the ceremony and not remain on the speakers' platform or in a conspicuous location where attendees or viewers could construe their presence as an endorsement or symbol of support. In addition, photos of candidates or Scouts in uniform or with Scout America marks and logos are not allowed in political campaign materials of any kind. Volunteers and professionals must be alert to situations that would imply Scouting America favoritism for one candidate over another. Scout-owned facilities may not be used in advocacy against the active participation of uniformed Scouts and leaders in political events is mandatory. ### Staying Nonpartisan We live in an age when political parties and outside groups seek to gain political advantage over one another. While Scouting remains strictly nonpartisan in matters of politics and citizenship, it is important to focus on the process and not the candidates. Keep in mind that everyone in your unit doesn't necessarily share the same political beliefs. ### GO ON THE ROAD This program feature offers great opportunities to take your program on the road. Rather than holding weekly meetings at the same place, hold them at city hall, the local courthouse, or another government facility. If possible, involve elected officials or other government officials in teaching. Volume 2 | Citizenship | 19–4 --- Location 46 of 259 • 17% --- # SCOUTS BSA PROGRAM FEATURES VOLUME 2 FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## CITIZENSHIP GAMES ### Mock Election **Equipment:** Poster board and craft supplies (optional), paper for ballots, a ballot box, pens and pencils **How to play:** Each team selects one member to run for president of the made-up land or country of your choosing (e.g., Candy Land, Country of Sandwiches). Each candidate can make a quick 1-minute speech about the issues on which they are running. Everyone is given a ballot and asked to vote for the candidate they feel best represent the land or country. Each team takes a turn to place their votes in the ballot box. After all teams have voted, ballots are read aloud, counted up, and the newly elected president is announced. **Scoring:** The candidate with the most votes wins the presidency. **Notes:** Review the Electoral College and point out the differences between the popular vote, the electoral vote, and how the game, and the Electoral College. ### Bill to Law **Equipment:** Poster showing the steps a bill takes to become a law (optional), paper and pens to write bills **How to play:** Divide into two teams to represent the two houses of a bicameral legislature. Have each team create a new law for an imaginary country, making sure they follow the bill-to-law process. Once a bill is created by the first team, the other team passes its new law, teams should trade laws and try to pass each bill again. Once a bill passes both houses, have it sent to the designated leader (president) for approval or veto. **Scoring:** No scoring for this activity. **Notes:** Explain the bill-to-law process (with a poster or other visual aid) before you start the activity. Have assistant leaders help each team go through the steps of the bill-to-law process. ### Mock Court Case **Equipment:** Tables for the judge, prosecution, defendant, jury, and observers, and a gavel (or an effective substitute) for the judge **How to play:** Decide on a judge, two lawyers, a defendant, a few witnesses, and a jury for the court case. All other Scouts will be observers in the courtroom. Allow each side five minutes in the room to conduct a second court case simulation. Go through a typical court session, and let the jury deliberate and come up with a verdict. Allow the jury to suggest a punishment for the defendant, with approval by the judge. **Scoring:** None. **Notes:** You might want to plan some of the case details prior to conducting this activity so the Scouts can prepare for the activity. Details could include stealing a bike or a car, littering, or committing a traffic violation. You also might want to encourage Scouts to dress for the parts they will play. ### Guess the President **Equipment:** Facts about famous U.S. presidents **How to play:** The teams assemble in different corners and are informed that they will be visited by a famous president shortly. They will have three minutes to ask the visitor questions. The famous visitor can answer only yes or no questions. The game leader assigns a president for each team (e.g., George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Teddy Roosevelt, Abraham Lincoln). The team leaders go to their corners, and the teams begin questioning. **Scoring:** If the team guesses the president correctly, the team receives 1 point. After a set number of rounds, the team with the most points wins. --- ## E.D.G.E. Ideas **Explain** how it is done—Tell them; **Demonstrate** the steps—Show them; **Guide** learners as they practice—Watch them do it. **Enable** them to succeed on their own—Have them practice/teach it. ### EXPLAIN - Explain why we need the government. - Describe the responsibilities of government. - Teach how bills become laws. - Compare and contrast forms of government from the city to the federal level. ### DEMONSTRATE - Demonstrate how voting takes place. - Explain the amendment process. - Use visual guides to demonstrate the bill-to-law process. - Show a video explaining how the Electoral College works. ### GUIDE - Guide Scouts through a mock election. - Help Scouts look up the names of their elected representatives. - Have Scouts brainstorm ideas for community service projects. ### ENABLE - Enable the Scouts to hold a unit election. - Encourage the Scouts to research candidates and conduct a meaningful service project for the community. - Allow Scouts to research new laws for your unit. - Have Scouts teach an Arrow of Light patrol what Scouting is really about. --- ## MAIN EVENT SUMMARIES | | **ESSENTIAL** | **CHALLENGING** | **ADVANCED** | |---|---|---|---| | **Day Activity** | Overnight service project—Complete a meaningful community service project in the county, city, or other community setting in a location where you can help with. | Day or Overnight Activity—Plan or research a service visit and complete a meaningful service project at your or state park or a historical site. Work with the site to identify service project opportunities, and then carry out the project for the site.
