Meeting Plan: Roles and Responsibilities of Citizens #### Week 1 Date _______________ | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | Prospecting (5 minutes before meeting) | Have each Scout display on their shirt to Scouts with the Citizenship in the Community, Citizenship in the Nation, Citizenship in the World merit badges. | | 6:50 p.m. | | Opening Ceremony (7 minutes) | • Flag presentation • Opening prayer • Uniform inspection • Safety Moment | | 7 p.m. | | Group Instruction (6 minutes) | • Present and lead a group discussion on responsibilities of citizens, including: • Supporting and defending the Constitution • Staying informed on issues • Contributing to their community | | 7:10 p.m. | | Skills Instruction (20 minutes) | Discuss the responsibility of actively participating and exercising rights and duties that follow out this responsibility. | | 7:15 p.m. | | | Discuss the importance of voting and participating in the government process. | | | | | Discuss the responsibility of paying income and other taxes honestly and on time, and learn how taxes are used. | | | | Breakout Groups (10 minutes) | • Begin playing participation in the main event. • Watch an advancement requirements as needed. | | 8 p.m. | | Game (15 minutes) | Play Guess the President (described earlier). | | 8:15 p.m. | | Closing (5 minutes) | Announcements Leader's minute Closing | | 8:25 p.m. | | After the Meeting (15 minutes) | Leadership team reviews plans for the next meeting and for the main event. | | | **Total 90 minutes of meeting** *All times are suggested. --- Volume 2 | Citizenship | 19–7 and 19–8 --- # CITIZENSHIP ## Meeting Plan: Citizens and the Courts ### Week 4 Date | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | Prospecting 5-10 minutes before meeting | Have copies of the U.S. Constitution on hand. Encourage Scouts to arrive early to read the preamble and find the answers to questions about how the Constitution and its functions affect families and communities. | | 7 p.m. | | Opening Ceremony 10 minutes | Flag presentation Uniform inspection Safety Moment | | 7 p.m. | | Group Instruction 20 minutes | • Learn the differences between the federal and state courts and why they are needed. • Discuss the role of the Supreme Court in our course, judge, and jury. Explore other professions in the courtroom. | | 7:10 p.m. | | Skills Instruction 50 minutes | • Explain the differences between criminal and civil trials. • Discuss the benefits afforded by the U.S. courts, such as the presumption of innocence and the presumption of innocence. • Explain and discuss how the Supreme Court checks the power of the other branches. • Research and review major court cases decided on by the Supreme Court. • Read one of the most controversial court cases of the Supreme Court, such as Dred Scott, Brown v. the Board of Education, Miranda v. Arizona, and others. | | 7:20 p.m. | | Breakout Groups 15 minutes | • Talk about the ways Supreme Court cases affect your everyday life. • Finalize plans for participation in the main event. | | 8:20 p.m. | | Game 15 minutes | Held a mock court case (described earlier). | | 8:40 p.m. | | Closing 5 minutes | Announcements Leader's minute Closing | | 8:55 p.m. | | | **Total 90 minutes of meeting** | | | | After the Meeting 15 minutes | Leadership team reviews plans for the next meeting and for the main event. | | | *All times are suggested. --- # SCOUTS BSA PROGRAM FEATURES VOLUME 2 FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## Left Page (19-11) ### CITIZENSHIP #### Main Event: Community Service Project **Date** _______________ ### Logistics **Location** _______________ **Departure time** _______________ **Return time** _______________ **Duration of activity:** 4 to 6 hours **Budget: Completed _____ Approved _____** **Camping: Duty roster _____ Menu _____** **Transportation: Group _____ Self _____ ### Essential (Tier I) Complete a meaningful community service project in your city. Work with city, county, or state officials, or community leaders, to learn about a project that your unit can help with. ### Equipment List - Appropriate tools and supplies to complete the project - Lunch (determine whether group or individual) - Water - Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Activity - Contact community leaders to determine available service projects. - Determine a time and place to transport to the location. - Complete the task as assigned. - Break for lunch. - Clean up appropriately. - Thank your community leader to make sure the project was completed acceptably. ### Safety - Assign tools to appropriate age groups. - Use gloves, safety glasses, and ear protection as appropriate. - Follow Scouting America guidelines for service projects, tool use, and work at elevations or excavations. - Have a cellphone available for emergencies. - Two-deep adult leadership is required for all activities. ### Notes _________________________________ --- ## Right Page (19-12) ### CITIZENSHIP #### Main Event: Park or Historic Site Visit and Service Project **Date** _______________ ### Logistics **Location** _______________ **Departure time** _______________ **Return time** _______________ **Duration of activity:** 6 to 8 hours or overnight **Budget: Completed _____ Approved _____** **Camping: Duty roster _____ Menu _____** **Transportation: Group _____ Self _____ ### Challenging (Tier II) Visit a national or state park or a historical monument near you. Learn the site's history and historical significance. Contact the appropriate management officials to see if there is a service project you can carry out at that service. Consider incorporating a campout to explore the history of the area. ### Equipment List - Appropriate tools and supplies to complete the project - Food - Water - Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Activity - Have unit decide what national or state park or historical monument will be visited and whether it is appropriate to incorporate camping. - Call the location, and determine availability for tours and/or camping. - Schedule a long day trip or a weekend trip, depending on location and how far the location is from your home. - Tour the park or site. - Complete a service project. ### Safety - Follow safe camping practices. - Use the buddy system. - Assign tools to appropriate age groups. - Use gloves, safety glasses, and ear protection as appropriate. - Follow Scouting America guidelines for service projects, tool use, and work at elevations or excavations. - Have a cellphone available for emergencies. - Two-deep adult leadership is required for all activities. ### Notes _________________________________ --- > In order to view this proof accurately, the Overprint Preview Option must be set to Always in Acrobat Professional or Adobe Reader. Please contact your customer service representative if you have questions about halftone files. **Back to 11** --- # CITIZENSHIP ## Main Event: State Capital Legislative Visit and Committee Testimony **Date** _______________ ### Logistics **Location** _________________________________ **Departure time:** _______________ **Return time:** _______________ **Duration of activity:** Overnight **Budget: Completed _______ Approved _______** **Camping: Duty roster _______ Menu _______** **Transportation: Group _______ Cost _______** ### Equipment List • A predetermined opinion on a legislative issue • Official uniforms for visit • Camping equipment • First aid kit • Water • Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Advanced (Tier III) *Develop expertise in your state by researching an issue of interest to the group that is the topic of an upcoming legislative committee hearing. Recruit group members to develop opinions and present testimony. Should even present testimony and/or written testimony should plan on camping as appropriate.* ### Activity • Determine a legislative issue of interest. • Draft an opinion that can be presented to lawmakers. • Plan your visit to the state capital. • Arrange a tour of the Capitol and a meeting with your representative. • Testify to a committee if appropriate. • If significant travel is required, plan on camping as appropriate. ### Safety • Avoid inappropriate behaviors while in the Capitol. • Follow safe camping practices. • Use the buddy system. • Have a cellphone available for emergencies. • Two-deep adult leadership is required for all activities. ### Notes --- # RESOURCES AND REFERENCES ## Websites **U.S. Courts** www.uscourts.gov **National Archives** www.archives.gov **National Park Service** www.nps.gov **U.S. Government Portal** www.usa.gov **U.S. House of Representatives** www.house.gov **U.S. Senate** www.senate.gov **U.S. Supreme Court** www.supremecourt.gov **White House** www.whitehouse.gov ## Books Barbour, Christine. *Keeping the Republic: Power and Citizenship in American Politics*, 6th ed. CQ Press, 2013. Bowman, Ann, and Richard C. Kearney. *State and Local Government*, 9th ed. Cengage Learning, 2013. Greenberg, Edward S., and Benjamin I. Page. *America's Democratic Republic*, 4th ed. Pearson, 2007. Kielburger, Marc, and Craig Kielburger. *Take Action! A Guide to Active Citizenship*. John Wiley & Sons Inc., 2002. Krull, Kathleen. *A Kid's Guide to America's Bill of Rights: Curfews, Censorship, and the 100-Pound Giant*. William Morrow, 1999. Mackintosh, Barry. *The National Parks: Shaping the System*. National Park Service, 2003. Rusch, Elisabeth. *Generation Fix: Young Ideas for a Better World*. Zest Books, 2011. Schmidt, Steffen. *American Government and Politics Today*, 2013–2014 ed. Cengage Learning, 2013. ## Related Program Features Camping, Duty to God, Ethics, Living (See Volume 1) ## Photo and Illustration Credits ©iStockphoto: Capitol building file; Shutterstock: vote buttons with red center and red stripes; ©Jupiterimages Corbis: "Your vote counts," ©iStockphoto; U.S. flag, ©Claudio Divizia; Statue of Liberty, ©Cliff Fruit; acala, ©Oliver Le Moal; Declaration of Independence, and Scout painting, Scouting America file; and 19-5 (Shutterstock.com/©Kim Seed) ## Acknowledgments We are grateful to Jillian Infusino, Whittier, California, 2014-2015 national Venturing vice president, and Scoutmaster Jack Clow, Washington, DC, and the staffs in Washington, DC, for helping to develop the Citizenship program feature. --- **Volume 2 Citizenship 19–13** **19–14 Citizenship Volume 2** --- # Job Name: 4898764 ## ENGINEERING ### Building Our World In 62 B.C., the people of Rome were faced with a problem: A wooden bridge spanning the Tiber River had been destroyed by fire, and they needed a way to reach Tiber Island. Their solution was to construct a new bridge made of stone and concrete. That engineering could not burn down again. Believe it or not, that bridge is still in use to the day—and it isn't even the oldest in the world. Bridges like the Pons Fabricius are great examples of what engineers do every day. Engineers work to solve the difficult problems that face our society. Whether it's building a bridge to cross a river, creating a new machine to manufacture cars, or fitting a huge amount of technology into a smartphone, an engineer's work can leave a lasting impact on society. This month, Scouts will have the opportunity to learn about the different disciplines within engineering. The weekly meetings will be full of fun activities that enable Scouts to see firsthand the broad reach of this field across a variety of disciplines. ## Objectives This month's activities should: - Define the different branches of engineering. - Help Scouts understand what engineers do. - Demonstrate key engineering concepts. - Help Scouts explore the different career paths available to them. - Demonstrate real-world applications of engineering principles. ## RELATED ADVANCEMENT AND AWARDS Earning merit badges in such areas as Maintenance, Aviation, Chemistry, Composite Materials, Digital Technology, Drafting, Electricity, Electronics, Energy, Engineering, Entrepreneurship, Farm Mechanics, Game Design, Inventing, Mining in Society, Model Design and Building, Nuclear Science, Pioneering, Programming, Robotics, Space Exploration, Surveying, and Welding merit badges --- # Job Name: 4898764 ## Leadership Planning As a leadership team, you may want to discuss the following items during your planning meetings when choosing engineering as your program feature. 1. Are there adults in our unit who are engineers or might be able to speak about how they solve difficult technical problems at their place of employment? 2. What videos will you show? 3. Will you be able to discuss mechanical, chemical, electrical, and civil concepts in detail? 4. How can we involve parents? 5. What engineering-related merit badges should we focus on? 6. Is there an amusement park or carnival nearby? 7. Where will we host our main event? 8. Will the main event be an overnighter? 9. In order to meet needs, what should we change in the sample meeting plans? ## DIFFERENT TYPES OF ENGINEERS Engineers work tirelessly to find solutions to problems. From building bridges to developing water-treatment systems, engineers can be found in almost every industry. Engineering can be broken down into four major branches: **Chemical engineering**—the study of chemicals and the process of creating new materials and compounds **Mechanical engineering**—the study of designing mechanical systems **Civil engineering**—the study of designing and constructing public and private works **Electrical engineering**—the study of designing electrical systems and components ## PARENTS CAN HELP WITH THE ENGINEERING PROGRAM 1. Sharing their expertise 2. Providing transportation and supervision for the main event 3. Providing materials for the different games and activities 4. Mentoring Scouts as they work on advancements 5. Maintaining current Youth Protection certification, which is required of all adults providing transportation for or participating in outings. --- Volume 2 | Engineering | 20–1 20–2 | Engineering | Volume 2 --- # SCOUTS BSA PROGRAM FEATURES VOLUME 2 FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## SUBDISCIPLINES WITHIN THE ENGINEERING BRANCHES ### Chemical Engineering - Biofomatics - Biotechnology - Ceramic engineering - Cheminformatics - Environmental engineering - Fluid dynamics - Molecular engineering - Nanotechnology - Polymer and plastics engineering - Textile engineering ### Civil Engineering - Materials engineering - Coastal engineering - Construction engineering - Structural and seismic engineering - Environmental engineering - Geotechnical engineering - Hydrological engineering - Surveying - Transportation, municipal, and urban engineering ### Mechanical Engineering - Biomechanics - Mechatronics - Acoustical engineering - Aerospace engineering - Manufacturing engineering - Automotive engineering - HVAC engineering - Process engineering - Robotics engineering ### Electrical Engineering - Power engineering - Control engineering - Electronic engineering - Microelectronics - Signal processing - Telecommunications engineering - Instrumentation engineering - Computer engineering - Lighting engineering - Solar engineering --- Location 60 of 259 • 22% --- # ENGINEERING GAMES ## Paper Tower **Equipment:** Measuring tape, newspaper, clear tape **How to play:** Give each Scout two full sheets of newspaper and 5 minutes to construct a tower. Scouts must use only newspaper and tape. The tower must stand alone and support a small weight. The Scout whose tower stands the tallest in 5 seconds wins. **Variation:** Follow the same instructions, but give each Scout 8 inches of clear tape. See how much taller the towers can become with this added support. ## Parachute Races **Equipment:** A plastic bag or lightweight fabric, scissors, string, a small object to act as weight **How to play:** Give teams of Scouts 5 minutes to design a parachute for the weighted object. Explain that their objective is to create a parachute that is the last one to touch the ground when all are released from a given height. **Scoring:** The last parachute to touch the ground wins. --- ## Geocache Scavenger Hunt **Equipment:** Two to three GPS units, prizes, paper, pen **How to play:** Before the meeting, hide prizes around your meeting area. With each prize, leave a card with GPS coordinates of the next prize. Divide Scouts into the same number of teams as there are GPS units. Give each team the coordinates of the first prize. The first team to find all the items wins. --- ## Hot Potato **Equipment:** Effervescent tablets (like Alka-Seltzer), balloon, water **How to play:** Have Scouts form a circle (preferably outdoors). Fill a small balloon with an inch of water, and drop a tablet into the water. You may need to break the tablet into pieces. Inflate the balloon, tie it shut, and pass it to a Scout. The objective of the game is to not be holding the balloon when the tablet makes it pop. Pass the balloon to the person next to them. The person holding the balloon when it pops is eliminated. Start another balloon around the circle. The winner is the remaining Scout. --- # E.D.G.E. Ideas > **Explain** how it is done—Tell them. > **Demonstrate** the steps—Show them. > **Guide** learners as they practice—Watch them do it. > **Enable** them to practice on their own—Have them practice/teach it. ## EXPLAIN - Define what engineering is and its history. - Discuss the major branches of engineering. - Discuss how an engineer's work can be seen in everyday life. - Find websites from the Internet that demonstrate how something is built using engineering techniques. ## DEMONSTRATE - Show a video of an engineering project underway. - Invite a speaker, preferably an engineer, to visit your troop to discuss the types of problems he or she works to solve. - Demonstrate how a simple circuit works. - Demonstrate a chemical reaction. (Mentos candy in Diet Coke is always a hit.) - Show how a solar panel can be used to generate electricity. - Show how a combustion engine works. ## GUIDE - Have Scouts come up with various approaches to a problem while you supervise. - Lead a reflection on the different activities Scouts complete. - Watch Scouts design and build the various experiments, offering guidance throughout the process. - Have Scouts assist in the planning of the activities. - Help Scouts identify an opportunity to teach and mentor another person. ## ENABLE - Challenge Scouts to improve their problem-solving skills and to learn something new. - Encourage Scouts to mentor younger Scouts in engineering activities. - Have Scouts watch engineering programs to get a better understanding of how things are designed and built. - Encourage Scouts to talk with some adults about the different engineering-related career paths. --- ## MAIN EVENT SUMMARIES | | **ESSENTIAL** | **CHALLENGING** | **ADVANCED** | |---|---|---|---| | **Day Activity** | Amusement park observations— Visit an amusement park or carnival and observe where examples of engineering mechanical, chemical, electrical, and civil can be seen. A unit can arrange for a park engineer to give a tour and explain how the park was designed and constructed. At the end of the day, have Scouts discuss their findings and note examples of engineering work that went into the development of each attraction. | Amusement park design— Visit an amusement park or carnival and decide on a project you could improve the designs of the attraction. Have Scouts brainstorm and engage in a discussion on how the amusement park might incorporate the Scouts' new designs. See if your Scouts can identify any significant differences between the old rides and the newer rides. | Amusement park experiments— Scouts visit an amusement park or carnival and have research on hand. Have Scouts conduct a number of experiments on various rides. All Scouts should participate in the activities and share their observations with one another. Lead a discussion on how the different forces of engineering affect the aspects of engineering. | --- # SCOUTS BSA PROGRAM FEATURES VOLUME 2 FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## Job Name: 4889764 --- ## ENGINEERING ### Meeting Plan: Introduction and Civil Engineering #### Week 1 Date | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | Preposting (5 minutes before meeting) | Play a video from a TV show such as "How It's Made" that demonstrates how something is made or constructed. Alternatively, show a video of how a skyscraper, water treatment plant, or bridge is constructed. (If you do not have access to a TV, you might check YouTube and search for "how it's made" and often post ten- to three-minute videos on their websites.) | | 5:45 p.m. | | Opening Ceremony (10 minutes) | Flag presentation Uniform inspection Safety Moment | | 6 p.m. | | Group Instruction (5 minutes) | Discuss the following: • The definition of engineering • The different areas within engineering • The basics of civil engineering and the types of work these engineers do | | 6:10 p.m. | | Skills Instruction (35 minutes) | • Experiment with numerous materials to see which ones work best at cleaning the water. • Build a dam. Experiment with a number of materials and designs to determine which ones work better as the water flows past. • Discuss how dams are used to provide water and electricity to municipal areas. • Build a cable-stayed bridge that is at least 3 feet long, 2 feet wide, is level, and has three or more cables on each side of the bridge. • Ensure the bridge is capable of holding up a few toy cars. • Discuss how bridge length and the distances between supports affect a structure's strength. | | 6:10 p.m. | | Breakout Groups (15 minutes) | • New members practice basic Scout skills. • Older members choose an engineering-related merit badge. • Review the last main event, and discuss what could have made it better. | | 8 p.m. | | Game (10 minutes) | Play Paper Tower (described earlier). | | 8:15 p.m. | | Closing (5 minutes) | Announcements Leader's minute Cleaning | | 8:25 p.m. | | After the Meeting (15 minutes) | Leadership team reviews plans for the next meeting and for the main event. | | | **Total 90 minutes of meeting** *All times are suggested. --- ## ENGINEERING ### Meeting Plan: Chemical Engineering #### Week 2 Date | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | Preposting (5 minutes before meeting) | Play a video from a TV show such as "How It's Made" that demonstrates how chemical reactions transform objects. | | 5:45 p.m. | | Opening Ceremony (10 minutes) | Flag presentation Uniform inspection Safety Moment | | 6 p.m. | | Group Instruction (5 minutes) | Discuss the following: • The definition of chemical engineering and the types of work these engineers do • The definition of compounds, polymers, bases, and acids | | 6:10 p.m. | | Skills Instruction (35 minutes) | • Make some goo similar to the details listed at www.scoutshop.org/cms/time-method-1-/ • Build pop rockets and see which design goes the farthest. Visit the website at https://outdoorprogram.org/ip/chemists-Celebrate-Earth-Week/2009-ccew-spring-edition-on-line-catalog-for-products-and-placement-ideas-for-scouts/ for more information. • Lead a discussion on the chemical reaction that causes the rockets to pop off the launch pad. • Discuss how a rocket engine is similar to a hobby stove. Have Scouts experiment with different fin designs and engines. • Lead a discussion on the chemical reaction that is occurring within the rocket engine and how it is similar or different from the engines used in remote spacecraft. | | 6:15 p.m. | | Breakout Groups (15 minutes) | • New members practice basic Scout skills. • Older members work on an engineering-related merit badge. • Review the plans for the main event. | | 8 p.m. | | Game (10 minutes) | Play Hot Potato (described earlier). | | 8:15 p.m. | | Closing (5 minutes) | Announcements Leader's minute Cleaning | | 8:25 p.m. | | After the Meeting (15 minutes) | Leadership team reviews plans for the next meeting and for the main event. | | | **Total 90 minutes of meeting** *All times are suggested. --- **Location 64 of 259 • 24%** **Volume 2** | **Engineering** | **20–7** **20–8** | **Engineering** | **Volume 2** --- # ENGINEERING ## Meeting Plan: Mechanical Engineering ### Week 3 Date | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | Prospecting 5 minutes before meeting | Play a video from a TV show such as "Modern Marvels" that demonstrates how an engine works. | | 6:45 p.m. | | Opening Ceremony 10 minutes | Flag presentation Oath and Law Scout Slogan Safety Moment | | 7 p.m. | | Group Instruction 7 minutes | Discuss the following: • What is mechanical engineering and the types of work these engineers do • Definition of drag, torque, hydraulics, and other mechanical concepts | | 7:10 p.m. | | Skills Instruction 25 minutes | • How Scouts build string telephones. • Lead a discussion on sound waves and how sound is changed by objects such as water. Have Scouts build a Rube Goldberg machine and see which Scouts can build the simplest way to accomplish an everyday task. • Calculate the mechanical advantage of a pulley. • Have Scouts build a catapult using craft sticks and rubber bands, or build an elevator. An example of a similar activity can be found at http://www.scouting.org/scoutsource/BoyScouts/ProgramFeatures/MeritBadges/mb_index/html. | | 7:15 p.m. | | Breakout Groups 15 minutes | • New members practice basic Scout skills. • Other members work on an engineering-related merit badge. • Make plans for participation in the main event. | | 9 p.m. | | Game 10 minutes | Play Parachute Race (described earlier). | | 9:15 p.m. | | Closing 3 minutes | Announcements Leader's minute Closing | | 9:25 p.m. | | | **Total 90 minutes of meeting** | | | | After the Meeting 15 minutes | Leadership team reviews plans for the next meeting and for the main event. | | | *All times are suggested. --- # ENGINEERING ## Meeting Plan: Electrical Engineering ### Week 4 Date | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | Prospecting 5 minutes before meeting | Play a video from a TV show such as "How It's Made" that demonstrates how a computer is built. | | 6:45 p.m. | | Opening Ceremony 10 minutes | Flag presentation Oath and Law Scout Slogan Safety Moment | | 7 p.m. | | Group Instruction 7 minutes | Discuss the following: • What is electrical engineering and the types of work these engineers do • What a circuit is and what a resistor, diode, inductor, and capacitor do | | 7:10 p.m. | | Skills Instruction 25 minutes | • Take apart a flashlight, and discuss the components that make it work. • Have Scouts build a simple LED flashlight using an LED, tape, and a CR2032 battery. • Discuss a variety of fashion resources. • Explore how each is designed, the components utilized, and the similarities in design. • Have Scouts take apart an alarm clock and explain how each caused the item to stop working. Have Scouts assemble their own USB charging kit. | | 7:15 p.m. | | Breakout Groups 15 minutes | • New members practice basic Scout skills. • Other members work on an engineering-related merit badge. • Make plans for participation in the main event. | | 9 p.m. | | Game 10 minutes | Play Parachute Race (described earlier). | | 9:15 p.m. | | Closing 3 minutes | Announcements Leader's minute Closing | | 9:25 p.m. | | | **Total 90 minutes of meeting** | | | | After the Meeting 15 minutes | Leadership team reviews plans for the next meeting and for the main event. | | | *All times are suggested. --- **Volume 2** | **Engineering** | **20–9** **20–10** | **Engineering** | **Volume 2** Location 66 of 259 + 25% --- # SCOUTS BSA PROGRAM FEATURES VOLUME 2 FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## Left Page: ENGINEERING ### Main Event: Amusement Park Observations **Date:** ### Logistics - Location: - Departure time: - Return time: - Duration of activity: Day/Half Day - Budget: Completed _____ Approved _____ - Camping: Duty roster _____ Menu _____ - Transportation: Group _____ Self _____ ### Essential (Tier I) Visit an amusement park or carnival to demonstrate how the disciplines of engineering are used to design rides that are safe, concealed and constructed. At the end of the day, have Scouts discuss their observations and what they learned. See if your unit can arrange for a park engineer to give a tour and explain how engineering was used to ensure rides were concealed and constructed. At the end of the day, have Scouts discuss their observations and what they learned about how an amusement park went into the development of each attraction. ### Equipment List - Uniform as decided upon - Lunch (decide on individual or group) - Personal water bottle - Camera - Spending money - Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ### Activity - Decide which amusement park, carnival, or fair your unit will visit. - Contact the park's management office or speak with a park engineer who can discuss the design and safety measures of various rides. - Encourage Scouts to share notes throughout the day on common engineering features they observe. - At the end of the day, have the Scouts share their observations with one another. --- asin: B0BJ7HG74Z extraction_date: '2026-03-19' ocr_backend: claude-haiku-4-5 (2-pass corrected) page_count: 128 source: kindle-cloud-reader-screenshots title: Scouts BSA Program Features Volume 3 --- # PROGRAM FEATURES FOR TROOPS AND CREWS ## A Guide to Program Planning ### Volume 3 ### Volume 1 - Camping - Climbing and Rappelling - Communication - Cycling - First Aid - Games - Geocaching - Hiking - Outdoor Ethics - Paddle Sports - Pioneering - Rifle Shooting - Safety - Science - Snowboarding and Skiing - Spectator Sports ### Volume 2 - Archery - Backpacking - Citizenship - Engineering - Fishing - Fitness and Nutrition - Living History - Mathematics - Mentoring - Music - Orienteering - Scuba Diving - Soccer - Special Needs Awareness - Wilderness Survival - Wildlife Management ### Volume 3 - Caving - Cooking - COPE - Duty to God - Emergency Preparedness - Ethics - Financial Management - Multimedia - Nature and Environment - Project Planning - Shotgun Shooting - Skateboarding - Sustainability - Swimming - Technology - Winter Camping ## Key - **Green** = Outdoor program features - **Red** = Sports program features - **Orange** = Health and safety program features - **Purple** = Citizenship and personal development program features - **Blue** = STEM program features - **Pink** = Arts and hobbies program features --- BOY SCOUTS OF AMERICA 33112 661801 ISBN 978-0-8395-3112-8 ©2024 Boy Scouts of America 300065533 Location 3 of 257 • 0% --- # CONTENTS Introduction-1 Introduction 33-1 Caving 34-1 Cooking 35-1 COPE 36-1 Duty to God 37-1 Emergency Preparedness 38-1 Ethics 39-1 Financial Management 40-1 Multimedia 41-1 Nature and Environment 42-1 Project Planning 43-1 Shotgun Shooting 44-1 Skateboarding 45-1 Sustainability 46-1 Swimming 47-1 Technology 48-1 Winter Camping Appendix-1 Appendix Appendix-2 Blank program planning chart Appendix-3 Blank meeting plan Appendix-4 Blank main event plan Appendix-5 Budget Worksheet Appendix-6 Acknowledgments --- # PROGRAM FEATURES ## FOR TROOPS AND CREWS As the Boy Scouts of America's mission statement says, Scouting exists "to prepare young people to make ethical and moral choices over their lifetimes by instilling in them the values of the Scout Oath and Scout Law." There is nothing in the mission statement about meetings, outings, other activities. However, it would be impossible to achieve our mission without dynamic, relevant programming, which also provides the context for the program to work. We have created Program Features for Troops and Crews to make planning a little easier for you, the unit leader. These three volumes of program features bring together all features for use by Scout troops and Venturing crews. The features are organized by program section to help with planning. While your unit may not use the material here exactly as written, it offers a launching point for you and your Scouts to be creative in your programming and get your members coming back, facilitate advancement and personal growth, and help you achieve Scouting's mission. ## How To Give a Safety Moment In Scouting, a safety moment serves as a brief yet crucial reminder of how to ensure safety during our activities. Here it provides a quick tip to help us enjoy our time together while being safe during each Scout meeting, one Scout is designated to deliver a safety moment. Here are some tips. **Introduction:** Greet everyone and introduce yourself as the Scout giving the safety moment. **Use a Relevant Topic:** Present the safety moment prop or video or choose one from www.scouting.org/health-safety/. Pick something directly related to an upcoming activity or outing. When possible, focus on preventing injury, and remember to make it brief. **Explain:** Use clear and simple language to explain the safety tip. For example, "When using a pocketknife, always cut away from yourself." **Demonstrate:** Demonstrate the safety tip or use visual aids to help everyone understand. **Take Questions:** Invite questions from the group to ensure everyone understands the safety tip. **Conclusion:** End by thanking everyone for listening and reminding them to stay safe. ## Annual Program Planning In Scouting, program planning is a two-phase process: - Long-term planning results in an annual calendar and a set of unit goals for the year. - Short-term planning yields detailed plans for one month's meetings and outings—and sometimes a little more. ## The Annual Planning Conference Long-term planning happens at the annual planning conference. This should be held as soon as possible after school, community, and council calendars have been published. A month or two before the planning conference, the committee chair, unit leader, and senior patrol leader or crew president should perform **Step 1—Gather the necessary information.** - Key school dates, like holidays, homecoming, and exams - Community event dates, including those the unit might want to participate in and dates to avoid - The chartered organization's key dates, again considering opportunities to collaborate as well as potential scheduling conflicts - Personal dates, such as family vacations, that may affect the unit's activities - Budget information - Data collected from the troops resource survey or activity interest survey - Last year's annual plan, if you have one - Unit priorities and goals - Advancement records for each member - A general outline of last year's program **Step 2—Discuss the planning process with your top youth and adult leaders.** Review with them the information you have gathered and his or her role in it. Discuss options for programs and activities and the unit's goals. Share a draft outline for next year's program, and ask for the youth leader's input and thoughts. Be flexible at this point. You should make adjustments to your draft before making it available online at www.scouting.org/programs/boy-scouts/planning/ for troops and www.venturing.org/annual-program-planning.html for crews, to be on the same page with the agenda and to work ahead. ## Setting Goals **What sorts of unit goals should the draft plan include?** Here are some possibilities: - Attend summer camp. - Have an outdoor adventure at least once a month. - Strengthen relations with the chartered organization by planning a service project to benefit the organization and by increasing the unit's presence, such as on Scout Sunday or Scout Sabbath. - Earn the National Camping Award and a gold Journey completion rating. - Conduct a fundraiser to help pay for unit expenses such as new tents and other camping gear. - (For troops) Have each patrol earn the National Honor Patrol award at least once. **Step 3—Have the top youth leader share the draft plan with other unit leaders, who then place it with other members to get their input and ideas.** Besides reviewing the draft plan, each member could evaluate the year's program using the feedback method, which is based on three simple questions: - What should we start doing that we are not currently doing? - What should we stop doing that is not working? - What should we continue doing that is working well and should be enhanced? It's useful for members to have copies of the current year's calendar available for review, as well as this year's plan. They shouldn't limit themselves to those materials, however. They might, for example, need a fresh look at opportunities that could be built more opportunities into the calendar for patrol activities that have not yet been considered. **Step 4—Invite the following people to attend the conference to maximize the efficiency of planning:** - The unit's youth leaders - Unit committee members and other adult leaders - The chartered organization representative - The unit commissioner (optional) - Anyone else who might be helpful, such as parents Keep in mind that these people will play very different roles at the conference. **Active roles—elected youth leaders (and all members if Venturing)** **Supportive roles—unit leader, assistant unit leaders, and any other adults** To keep the planning conference as purposeful and efficient as possible, invite only those adults who actively and regularly engage in unit activities and decision making. ## Holding the Annual Planning Conference The annual planning conference can be held at any regular meeting place in three or four hours on a Saturday morning or Sunday afternoon. However, if you have access to a lodge or retreat center, the experience will be more enjoyable. Try to set aside a full day to allow sufficient time for fellowship and team building. If the conference is more than a day, build in sessions and allow the youth leaders to focus on the task at hand. Most adults may not stay long enough to complete the entire planning conference. Instead, they should give their input ahead of time to the unit adults attending the conference and allow the youth to participate, set time for the planning conference. Besides the materials described above, you might prepare a worksheet on which that will be planned, including wall planners with erasable surfaces. Another useful tool is the program planning chart, a worksheet for recording by month the program features, activities, courts of honor, service projects, and more. This worksheet can help ensure that each type of activity is scheduled every month and that courts of honor and service projects are evenly spaced throughout the year. The outcome of the conference should be an annual calendar and a list of monthly program features (e.g., hiking, camping, canoeing, and service). The plan will list only the main events. The amount of detail in the plan will vary. For some units, the group will decide both where it wants to go and which ski slope it might want to hit. Other units may choose either the destination or the outing's focus. While discussing ideas at the conference, use these group rules: - It is important to respect one another's views. Listen when others interrupt. - Keep focused on the task to plan the unit's annual program. Don't get sidetracked. Some patrols may want to plan unfamiliar activities and destinations. A new traditional outing each can be fun; more than a few can make the program feel repetitive. Even traditional outings can benefit from occasional tweaks, such as doing a favorite activity in a favorite new location. --- # Annual Planning Conference, Step by Step **Step 1 —** The unit leader leads a discussion on the unit's goals for the coming year. Write the goals on a flip chart or eraser board, and agree that list of goals. **Step 2 —** Share with the rest of the meeting attendees the printed draft of the calendar that shows the dates researched (or review if dates listed on poster-size calendars around the room) and events planned. Ask if there are any conflicts with the dates selected. **Step 3 —** Take a few minutes to discuss these dates and events. Once you feel comfortable with this stage of the calendar, you might take a vote to approve the dates and activities listed so far. **Step 4 —** The two youth leaders share updates from members about what they want to do. This exercise can be the most challenging in the program planning conference, so take as much time as needed. The conference is a time to listen and learn about the youth's desired programs or themes. You might take it one month at a time. Don't forget to include Venturers' input if your troop has Venturers. If one of the group's desired programs is up to you and could be driven by your goals. As an example, if one of your goals is for the unit to take a high-adventure trip, then during the program planning conference, focus on traveling to Alaska, wilderness travel, trips, and water sports. Write this down, the first aid, and van safety. Again, as the group agrees on a monthly feature or program theme, write it on a flip chart or board and take a vote. Designate someone to record all this on a master calendar, and take good notes! **Step 5 —** Add other important dates such as: - Unit meetings and leadership meetings - Boards of review - Courts of honor - Elections - Open house - Service projects - Webelos Scout joint outings and transition ceremonies - Any other activities that can be scheduled this far in advance (for example, fundraising events) **Step 6 —** Hold a final discussion on the plan, calendar, and goals, and then take a vote for approval. Once youth members approve the annual plan, it will go to the unit committee for final approval. Work with the chartered organization representative to make any adjustments needed based on organizational calendars and facility availability for the dates and locations selected. **Step 7 —** To make this plan a truly valuable tool, it must be shared with each unit family, the chartered organization, and all other interested parties. The unit must follow its plan, share it with everyone, and review it regularly to see if modifications are needed. # After the Planning Conference Prepare the calendar in an easy-to-use format for distribution. Here are some features to consider: - Include as much detail as possible, including meeting start and end times. - Indicate when regular meetings won't be held or will be held at different times. - Color-code events for easy reference. For example, green for regular meetings and outings, blue for summer camp and high-adventure trips, red for meetings that don't affect all members (such as patrol leaders' council and committees, boards of review), red for special notices (such as meeting cancellations), and black for external events (such as district or council events, blue sky courses). - Add a revision date at the bottom of every page. If the calendar must be updated later, change the revision date and highlight the changes. Distribute copies of the final plan to youth members and their families, adult leaders, members of the unit committee, the chartered organization's leader, secretary, and facilities manager. Also be sure to post the calendar on the unit's website and social media. # Monthly Program Planning No detailed calendar is needed for monthly planning. Unlike the annual calendar, it's simply a skeleton on which to build your program. Short-term planning puts flesh on the bones. Each month, the patrol leaders' council or crew officers plan in detail the coming month's outings and meetings. - Briefly review the month that's just ending using the "start, stop, continue" model. - Plan in detail everything the unit will do in the month that is just beginning (or will soon begin). # How to Use the Program Features Each program feature in this book provides a month's worth of program ideas for Scout troops and Venturing crews. You will find five categories: - General information about the topic - Related advancement requirements and belt awards - Games related to the topic for that are sure to be fun - Ideas for using the EDGE method (Explain, Demonstrate, Guide, Enable) to teach topics - Four weeks of outings or monthly plans - Outlines of three main events (outings) that would be worth and intensify - Print and online resources related to the topic, including a list of related program features You will find these icons in each program feature: | Icon | Meaning | |------|---------| | 🟢 | Essential: Skills-development information and beginner level activities | | 🔵 | Challenging: Skills-development information and activities for those who have moved beyond the essential | | ◆ | Advanced: Learning topics and activities for those who are ready for the ultimate challenge | ## A NOTE TO VENTURERS For the purpose of earning the Discovery Trail awards, examples from each program feature can be adapted as follows: **Essential (Tier 1):** Beginner level activities requiring little preparation or planning; events that can be put together in a relatively short time; activities are not far beyond the comfort zone **Challenging (Tier II):** Midlevel activities requiring some detail and preparation; events that typically last less than four days; some prior planning; advancement may be the site leader or guide **Advanced (Tier III):** Advanced activities requiring extensive preparation; events last least four days; activities are mentally challenging and physically demanding; considerable planning and development prior to participation; such adventures as highlights of the program year and may take place once or twice annually --- Volume 3 | Introduction | Introduction–3 | Introduction–4 | Introduction | Volume 3 Location 8 of 257 • 2% --- # SCOUTS BSA TROOP PROGRAM PLANNING CHART [Complex planning chart with months and various program activities listed] --- # CAMPING ## Meeting Plan: Campsites **Week 1 Date_______________** | ACTIVITY | DESCRIPTION | RUN BY | TIME* | |----------|-------------|--------|-------| | **1** Preopening | Prepare several decks of index cards listing various items of camping gear, one item per card. Camp overnight and hone your camping skills. ## Equipment List - Camping gear (individual and group) - Backpacks, canoes, or bicycles to get the group to and from the campsite (optional) - Food - Water - Scout Basic Essentials (Review the list and take what you need.) ## Activity - Choose your campsite and event location. - Plan camping duty roster and meal plan. - Develop a schedule that allows plenty of time for setting up camp the right way, cooking meals, and enjoying activities. Plan for a comfortable local, and cooking areas. - Practice camping skills Scouts need to work on (fire building, cooking, knots, etc.). - Participate in other activities as deemed fitting (hiking, boating, mountain biking, etc.). - On departure, be sure you are leaving no trace of your presence. ## Safety - Normal camping safety guidelines apply. Use a buddy system; cellphones are a good idea as appropriate. Have a first aid kit handy. - Two-deep leadership is required at all times. ## Notes For an extra challenge, plan for all Scouts to reach camp under their own power—by hiking, cycling, or canoeing. If possible, have them carry their own gear. All could travel by the same means, or different groups could choose different options. --- # Parts of a Main Event Plan 1. A quick description of the main event 2. Basic information such as times and transportation plans 3. A quick reference to the event's ability level (essential, challenging, advanced) and more in-depth description of the main event 4. Key equipment you will need to take, including specialized gear for the type of event 5. A thumbnail planning worksheet 6. Safety concerns you should keep in mind 7. Special notes about the activity, including variations you could consider --- # CAVING ## An Adventure Underground Beneath the earth's surface lies a magnificent realm darker than a moonless night. No rain falls. No storms rage. The seasons never change. Other than the ripple of hidden streams and the occasional splash of dripping water, this underground world is silent. Yet it is not without life. Bats fly with sure reckoning through mazes of tunnels, and otherworldly fish swim through unseen rivers. This is the world of the cave. Few places on earth remain unexplored and unchanged by humans. Caves, however, are remote enough to have escaped the temptation by summits that rise far above familiar ground. Caves are drawn into a subterranean wilderness rarely left as exciting and remarkable as any place warmed by the rays of the sun. This month's meetings and activities will take you into this hidden world. Whether you tour a show cave or explore a wild cave, you will come to appreciate a part of the earth that most people will never experience. ## Objectives This month's activities should: - Teach Scouts the safety rules for caving. - Help Scouts understand white nose fungus and how to avoid spreading it. - Demonstrate proper clothing for caving. - Help Scouts understand the importance of a caving expert. - Teach Scouts proper caving techniques. - Enable Scouts to practice good caving ethics. ## RELATED ADVANCEMENT AND AWARDS - Exploration and Geology merit badges - Ranger: Cave Exploration elective --- ## Leadership Planning As a leadership team, you may want to discuss the following items during your planning meetings when choosing caving as your program feature. 1. How do we locate a caving expert? 2. What is our group's previous experience and skill level? 3. Which nearby caves are appropriate destinations based on our skill level? 4. How are we going to practice outdoor ethics? 5. How will we acquire needed equipment? 6. What advancement opportunities are available with this activity? 7. Will there be a cost for cave access? 8. Can we do a service project in conjunction with the activity? 9. To meet our needs, what should we change in our meeting night plans? ## PARENTS CAN HELP WITH THE CAVING PROGRAM FEATURE BY: 1. Helping to locate a caving expert 2. Assisting with transportation 3. Providing funding for the main event 4. Assisting with gear procurement and preparation 5. Chaperoning the main event 6. Maintaining current Youth Protection certification, which is required of all adults providing transportation for or participating in outings --- ## WHAT IS A CAVE? A cave is a naturally formed void located beneath the surface of the earth. By definition, it must have passages or rooms large enough to admit a human. By popular definition, it must be long enough for a caver to get out of the twilight and enter total darkness. Most states have some "show caves"—caves with paid tours and knowledgeable guides, complete with lights and easy paths and stairways. If Scouts have no idea what a cave environment is like, the show cave is a good place to start. Some of them offer special "wild" tours for groups that want more of a muddy, climbing adventure. Commercial caves may have a different set of commercial caves that they've developed, and probably will charge modest fees. ## SELECTING A CAVE GUIDE Selection of a qualified cave guide is critical to a team's success and safety in cave exploration. The cave guide should have all of the following qualifications: - Three years of continuous membership in the National Speleological Society (NSS) - Three years of continuous membership in a grotto (the proper name for a cave club) sanctioned by the NSS - Three years of experience in cave exploration, verified by trip log or grotto attestation - Current certification in first aid and CPR - Access to suitable caves - Instruction in or exposure to cave rescue techniques If you have trouble finding a cave guide, visit the NSS website or contact local grottoes. You can also send a self-addressed, stamped envelope to the NSS at 6001 Pulaski Pike, Huntsville, AL 35810-1122, to request assistance in locating qualified cavers in your area. --- Volume 3 Caving 33–1 33–2 Caving Volume 3 --- # Caving Techniques The goals of every caving expedition are to get in and out of the cave safely, to enjoy yourself while you are underground, and to leave no trace of your visit. Meeting these objectives requires planning. Familiarize yourself with any rules of caving guide-lines by contacting the agency responsible for managing the cave you plan to visit. There may be limitations on the size of groups allowed to go into a cave, and permits could be required. In addition, agency officials might be able to provide you with maps and suggestions for ways to enhance your experience. ## Risks and Safety Caves are the last place in the world to get hurt. There are often very difficult obstacles to get around and immobilized person through. Cavers often say the strictest rule in caving is DON'T GET HURT. There are two types of rescues if someone does get hurt: 1) Self-rescue and 2) Rescue by rescuers. Type 1 is by far preferred. If you are hurt, getting yourself out of the cave is the best thing to do. Before going into a cave, discuss what dangers there may be, how to avoid them, and what will be done if someone is injured. Order a copy of *American Caving Accidents* from the NSS and take turns reading some of the stories at a Scout meeting. ### Caving safety concerns: - Loose rocks can be knocked off on a person below. Care should always be taken not to do this. If a rock has been set in place by cavers that the most logical thing to do is to leave it where it is because of the possibility that he or she may knock a rock on another caver. - Don't jump in a cave. Climb down slowly. Shadows can throw off your depth perception. - Be certain of where you are in a cave and how to get back out. Sometimes it's necessary to leave a piece of flagging tape or some other indicator at a confusing junction for the trip out. Be sure to mark it with you when you leave. - Watch the weather. Some caves can flood to the ceiling. Err on the side of caution. - Tell someone where you will be, what time to expect to hear from you, and how long they should wait if you are late calling before they should get concerned and call someone to check on you. (This is good practice for any backcountry trip.) - Usually the only place a poisonous snake will be in a cave is at the entrance. Be extra cautious there. The first person in should poke a stick around in leaves and rocks and look for a snake. # Ethics Cavers take very seriously their creed to take nothing but pictures, leave nothing but footprints, and kill nothing but time. Leave caves cleaner than you found them. Pick up other cavers' litter. Do no harm. Marvel at living organisms in caves, and take photos of them—but don't touch them. Don't ever leave crumbs or food scraps in a cave. Go to the bathroom before you go underground and do your best not to even urinate in the cave. Some caves have open pits or passages that no one has entered since ancient people did. On some occasions ancient footprints are found. This attests to how timeless they are. The wind doesn't blow, the rain doesn't fall, and things stay the same for many, many years. Respect that and pride yourselves on treading gently in this marvelous world. ### Caving ethical concerns: - Never add to litter in a cave. Always remove some. - Be gentle. Don't disturb anything. Leave it like you found it or better. - Do your best to not leave human bodily waste in a cave. Go before you go. - Never write on the walls. It's illegal to do so in most states. - Leave cave wildlife alone. Take photos only. - Never touch or break a cave formation. Stalactite, stalagmite, drapery, bacon rind, helictite, and soda straw formations should be left for others to enjoy. Damaging cave formations is illegal in most states. --- # Safety Moments Each safety moment should focus on one or two components of the SAFE Checklist: - **S:** Supervision - **A:** Assessment - **F:** Fitness and Skill - **E:** Equipment and Environment ## MEETING 1: SAFE A and F ### Assessment and Fitness As we prepare for our caving trip we want to make sure everyone is prepared for the trip. We need to evaluate yourself and determine if this is the right type of trip for you. This might include evaluating your fitness level, your attitude, or lack of light. If you are frightened by tight spaces or by lack of light, please tell your troop leader or another person in our troop so we can help you. Explain the type of cave trip to the scouts if we are planning to explore and share any specific physical limitations that may keep someone from being able to experience the cave successfully. This might include some fitness programs that scouts need to participate in to be ready for the caving trip. ## MEETING 2: SAFE E ### Equipment Today we are going to learn about equipment used in caving. When we select equipment, it is important to ensure that our equipment lets us properly and functions correctly. Gear for caving and the clothing we use needs to protect us while still allowing freedom of movement and exploring. One of the most important pieces of equipment in caving is a source of light. When choosing and inspecting our gear it is important to be extra cautious for lights and even bring a secondary light source. ## MEETING 3: SAFE E ### Environment In the caves, we want to ensure that we practice the Leave No Trace Seven Principles. This includes not harming any of the features of the cave. In addition, we want to ensure that we do not take or leave anything in a cave that could harm the cave system or adversely affect the biodiversity that exists inside the cave. We need to check out all trash and be careful not to leave anything behind. Even the smallest particles can throw off the ecosystem below the earth. section above in this meeting plan and explain how our trip leader meets these guidelines. --- # CAVING GAMES ## Stalactite and Stalagmite Contest **Equipment:** For each group, a tray, two glass jars, a saucer, wooden thread, and baking soda **How to play:** Place both jars on the tray. Fill the jars with water and stir in as much baking soda as you can into each one. Place the jars in a warm place and put the saucer between them. Twist several strands of thread together before dipping the ends into the jars and letting the middle of the thread hang down between them. (The ends can be weighed down with marbles or washers to help them in the jars.) The two solutions should creep along the thread until they reach the middle and then drip down onto the saucer. Place the experiments in a place that can be undisturbed for the next week. **Scoring:** Wait until the next week's preparing; then judge each entry on shape, size, and how well they represent actual cave formations. **Notes:** Discuss how stalactites and stalagmites are formed in caves. How long would that take? ## Blindfold Maze **Equipment:** Blindfolds **How to play:** Divide into two or more groups, and have each group form a circle. Next, each player is blindfolded, and takes someone else's right hand. Then each group member does the same with their left hand, but they must take the left hand of a different person. On signal, two players unwind and attempt to straighten out the maze of hands into a straight line. Next, put on blindfolds and attempt to do the same thing. **Scoring:** The group that makes a straight line first wins. **Notes:** Discuss the difference between being blindfolded and not being blindfolded. Relate this to attempting tasks in a cave without light. ## Blindfold Battery Exchange **Equipment:** For each player, a flashlight with extra batteries and a blindfold **How to play:** The goal is to be able to replace batteries completely by touch in case of power failure in a cave. All batteries and flashlights are placed on a table in front of the blindfolded player. When directed to do so, each player races to be the first one to replace the batteries in his or her flashlight. **Scoring:** The first player to finish wins. **Variations:** This game can be played by groups or pairs. Players can also trade out lightbulbs instead of batteries. **Notes:** If a completely dark room is available, the activity can be done without blindfolds. ## Crowded Circle **Equipment:** A piece of chalk **How to play:** Draw a circle on the floor about 6 feet in diameter. Have the players walk freely around the room. Turn the lights off for 10 seconds. In the darkness, all players must get inside the circle. When the lights come back on, everybody must still be inside the circle. Then turn the lights off for another 10 seconds and try to move outside the circle. Any players who end up outside the circle are out of the game. The game resumes with shorter darkness periods, if necessary, until only one player remains in the circle. **Scoring:** The last player in the circle wins. **Variations:** 1) Instead of one circle, draw three circles on the floor and number them. When the lights go out, announce which circle should be used. 2) Make as many circles as there are players. To add an element of darkness and challenge to the activity, members must find their team's circle. **Notes:** If complete darkness is not available in the lights out, consider blindfolds. Follow up with a review about the experience of moving in the dark. Relate how that might apply in caves and why backup lights are important. --- # E.D.G.E. IDEAS *Explain how it is done—Tell them.* *Demonstrate the steps—Show them.* *Guide the activity—Watch them do it.* *Enable them to succeed on their own—Have them practice/teach it.* ## EXPLAIN - Define what caving is - Explain the difference between developed and wild caves - Introduce the importance of using a caving expert - Discuss the issue of white nose fungus - Explain the dangers of vertical caving ## DEMONSTRATE - Show videos of caving expeditions - Do a show-and-tell of proper equipment - Demonstrate how to use caving equipment - Show how to properly clean equipment - Demonstrate the process of mapping a cave ## GUIDE - An ability to show how stalactites and stalagmites are formed - Play a game that involves changing flashlight batteries in the dark - Guide Scouts as they map their meeting place to simulate mapping a cave - Lead Scouts on a show cave tour to prepare them for a real cave excursion ## ENABLE - Have experienced Scouts lead small groups during caving trips - On actual caving trips, have Scouts discuss how they are practicing outdoor ethics/techniques - Gradually build up to more challenging caving excursions - Prepare Scouts for a lifetime of safely enjoying caves --- # MAIN EVENT SUMMARIES | ESSENTIAL | CHALLENGING | ADVANCED | |-----------|-------------|----------| | Commercial show cave visit—Visit a commercially operated show cave.
